Every President and every person acting as President or discharging the functions of the President shall, before entering upon his office, make and subscribe in the presence of the Chief Justice of India or, in his absence, the senior-most Judge of the Supreme Court available, an oath or affirmation in the following form, that is to say —

 

"I, A.B., do swear in the name of God / solemnly affirm that I will faithfully execute the office of President (or discharge the functions of the President) of India and will to the best of my ability preserve, protect and defend theConstitution and the law and that I will devote myself to the service and well-being of the people of India”.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that before entering office, the President must take an oath or affirmation.

Also, this oath is taken in the presence of the Chief Justice of India or the senior-most available judge of the Supreme Court.

In addition, through the oath, the President promises to faithfully perform duties, preserve and protect the Constitution and the law, and serve the people of India.

This means that the President formally promises to defend the Constitution and work for the country.

Ex : Before starting the term, the President takes an oath to protect the Constitution.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Prescribes the oath of office for the President.
The oath is to be administered by the Chief Justice of India (or the senior-most Supreme Court judge in their absence).
The President's oath is unique as it includes the pledge to 'preserve, protect and defend the Constitution and the law'.
Historical_Context: The specific wording of the President's oath underscores their role as the constitutional head and the ultimate guardian of the Constitution. Unlike the ministers' oath of 'allegiance' to the Constitution, the President's oath is to 'preserve, protect and defend' it, highlighting a higher level of responsibility.
Landmark_Cases: The oath is a constitutional requirement and its text can be referred to by courts to understand the nature and duties of the presidential office.
Related_Articles: Third Schedule (Forms of Oaths or Affirmations for other constitutional posts)

Title: Procedure for impeachment of the President
Original_Description:
(1) When a President is to be impeached for violation of the Constitution, the charge shall be preferred by either House of Parliament.

 

(2) No such charge shall be preferred unless—

 

(a) the proposal to prefer such charge is contained in a resolution which has been moved after at least fourteen days' notice in writing signed by not less than one-fourth of the total number of members of the House has been given of their intention to move the resolution, and

 

(b) such resolution has been passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the House.

 

(3) When a charge has been so preferred by either House of Parliament, the other House shall investigate the charge or cause the charge to be investigated and the President shall have the right to appear and to be represented at such investigation.

 

(4) If as a result of the investigation a resolution is passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the House by which the charge was investigated or caused to be investigated, declaring that the charge preferred against the President has been sustained, such resolution shall have the effect of removing the President from his office as from the date on which the resolution is so passed.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the procedure for removing the President from office for violation of the Constitution.

Also, the charge can be started by either House of Parliament. However, it cannot be started without proper notice. At least fourteen days before moving the resolution, a written notice must be given. This notice must be signed by at least one-fourth of the total members of that House.

In addition, the resolution to bring the charge must be passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of that House.

When one House passes the charge, the other House must investigate the matter or arrange for it to be investigated. The President has the right to appear and defend himself during this investigation.

If the second House also passes a resolution by not less than two-thirds of its total membership declaring that the charge is proved, the President is removed from office from the date the resolution is passed.

Ex : If both Houses of Parliament pass the required majority resolutions after investigation, the President can be removed from office.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Lays down the procedure for the impeachment of the President for 'violation of the Constitution'.
The process can be initiated by either House of Parliament.
Charges must be signed by at least one-fourth of the members of the House and a 14-day notice must be given.
The resolution must be passed by a special majority of two-thirds of the total membership of that House.
The other House then investigates the charges. If it also passes the resolution by a two-thirds majority of its total membership, the President stands impeached.
Historical_Context: This article provides a quasi-judicial and difficult procedure for removing the President, ensuring that the head of state cannot be removed on frivolous political grounds. The high threshold of a two-thirds majority of the total membership of both Houses makes the process extremely challenging. No President of India has been impeached to date.
Landmark_Cases: This procedure has never been used, so there is no case law directly interpreting it.
Related_Articles: Article 56 (Term of office of President, which mentions impeachment)
Article 124(4) (Similar procedure for removal of a Supreme Court Judge)

Title: Time of holding election to fill vacancy in the office of President and the term of office of person elected to fill casual vacancy
Original_Description:
(1) An election to fill a vacancy caused by the expiration of the term of office of President shall be completed before the expiration of the term.

 

(2) An election to fill a vacancy in the office of President occurring by reason of his death, resignation or removal, or otherwise shall be held as soon as possible after, and in no case later than six months from, the date of occurrence of the vacancy; and the person elected to fill the vacancy shall, subject to the provisions of article 56, be entitled to hold office for the full term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains when an election must be held to fill a vacancy in the office of the President.

Also, if the President’s term is about to end normally, the election must be completed before the term expires.

However, if the vacancy happens because of death, resignation, removal, or any other reason, the election must be held as soon as possible and not later than six months from the date the vacancy occurs.

In addition, the person elected to fill such a vacancy will serve a full term of five years from the date they enter office.

Ex : If a President resigns, a new election must be held within six months.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Mandates that an election to fill a vacancy caused by the expiry of the President's term must be completed before the term ends.
In case of a vacancy due to death, resignation, or impeachment, the election must be held within six months.
A person elected to fill such a mid-term vacancy is entitled to a full five-year term.
Historical_Context: This article ensures that the highest office of the land does not remain vacant for long, providing for constitutional continuity. The provision for a full five-year term for a president elected mid-term contrasts with the US system, where the Vice President serves out the remainder of the term.
Landmark_Cases: In re Presidential Election (1974): The Supreme Court clarified that the election for the President must be held before the expiration of the term and cannot be delayed on the grounds that a state assembly is dissolved.
Related_Articles: Article 65 (The Vice-President to act as President)

Title: The Vice-President of India
Original_Description: There shall be a Vice-President of India.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that there shall be a Vice-President of India.

This means that the Constitution creates the office of the Vice-President as an important constitutional position in the country.

Ex : India must always have a Vice-President as provided by the Constitution.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: This article simply and formally establishes the office of the Vice-President of India.
The Vice-President is the second-highest constitutional office in the country.
Historical_Context: The office of the Vice-President of India is modeled on that of the Vice President of the United States. However, the Indian Vice-President's primary role is to preside over the Rajya Sabha, and they step in for the President only in specific circumstances, unlike the more defined executive role of the US Vice President.
Landmark_Cases: This is a declaratory article and not subject to litigation.
Related_Articles: Article 64 (Vice-President to be ex-officio Chairman of the Council of States)

Title: The Vice-President to be ex officio Chairman of the Council of States
Original_Description:
The Vice-President shall be ex officio Chairman of the Council of States and shall not hold any other office of profit:

 

Provided that during any period when the VicePresident acts as President or discharges the functions of the President under article 65, he shall not perform the duties of the office of Chairman of the Council of States and shall not be entitled to any salary or allowance payable to the Chairman of the Council of States under article 97.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Vice-President shall be the Chairman of the Council of States.

Also, the Vice-President cannot hold any other office of profit.

However, when the Vice-President acts as President or performs the duties of the President, he will not perform the duties of Chairman of the Council of States during that time. He will also not receive the salary meant for the Chairman during that period.

This means that the Vice-President mainly presides over the Council of States but must stop doing so when acting as President.

Ex : If the Vice-President is acting as President, he will not preside over the Rajya Sabha during that time.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies the primary function of the Vice-President: to serve as the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha (Council of States).
Prohibits the Vice-President from holding any other office of profit.
The Vice-President draws their salary as the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, not as the Vice-President.
Historical_Context: This provision gives the Vice-President a defined legislative role, similar to the US Vice President presiding over the Senate. It ensures that the upper house of Parliament has a presiding officer who is not a member of the house, thus expected to be impartial.
Landmark_Cases: This article defines a clear function and is not typically a subject of legal dispute.
Related_Articles: Article 89 (The Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Council of States)

Title: The Vice-President to act as President or to discharge his functions during casual vacancies in the office, or during the absence, of President
Original_Description:
(1) In the event of the occurrence of any vacancy in the office of the President by reason of his death, resignation or removal, or otherwise, the Vice-President shall act as President until the date on which a new President elected in accordance with the provisions of this Chapter to fill such vacancy enters upon his office.

 

(2) When the President is unable to discharge his functions owing to absence, illness or any other cause, the Vice-President shall discharge his functions until the date on which the President resumes his duties.

 

(3) The Vice-President shall, during, and in respect of, the period while he is so acting as, or discharging the functions of, President, have all the powers and immunities of the President and be entitled to such emoluments, allowances and privileges as may be determined by Parliament by law and, until provision in that behalf is so made, such emoluments, allowances and privileges as are specified in the Second Schedule.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Vice-President will act as President if the office of the President becomes vacant.

Also, if the vacancy happens because of death, resignation, removal, or any other reason, the Vice-President will act as President until a new President is elected and takes office.

In addition, if the President is unable to perform duties because of illness, absence, or any other reason, the Vice-President will perform those duties until the President resumes office.

While acting as President, the Vice-President will have all the powers and protections of the President and will receive the related benefits.

Ex : If the President is ill and unable to work, the Vice-President will perform the President’s duties during that time.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Outlines the circumstances under which the Vice-President assumes the President's role.
The Vice-President 'acts as President' when the office is vacant (due to death, resignation, etc.) until a new President is elected.
The Vice-President 'discharges the functions' of the President when the President is temporarily unable to do so (due to absence, illness, etc.).
When acting as President, the Vice-President has all the powers and immunities of the President.
Historical_Context: This article provides for a smooth succession and continuity of the office of the Head of State. It ensures that there is no vacuum in the highest constitutional office. This provision has been invoked on occasions of the death of a sitting President, such as Dr. Zakir Hussain and Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article ensuring continuity of office and has not been a subject of major litigation.
Related_Articles: Article 62 (Time of holding election to fill vacancy)
Article 70 (Discharge of President's functions in other contingencies)

Title: Election of Vice - President
Original_Description:
(1) The Vice-President shall be elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of the members of both Houses of Parliament in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote and the voting at such election shall be by secret ballot.

 

(2) The Vice-President shall not be a member of either House of Parliament or of a House of the Legislature of any State, and if a member of either House of Parliament or of a House of the Legislature of any State be elected Vice-President, he shall be deemed to have vacated his seat in that House on the date on which he enters upon his office as Vice-President.

 

(3) No person shall be eligible for election as VicePresident unless he —

 

(a) is a citizen of India;

 

(b) has completed the age of thirty-five years; and

 

(c) is qualified for election as a member of the Council of States

 

(4) A person shall not be eligible for election as Vice-President if he holds any office of profit under the Government of India or the Government of any State or under any local or other authority subject to the control of any of the said Governments.

 

Explanation.— For the purposes of this article, a person shall not be deemed to hold any office of profit by reason only that he is the President or Vice-President of the Union or the Governor of any State or is a Minister either for the Union or for any State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how the Vice-President is elected and who can become Vice-President.

Also, the Vice-President is elected by the members of both Houses of Parliament using proportional representation and secret ballot.

In addition, if a member of Parliament or a State Legislature becomes Vice-President, they must give up their seat.

A person can become Vice-President only if they are a citizen of India, are at least thirty-five years old, and are qualified to be a member of the Council of States.

However, a person cannot be elected if they hold an office of profit under the government, except certain specified offices.

Ex : Members of Parliament vote by secret ballot to elect the Vice-President.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Prescribes the method and qualifications for the election of the Vice-President.
The electoral college consists of all members (elected and nominated) of both Houses of Parliament.
The election is by proportional representation using the single transferable vote and secret ballot.
Qualifications include being a citizen of India, being at least 35 years old, and being qualified for election as a member of the Rajya Sabha.
Historical_Context: The electoral college for the Vice-President is different from that of the President, as it only includes members of Parliament. This is because the Vice-President's primary role is to be the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, a parliamentary function. State legislators do not participate in this election.
Landmark_Cases: Disputes regarding the election of the Vice-President are adjudicated by the Supreme Court under Article 71.
Related_Articles: Article 71 (Matters relating to the election of a ... Vice-President)

Title: Term of office of Vice-President
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the term of office of the Vice-President.

Also, the Vice-President holds office for five years from the date of entering office.

The Vice-President may resign by writing to the President.

In addition, the Vice-President can be removed by a resolution passed by the Council of States by a majority of all its members and agreed to by the House of the People. At least fourteen days notice must be given before moving such a resolution.

However, even after the term ends, the Vice-President continues in office until a successor takes charge.

Ex : The Rajya Sabha can pass a resolution to remove the Vice-President after giving proper notice.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Fixes the term of office for the Vice-President at five years.
The Vice-President can resign by writing to the President.
Lays down the procedure for removal: A resolution must be passed by the Rajya Sabha with an 'effective majority' (majority of all then members) and agreed to by the Lok Sabha with a simple majority.
A 14-day notice is required before moving such a resolution.
Historical_Context: The removal process for the Vice-President is simpler than the impeachment of the President. The initiative for removal must come from the Rajya Sabha, as the Vice-President is its Chairman. This reinforces the Vice-President's accountability to the house he presides over.
Landmark_Cases: No Vice-President has ever faced removal proceedings.
Related_Articles: Article 61 (Procedure for impeachment of the President)

Title: Time of holding election to fill vacancy in the office of Vice-President and the term of office of person elected to fill casual vacancy
Original_Description:
(1) An election to fill a vacancy caused by the expiration of the term of office of Vice-President shall be completed before the expiration of the term.

 

(2) An election to fill a vacancy in the office of VicePresident occurring by reason of his death, resignation or removal, or otherwise shall be held as soon as possible after the occurrence of the vacancy, and the person elected to fill the vacancy shall, subject to the provisions of article 67, be entitled to hold office for the full term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains when an election must be held to fill a vacancy in the office of the Vice-President.

Also, if the term is ending normally, the election must be completed before the term expires.

However, if the vacancy occurs because of death, resignation, removal, or any other reason, the election must be held as soon as possible.

In addition, the person elected to fill such a vacancy will serve a full term of five years from the date they enter office.

Ex : If the Vice-President resigns, a new election must be held as soon as possible.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: An election for a normal vacancy (end of term) must be completed before the term expires.
An election for a casual vacancy (death, resignation, removal) must be held 'as soon as possible'.
Unlike the 6-month deadline for the President's election, no specific time limit is given for the Vice-President's casual vacancy.
A person elected to fill a casual vacancy serves a full five-year term.
Historical_Context: This article ensures continuity in the office of the Vice-President. The lack of a strict deadline for filling a casual vacancy is because there is no provision for the Vice-President's role to be temporarily filled by another functionary (unlike the President's role, which the VP can assume). The Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha can preside over the house in the interim.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article and has not been a subject of litigation.
Related_Articles: Article 62 (Time of holding election to fill vacancy in the office of President)

Title: Oath or affirmation by the Vice-President
Original_Description:
Every Vice-President shall, before entering upon his office, make and subscribe before the President, or some person appointed in that behalf by him, an oath or affirmation in the following form, that is to say —

 

“I, A.B., do swear in the name of God / solemnly affirm that I will bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India as by law established and that I will faithfully discharge the duty upon which I am about to enter.”
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that before entering office, the Vice-President must take an oath or affirmation.

Also, the oath is taken before the President or a person appointed by the President.

In addition, through this oath, the Vice-President promises to bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India and to faithfully perform the duties of the office.

This means that the Vice-President formally promises loyalty to the Constitution before starting work.

Ex : Before beginning duties, the Vice-President takes an oath to follow the Constitution.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Prescribes the oath of office for the Vice-President.
The oath is administered by the President or a person appointed by the President.
The Vice-President swears to bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution and to faithfully discharge their duties.
Historical_Context: The oath binds the Vice-President to the Constitution, similar to other high constitutional functionaries. The administration of the oath by the President signifies the hierarchical relationship between the two highest offices.
Landmark_Cases: The oath is a constitutional formality and not a subject of legal disputes.
Related_Articles: Article 60 (Oath or affirmation by the President)
Third Schedule (Forms of Oaths or Affirmations)

Title: Discharge of President's functions in other contingencies
Original_Description: Parliament may make such provisions as it thinks fit for the discharge of the functions of the President in any contingency not provided for in this Chapter.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament may make laws for situations not already covered in the Constitution regarding the discharge of the President’s functions.

Also, this applies to unexpected situations where the Constitution does not clearly say who should perform the President’s duties.

This means that Parliament can create rules to handle special or unforeseen circumstances related to the President’s office.

Ex : If both the President and Vice-President offices are vacant, Parliament can make a law to decide who will perform the President’s duties.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to make laws for situations not covered by the Constitution where the President's functions need to be discharged.
This is a residual power to handle unforeseen contingencies related to the office of the President.
Historical_Context: This article provides a constitutional safety net. The framers foresaw that situations not explicitly mentioned in Articles 62 or 65 (like the simultaneous vacancy in the offices of both President and Vice-President) might arise. Parliament enacted the President (Discharge of Functions) Act, 1969, which provides that in such a case, the Chief Justice of India (or the senior-most Supreme Court judge) would act as President.
Landmark_Cases: This article provides the legal basis for the President (Discharge of Functions) Act, 1969.
Related_Articles: Article 62 (Time of holding election to fill vacancy)
Article 65 (The Vice-President to act as President)

Title: Matters relating to, or connected with, the election of a President or Vice-President
Original_Description:
(1) All doubts and disputes arising out of or in connection with the election of a President or Vice - President shall be inquired into and decided by the Supreme Court whose decision shall be final.

 

(2) If the election of a person as President or Vice - President is declared void by the Supreme Court, acts done by him in the exercise and performance of the powers and duties of the office of President or Vice - President, as the case may be, on or before the date of the decision of the Supreme Court shall not be invalidated by reason of that declaration.

 

(3) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, Parliament may by law regulate any matter relating to or connected with the election of a President or Vice - President.

 

(4) The election of a person as President or Vice - President shall not be called in question on the ground of the existence of any vacancy for whatever reason among the members of the electoral college electing him
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that all doubts and disputes related to the election of the President or Vice-President shall be decided by the Supreme Court.

Also, the decision of the Supreme Court in such matters is final.

In addition, if the Supreme Court declares the election void, the acts done by that person before the decision will remain valid.

However, the election cannot be challenged only because there was a vacancy in the electoral college.

This means that only the Supreme Court can decide election disputes of these high offices.

Ex : If someone challenges the President’s election, the Supreme Court will decide the matter.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Gives the Supreme Court exclusive and final jurisdiction over all doubts and disputes related to the election of the President or Vice-President.
If the election is declared void by the Supreme Court, acts done by the person as President/Vice-President before the court's decision remain valid.
The election cannot be challenged on the ground that the electoral college was incomplete (e.g., if a state assembly was dissolved).
Historical_Context: Placing the authority to decide presidential election disputes with the Supreme Court ensures that such a high-stakes issue is adjudicated by the highest judicial body in an impartial manner, free from political influence. This reinforces the sanctity and legitimacy of these high offices.
Landmark_Cases: Dr. N.B. Khare vs The Election Commission of India (1957): The court clarified that doubts and disputes can only be brought before the Supreme Court after the election is concluded, not during the process.

Title: Power of President to grant pardons, etc, and to suspend, remit or commute sentences in certain cases
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President has the power to grant pardons and other forms of relief from punishment.

Also, the President can grant pardon, reduce a sentence, suspend a sentence, or change the punishment in certain cases.

In addition, this power applies in cases of punishment by a Court Martial, offenses related to Union laws, and in all cases of death sentence.

However, this power does not remove similar powers given by law to officers of the Armed Forces or to the Governor of a State in certain cases.

This means that the President can give mercy or reduce punishment in special cases.

Ex : A person sentenced to death can request the President to reduce the punishment.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Grants the President the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites, remissions, and to suspend, remit, or commute sentences.
This pardoning power applies to offenses against Union laws, sentences by a Court Martial, and all cases of a death sentence.
This power is an executive power and is exercised on the advice of the Council of Ministers.
The exercise of this power is subject to judicial review on limited grounds.
Historical_Context: The pardoning power is a sovereign prerogative, intended to be a safeguard against judicial error or to be used in cases where clemency is justified. It acts as a final recourse for justice and mercy. The scope and reviewability of this power have been extensively debated and defined by the judiciary over time.
Landmark_Cases: Maru Ram vs Union of India (1980): The Supreme Court held that the President's pardoning power is not a private act but a public one, to be exercised on the advice of the Council of Ministers.

Epuru Sudhakar vs Government of Andhra Pradesh (2006): The Court ruled that the exercise of pardoning power is subject to judicial review on grounds of arbitrariness, mala fides, or if the decision was made without application of mind.
Related_Articles: Article 161 (Power of Governor to grant pardons...)

Title: Extent of executive power of the Union
Original_Description:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, the executive power of the Union shall extend —

 

(a) to the matters with respect to which Parliament has power to make laws; and

 

(b) to the exercise of such rights, authority and jurisdiction as are exercisable by the Government of India by virtue of any treaty or agreement:

 

Provided that the executive power referred to in subclause (a) shall not, save as expressly provided in this Constitution or in any law made by Parliament, extend in any State to matters with respect to which the Legislature of the State has also power to make laws.

 

(2) Until otherwise provided by Parliament, a State and any officer or authority of a State may, notwithstanding anything in this article, continue to exercise in matters with respect to which Parliament has power to make laws for that State such executive power or functions as the State or officer or authority thereof could exercise immediately before the commencement of this Constitution.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the extent of the executive power of the Union.

Also, the executive power of the Union extends to matters on which Parliament can make laws.

In addition, it also extends to rights and authority that come from treaties or agreements made by the Government of India.

However, this power does not extend to matters where the State Legislature also has power to make laws, unless the Constitution or a law made by Parliament clearly provides so.

This means that the Union government can take executive action on subjects where it has law-making power.

Ex : The Union government can take executive action on defense matters because Parliament can make laws on defense.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Defines the extent of the executive power of the Union government.
The Union's executive power is co-extensive with the legislative power of the Parliament. This means the Union can take executive action on any subject in the Union List.
It also extends to rights, authority, and jurisdiction derived from any treaty or agreement.
Historical_Context: This article delineates the administrative boundaries of the central government, linking its executive authority directly to its legislative competence as defined in the Seventh Schedule. It establishes the principle that for every legislative power, there is a corresponding executive power.
Landmark_Cases: Ram Jawaya Kapur vs State of Punjab (1955): The court observed that executive power is not just about executing laws but also includes all functions of government that are not legislative or judicial.
Related_Articles: Article 162 (Extent of executive power of State)
Article 246 (Subject-matter of laws made by Parliament and by the Legislatures of States)
Seventh Schedule (Union List, State List, Concurrent List)

Title: Council of Ministers to aid and advise President
Original_Description:
(1) There shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President who shall, in the exercise of his functions, act in accordance with such advice:

 

Provided that the President may require the Council of Ministers to reconsider such advice, either generally or otherwise, and the President shall act in accordance with the advice tendered after such reconsideration.

 

 (2) The question whether any, and if so what, advice was tendered by Ministers to the President shall not be inquired into in any court.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that there shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President.

Also, the President must act according to the advice given by the Council of Ministers.

However, the President may ask the Council to reconsider its advice once. After reconsideration, the President must act according to the advice given again.

In addition, the courts cannot question what advice was given by the Ministers to the President.

This means that the real executive decisions are made by the Council of Ministers, and the President acts based on their advice.

Ex : If the Council of Ministers advises the President to approve a decision, the President must act according to that advice.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Establishes a Council of Ministers (CoM) headed by the Prime Minister.
The primary role of the CoM is to aid and advise the President.
Crucially, it mandates that the President 'shall act in accordance with such advice'.
The President has one power of review: they can ask the CoM to reconsider its advice once, but must accept the reconsidered advice.
The advice tendered by the ministers cannot be inquired into by any court.
Historical_Context: This article is the lynchpin of the parliamentary system in India. Originally, the wording was less explicit, leading to some ambiguity. The 42nd Amendment (1976) made the advice binding on the President. The 44th Amendment (1978) added the proviso allowing the President to ask for reconsideration once, providing a limited check.
Landmark_Cases: Shamsher Singh vs State of Punjab (1974): The Supreme Court clarified that the President is a constitutional head and must act on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers.

Title: Other provisions as to Ministers
Original_Description:
(1) The Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President and the other Ministers shall be appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister.

 

(1A) The total number of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, in the Council of Ministers shall not exceed fifteen per cent. of the total number of members of the House of the People.

 

(1B) A member of either House of Parliament belonging to any political party who is disqualified for being a member of that House under paragraph 2 of the Tenth Schedule shall also be disqualified to be appointed as a Minister under clause (1) for duration of the period commencing from the date of his disqualification till the date on which the term of his office as such member would expire or where he contests any election to either House of Parliament before the expiry of such period, till the date on which he is declared elected, whichever is earlier.

 

(2) The Ministers shall hold office during the pleasure of the President.

 

(3) The Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the House of the People.

 

(4) Before a Minister enters upon his office, the President shall administer to him the oaths of office and of secrecy according to the forms set out for the purpose in the Third Schedule.

 

(5) A Minister who for any period of six consecutive months is not a member of either House of Parliament shall at the expiration of that period cease to be a Minister.

 

(6) The salaries and allowances of Ministers shall be such as Parliament may from time to time by law determine and, until Parliament so determines, shall be as specified in the Second Schedule.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the provisions related to Ministers.

Also, the Prime Minister is appointed by the President, and other Ministers are appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister.

In addition, the total number of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, must not exceed fifteen percent of the total members of the House of the People.

The Ministers hold office during the pleasure of the President and are collectively responsible to the House of the People.

A Minister must take oath before entering office. If a Minister is not a member of either House of Parliament for six consecutive months, they cease to be a Minister.

This means that Ministers must maintain the confidence of the House of the People and follow constitutional rules.

Ex : A person appointed as Minister must become a Member of Parliament within six months.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: The President appoints the Prime Minister and other ministers on the PM's advice.
Ministers hold office 'during the pleasure of the President', which in practice means they can be removed by the PM.
The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.
The total number of ministers cannot exceed 15% of the total strength of the Lok Sabha.
A minister who is not a member of Parliament for six consecutive months ceases to be a minister.
Historical_Context: This article lays down the core principles of cabinet government: prime ministerial authority, collective responsibility, and accountability to the lower house of Parliament. The 15% size limit was added by the 91st Amendment Act, 2003, to prevent bloated cabinets and the associated problem of political defections.
Landmark_Cases: S.R. Bommai vs Union of India (1994): While dealing with President's rule, the court discussed the principle of collective responsibility and the importance of a government enjoying the confidence of the Lok Sabha.
Related_Articles: Article 74 (Council of Ministers to aid and advise President)

Title: Attorney-General for India
Original_Description:
(1) The President shall appoint a person who is qualified to be appointed a Judge of the Supreme Court to be Attorney-General for India.

 

(2) It shall be the duty of the Attorney-General to give advice to the Government of India upon such legal matters, and to perform such other duties of a legal character, as may from time to time be referred or assigned to him by the President, and to discharge the functions conferred on him by or under this Constitution or any other law for the time being in force.

 

(3) In the performance of his duties the AttorneyGeneral shall have right of audience in all courts in the territory of India.

 

(4) The Attorney-General shall hold office during the pleasure of the President, and shall receive such remuneration as the President may determine.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that there shall be an Attorney-General for India appointed by the President.

Also, the person appointed must be qualified to be a Judge of the Supreme Court.

In addition, the Attorney-General gives legal advice to the Government of India and performs legal duties assigned by the President.

The Attorney-General has the right to speak in all courts in India and holds office during the pleasure of the President.

This means that the Attorney-General is the chief legal advisor of the Union government.

Ex : The Attorney-General represents the Government of India in important cases before the Supreme Court.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Establishes the office of the Attorney-General for India.
The Attorney-General is appointed by the President and must be qualified to be a Supreme Court judge.
They are the chief legal advisor to the Government of India.
They have the right of audience in all courts in India.
They can speak and take part in the proceedings of both Houses of Parliament but do not have the right to vote.
Historical_Context: The office of the Attorney-General is designed to provide the Union government with legal expertise for its functions. They represent the government in legal proceedings and provide advice on complex legal matters. They are not a full-time government servant and are allowed to have a private legal practice, provided it does not conflict with their duties.
Landmark_Cases: The role and functions of the Attorney-General are generally well-defined and not a common subject of litigation.
Related_Articles: Article 88 (Rights of Ministers and Attorney-General as respects Houses)

Title: Conduct of business of the Government of India
Original_Description:
(1) All executive action of the Government of India shall be expressed to be taken in the name of the President.

 

(2) Orders and other instruments made and executed in the name of the President shall be authenticated in such manner as may be specified in rules to be made by the President, and the validity of an order or instrument which is so authenticated shall not be called in question on the ground that it is not an order or instrument made or executed by the President.

 

(3) The President shall make rules for the more convenient transaction of the business of the Government of India, and for the allocation among Ministers of the said business.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that all executive actions of the Government of India shall be expressed in the name of the President.

Also, orders made in the name of the President must be authenticated according to rules made by the President.

In addition, the President makes rules for conducting the business of the Government and for allocating work among Ministers.

This means that government decisions are formally issued in the name of the President, even though they are made by the Council of Ministers.

Ex : A government order is issued in the name of the President.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: All executive actions of the Union Government are formally taken in the name of the President.
The President makes rules for the authentication of orders and instruments made in their name.
The President also makes rules for the convenient transaction of business of the Government of India and for the allocation of this business among ministers.
Historical_Context: This article formalizes the constitutional position of the President as the head of the executive. While the decisions are made by the Council of Ministers, the official expression of those decisions is in the name of the President. The 'Allocation of Business Rules' made under this article are crucial for the day-to-day functioning of government ministries.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article. Courts have held that while actions must be expressed in the President's name, a failure to do so does not automatically invalidate the action if it can be shown that the decision was properly made by the government.
Related_Articles: Article 74 (Council of Ministers to aid and advise President)

Title: Duties of Prime Minister as respects the furnishing of information to the President, etc
Original_Description:
It shall be the duty of the Prime Minister —

 

(a) to communicate to the President all decisions of the Council of Ministers relating to the administration of the affairs of the Union and proposals for legislation;

 

(b) to furnish such information relating to the administration of the affairs of the Union and proposals for legislation as the President may call for; and

 

(c) if the President so requires, to submit for the consideration of the Council of Ministers any matter on which a decision has been taken by a Minister but which has not been considered by the Council.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the duties of the Prime Minister in relation to the President.

Also, the Prime Minister must inform the President about all decisions of the Council of Ministers related to administration and proposed laws.

In addition, the Prime Minister must provide any information the President asks for.

If the President requires, the Prime Minister must place before the Council of Ministers any matter decided by an individual Minister but not yet considered by the whole Council.

This means that the President has the right to be informed about government decisions.

Ex : The Prime Minister must inform the President about a major policy decision taken by the government.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Lays down the duties of the Prime Minister in relation to the President.
The PM must communicate all decisions of the Council of Ministers to the President.
The PM must furnish any information that the President calls for.
The PM must, if the President requires, submit a matter for the consideration of the whole Council of Ministers if a decision was taken by an individual minister alone.
Historical_Context: This article establishes a crucial communication link between the real executive (PM and CoM) and the nominal executive (President). It empowers the President to be kept informed about the affairs of the government. This 'right to be informed' is a key aspect of the President's constitutional role.
Landmark_Cases: The relationship defined by this article is primarily between the PM and the President and is governed by constitutional convention rather than court cases.
Related_Articles: Article 74 (Council of Ministers to aid and advise President)

Title: Constitution of Parliament
Original_Description: There shall be a Parliament for the Union which shall consist of the President and two Houses to be known respectively as the Council of States and the House of the People.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that there shall be a Parliament for the Union.

Also, Parliament consists of the President and two Houses known as the Council of States and the House of the People.

This means that the President is a part of Parliament along with the two Houses.

Ex : A law is passed only after it is approved by both Houses and the President.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Establishes the Parliament of India.
States that Parliament consists of three parts: the President, the Council of States (Rajya Sabha), and the House of the People (Lok Sabha).
This highlights that the President is an integral part of the legislature, even though they do not sit in either house.
Historical_Context: The framers chose a bicameral legislature. The Lok Sabha represents the people directly, while the Rajya Sabha represents the states. The inclusion of the President as part of Parliament is based on the British model, where the Crown-in-Parliament is the sovereign legislative body. The President's role in assenting to bills (Article 111) is a key legislative function.
Landmark_Cases: This is a foundational, descriptive article and not a subject of litigation.
Related_Articles: Article 80 (Composition of the Council of States)
Article 81 (Composition of the House of the People)

Title: Composition of the Council of States
Original_Description:
The Council of States] shall consist of —

 

(a) twelve members to be nominated by the President in accordance with the provisions of clause (3); and

 

(b) not more than two hundred and thirty-eight representatives of the States and of the Union territories.

 

(2) The allocation of seats in the Council of States to be filled by representatives of the States and of the Union territories] shall be in accordance with the provisions in that behalf contained in the Fourth Schedule.

 

(3) The members to be nominated by the President under sub-clause (a) of clause (1) shall consist of persons having special knowledge or practical experience in respect of such matters as the following, namely:-

 

Literature, science, art and social service.

 

(4) The representatives of each State  in the Council of States shall be elected by the elected members of the Legislative Assembly of the State in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote.

 

(5) The representatives of the Union territories in the Council of States shall be chosen in such manner as Parliament may by law prescribe.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the composition of the Council of States.

Also, the Council of States consists of twelve members nominated by the President and not more than two hundred and thirty-eight representatives of the States and Union Territories.

In addition, the nominated members must have special knowledge or practical experience in literature, science, art, or social service.

The representatives of the States are elected by the elected members of the State Legislative Assemblies using proportional representation and single transferable vote. Representatives of Union Territories are chosen in a manner prescribed by Parliament.

This means that the Council of States includes both elected representatives and nominated experts.

Ex : A person with special contribution in art may be nominated by the President to the Council of States.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies the composition of the Rajya Sabha.
The maximum strength is 250 members.
12 members are nominated by the President from among persons with special knowledge or practical experience in literature, science, art, and social service.
Up to 238 members are representatives of the States and Union Territories, elected indirectly by the elected members of the State Legislative Assemblies.
Historical_Context: The Rajya Sabha was created to provide a forum for the states in the federal legislature and to act as a revising chamber that can hold the directly elected Lok Sabha to account. The nomination of 12 members was intended to bring distinguished experts and eminent personalities into Parliament who might not participate in the electoral process.
Landmark_Cases: The process of nomination and election to the Rajya Sabha is governed by this article and the Representation of the People Act, 1951.
Related_Articles: Article 81 (Composition of the House of the People)
Fourth Schedule (Allocation of seats in the Council of States)

Title: Composition of the House of the People
Original_Description:
(1) Subject to the provisions of article, the House of the People shall consist of —

 

(a) not more than 4 [five hundred and thirty members] chosen by direct election from territorial constituencies in the States, and

 

(b) not more than 5 [twenty members] to represent the Union territories, chosen in such manner as Parliament may by law provide.

 

(2) For the purposes of sub-clause (a) of clause (1),—

 

(a) there shall be allotted to each State a number of seats in the House of the People in such manner that the ratio between that number and the population of the State is, so far as practicable, the same for all States; and

 

(b) each State shall be divided into territorial constituencies in such manner that the ratio between the population of each constituency and the number of seats allotted to it is, so far as practicable, the same throughout the State:

 

Provided that the provisions of sub-clause (a) of this clause shall not be applicable for the purpose of allotment of seats in the House of the People to any State so long as the population of that State does not exceed six millions.

 

(3) In this article, the expression “population” means the population as ascertained at the last preceding census of which the relevant figures have been published:

 

Provided that the reference in this clause to the last preceding census of which the relevant figures have been published shall, until the relevant figures for the first census taken after the year 2026 have been published, be construed,—

 

(i) for the purposes of sub-clause (a) of clause (2) and the proviso to that clause, as a reference to the 1971 census; and

 

(ii) for the purposes of sub-clause (b) of clause (2) as a reference to the 2001 census.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the composition of the House of the People.

Also, the House of the People can have not more than five hundred and thirty members elected directly from the States. In addition, not more than twenty members can represent the Union Territories in a manner decided by Parliament.

Each State is given seats in such a way that the number of seats is based on its population. This means that the ratio between the population of a State and the number of seats given to it should be nearly equal for all States.

Also, every State is divided into territorial constituencies. The population of each constituency should be nearly equal so that each vote has similar value within the State.

However, this rule about equal ratio does not apply to a State whose population is less than six million.

In this article, population means the population counted in the last census whose figures are officially published. However, until the census taken after the year 2026 is published, certain seat allocations continue to be based on earlier census figures as specified.

Ex : A State with a larger population will have more elected members in the House of the People than a smaller State.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies the composition of the Lok Sabha.
The maximum strength is fixed at 552 (currently 550, as the Anglo-Indian nomination has been discontinued).
Up to 530 members represent the States, and up to 20 members represent the Union Territories.
Members are chosen by direct election on the basis of adult suffrage.
Seats are allocated to states in such a way that the ratio between the number of seats and the state's population is, as far as practicable, the same for all states.
Historical_Context: The Lok Sabha is the body that represents the will of the people directly. Its composition is based on population to ensure democratic representation. The provision for representation of Anglo-Indians by nomination (Article 331) was not extended by the 104th Amendment Act, 2019.
Landmark_Cases: Delimitation exercises, which redraw constituency boundaries based on census data, are based on the principles laid out in this article.
Related_Articles: Article 80 (Composition of the Council of States)
Article 326 (Elections...to be on the basis of adult suffrage)

Title: Readjustment after each census
Original_Description:
Upon the completion of each census, the allocation of seats in the House of the People to the States and the division of each State into territorial constituencies shall be readjusted by such authority and in such manner as Parliament may by law determine:

 

Provided that such readjustment shall not affect representation in the House of the People until the dissolution of the then existing House:

 

Provided further that such readjustment shall take effect from such date as the President may, by order, specify and until such readjustment takes effect, any election to the House may be held on the basis of the territorial constituencies existing before such readjustment:

 

Provided also that until the relevant figures for the first census taken after the year 2026 have been published, it shall not be necessary to readjust - 

 

(i) the allocation of seats in the House of People to the States as readjusted on the basis of the 1971 census; and

 

(ii) the division of each State into territorial constituencies as may be readjusted on the basis of the 2001 census,

 

under this article.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that after every census, the allocation of seats in the House of the People among the States must be readjusted.

Also, the division of each State into territorial constituencies must be readjusted in a manner decided by Parliament through law.

However, this readjustment does not affect the current House of the People until it is dissolved.

In addition, the changes will take effect from a date specified by the President. Until that date, elections may be held based on the old constituencies.

However, until the census taken after the year 2026 is published, it is not necessary to change the allocation of seats among States as previously fixed, and the division of constituencies continues as earlier determined.

Ex : After a new census shows population changes, constituency boundaries can be redrawn for future elections.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Mandates the readjustment of Lok Sabha seats among states after every census.
Also mandates the redrawing of territorial constituencies within each state.
Parliament is empowered to determine the authority (i.e., the Delimitation Commission) and the manner of this readjustment.
Such readjustment only takes effect from a date specified by the President and does not affect the existing Lok Sabha.
Constitutional amendments have frozen the allocation of seats among states based on the 1971 census.
Historical_Context: The purpose of this article is to ensure the democratic principle of 'one person, one vote, one value' by realigning political representation with demographic changes. However, the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976, froze the allocation of seats based on the 1971 census until the year 2000 to encourage states to pursue population control measures without fearing a loss of political representation. The 84th Amendment Act, 2001, extended this freeze until the first census after 2026, but allowed for the readjustment of constituencies within states based on the 1991 (later updated to 2001) census.
Landmark_Cases: The actions and recommendations of the Delimitation Commission, set up under laws made through this article, have been the subject of various legal proceedings, although the final orders of the Commission are not questionable in court.
Related_Articles: Article 81 (Composition of the House of the People)
Article 327 (Power of Parliament to make provision with respect to elections)

Title: Duration of Houses of Parliament
Original_Description:
(1) The Council of States shall not be subject to dissolution, but as nearly as possible one-third of the members thereof shall retire as soon as may be on the expiration of every second year in accordance with the provisions made in that behalf by Parliament by law.

 

(2) The House of the People, unless sooner dissolved, shall continue for five years from the date appointed for its first meeting and no longer and the expiration of the said period of five years shall operate as a dissolution of the House:

 

Provided that the said period may, while a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, be extended by Parliament by law for a period not exceeding one year at a time and not extending in any case beyond a period of six months after the Proclamation has ceased to operate.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the duration of the Houses of Parliament.

Also, the Council of States is a permanent body and is not dissolved. However, as nearly as possible, one-third of its members retire every two years according to law made by Parliament.

The House of the People continues for five years from the date of its first meeting, unless it is dissolved earlier.

However, during a national emergency, Parliament may extend this five-year period by law for not more than one year at a time. Such extension cannot continue beyond six months after the emergency ends.

Ex : The House of the People normally works for five years, but during an emergency it can be extended for a limited time.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: The Rajya Sabha (Council of States) is a permanent body and is not subject to dissolution.
One-third of the members of the Rajya Sabha retire every two years.
The Lok Sabha (House of the People) has a normal term of five years from the date of its first meeting.
The Lok Sabha can be dissolved before the completion of its term by the President.
The term of the Lok Sabha can be extended by Parliament for one year at a time during a national emergency.
Historical_Context: The continuous nature of the Rajya Sabha ensures there is always a legislative body at the Union level, providing stability. The fixed five-year term for the Lok Sabha ensures democratic accountability. During the Emergency, the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976, controversially extended the Lok Sabha's term to six years, but the 44th Amendment Act, 1978, restored it to five years.
Landmark_Cases: This article's provisions are fundamental to parliamentary procedure and are generally not a source of litigation, except in cases concerning the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.
Related_Articles: Article 85 (Sessions of Parliament, prorogation and dissolution)

Title: Qualification for membership of Parliament
Original_Description:
A person shall not be qualified to be chosen to fill a seat in Parliament unless he —

 

(a) is a citizen of India, and makes and subscribes before some person authorised in that behalf by the Election Commission an oath or affirmation according to the form set out for the purpose in the Third Schedule;

 

(b) is, in the case of a seat in the Council of States, not less than thirty years of age and, in the case of a seat in the House of the People, not less than twenty-five years of age; and

 

(c) possesses such other qualifications as may be prescribed in that behalf by or under any law made by Parliament.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the qualifications required to become a member of Parliament.

Also, a person must be a citizen of India and must take an oath or affirmation in the prescribed form before an authorized person.

In addition, a person must be at least thirty years old to become a member of the Council of States and at least twenty-five years old to become a member of the House of the People.

A person must also have any other qualifications that Parliament may prescribe by law.

Ex : A twenty-six year old Indian citizen can contest for the House of the People if other legal qualifications are fulfilled.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies the eligibility criteria for becoming a Member of Parliament (MP).
A candidate must be a citizen of India.
Minimum age for Rajya Sabha is 30 years.
Minimum age for Lok Sabha is 25 years.
Parliament can prescribe other qualifications by law (as done in the Representation of the People Act, 1951).
Historical_Context: These qualifications were set to ensure a minimum level of maturity and a fundamental commitment (citizenship) for the nation's lawmakers. The higher age limit for the Rajya Sabha reflects its intended role as the 'House of Elders', providing a more seasoned perspective on legislation.
Landmark_Cases: Cases related to the qualifications and disqualifications of MPs are often heard by the courts, usually interpreting the provisions of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, which was enacted under the authority of this article.

Title: Sessions of Parliament, prorogation and dissolution
Original_Description:
(1) The President shall from time to time summon each House of Parliament to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit, but six months shall not intervene between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session.

 

(2) The President may from time to time —

 

(a) prorogue the Houses or either House;

 

(b) dissolve the House of the People.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the power of the President to summon, prorogue, and dissolve Parliament.

Also, the President calls each House of Parliament to meet at a time and place considered appropriate.

However, there must not be a gap of more than six months between the last sitting of one session and the first sitting of the next session.

In addition, the President may end a session of either House and may dissolve the House of the People.

Ex : Parliament must meet at least once within every six months.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: The President has the power to summon (call a session), prorogue (end a session), and dissolve the Lok Sabha.
The maximum gap between two sessions of Parliament cannot be more than six months.
Prorogation ends a session, while dissolution ends the life of the Lok Sabha itself.
The Rajya Sabha, being a permanent house, is not subject to dissolution.
Historical_Context: This article establishes the executive's power over the legislative schedule. The six-month rule is a crucial safeguard that prevents a government from avoiding parliamentary scrutiny for an extended period. The President's power to dissolve the Lok Sabha is a key feature of the parliamentary system, exercised on the advice of the Council of Ministers, which can lead to early elections.
Landmark_Cases: S.R. Bommai vs Union of India (1994): While primarily about state governments, the case discussed the principles of parliamentary democracy and the confidence of the house, which are relevant to the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.
Related_Articles: Article 74 (Council of Ministers to aid and advise President)

Title: Right of President to address and send messages to Houses
Original_Description:
(1) The President may address either House of Parliament or both Houses assembled together, and for that purpose require the attendance of members.

 

(2) The President may send messages to either House of Parliament, whether with respect to a Bill then pending in Parliament or otherwise, and a House to which any message is so sent shall with all convenient despatch consider any matter required by the message to be taken into consideration
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the right of the President to address and send messages to the Houses of Parliament.

Also, the President may address either House separately or both Houses together and may require members to attend for that purpose.

In addition, the President may send messages to either House regarding a pending Bill or any other matter. The House receiving the message must consider the matter without unnecessary delay.

Ex : The President may address both Houses together to speak on an important national matter.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Gives the President the right to address either House of Parliament or a joint sitting of both Houses.
Gives the President the right to send messages to Parliament, concerning a pending bill or any other matter.
The House to which a message is sent must consider it with 'all convenient despatch'.
Historical_Context: This is a general power of the President to communicate with Parliament. It is distinct from the 'special address' under Article 87. The right to send messages has been rarely used, as formal communication between the executive and legislature typically happens through ministers.
Landmark_Cases: This article outlines a power of the President and is not typically a subject of legal disputes.

Title: Special address by the President
Original_Description:
(1) At the commencement of the first session after each general election to the House of the People and at the commencement of the first session of each year the President shall address both Houses of Parliament assembled together and inform Parliament of the causes of its summons.

 

(2) Provision shall be made by the rules regulating the procedure of either House for the allotment of time for discussion of the matters referred to in such address.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President must give a special address to both Houses of Parliament assembled together on certain occasions.

Also, this address must be given at the beginning of the first session after each general election to the House of the People and at the beginning of the first session each year.

In this address, the President informs Parliament about the reasons for summoning the session and outlines the matters to be considered.

In addition, time must be provided in both Houses to discuss the matters mentioned in the address.

Ex : At the start of the year, the President addresses Parliament and explains the government’s plans.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Mandates a 'special address' by the President on two occasions: the first session after a general election, and the first session of every year.
The address is delivered to both Houses of Parliament assembled together.
In the address, the President informs Parliament of the 'causes of its summons', which essentially means outlining the government's policies and legislative agenda for the upcoming year.
The address is followed by a 'Motion of Thanks' which is debated and voted upon.
Historical_Context: This procedure is derived from the 'Speech from the Throne' in the British Parliament. The President's address is prepared by the government and serves as a statement of its policy intentions. The defeat of a Motion of Thanks is considered a vote of no-confidence and can lead to the government's resignation.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article and is a part of established parliamentary practice.
Related_Articles: Article 86 (Right of President to address and send messages to Houses)

Title: Rights of Ministers and Attorney General as respects Houses
Original_Description: Every Minister and the Attorney-General of India shall have the right to speak in, and otherwise to take part in the proceedings of, either House, any joint sitting of the Houses, and any committee of Parliament of which he may be named a member, but shall not by virtue of this article be entitled to vote.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the rights of Ministers and the Attorney General in relation to the Houses of Parliament.

Also, every Minister and the Attorney General has the right to speak and take part in the proceedings of either House, any joint sitting, and any committee of which they are a member.

However, they are not entitled to vote in a House unless they are a member of that House.

This means they can participate in discussions but cannot vote if they are not members.

Ex : A Minister who is not a member of the Lok Sabha can speak there but cannot vote.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Grants Ministers and the Attorney-General the right to speak and participate in the proceedings of both the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha.
They can also participate in joint sittings and parliamentary committees of which they are members.
Crucially, it clarifies that this right to participate does not give them the right to vote in a House of which they are not a member.
Historical_Context:
This provision allows for better coordination and functioning of the government. 

 
Ex : a minister who is a member of the Rajya Sabha can go to the Lok Sabha to introduce a bill or answer questions related to their ministry, and vice versa. It also allows the government's chief legal officer, the Attorney-General, to present the government's legal position directly to Parliament.
Landmark_Cases: This article defines the rights of participation and is a clear procedural rule.

Title: The Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Council of States
Original_Description:
(1) The Vice-President of India shall be ex officio Chairman of the Council of States.

 

(2) The Council of States shall, as soon as may be, choose a member of the Council to be Deputy Chairman thereof and, so often as the office of Deputy Chairman becomes vacant, the Council shall choose another member to be Deputy Chairman thereof.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Vice-President of India shall be the Chairman of the Council of States.

Also, the Council of States must choose one of its members to be the Deputy Chairman.

Whenever the office of Deputy Chairman becomes vacant, the Council must choose another member to fill the position.

Ex : The Rajya Sabha elects one of its members to serve as Deputy Chairman.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Declares that the Vice-President of India is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.
Mandates that the Rajya Sabha shall elect one of its own members to be the Deputy Chairman.
Historical_Context: This article establishes the leadership structure of the Rajya Sabha. Having the Vice-President as the Chairman provides the house with a presiding officer of high rank who is not a member of any political party within the house. The Deputy Chairman, elected from among the members, presides in the Chairman's absence and is usually a member of the ruling coalition.
Landmark_Cases: This is a structural article and not a subject of legal dispute.
Related_Articles: Article 64 (The Vice-President to be ex officio Chairman...)
Article 93 (The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of the People)

Title: Vacation and resignation of, and removal from, the office of Deputy Chairman
Original_Description:
A member holding office as Deputy Chairman of the Council of States —

 

(a) shall vacate his office if he ceases to be a member of the Council;

 

(b) may at any time, by writing under his hand addressed to the Chairman, resign his office; and

 

(c) may be removed from his office by a resolution of the Council passed by a majority of all the then members of the Council:

 

Provided that no resolution for the purpose of clause (c) shall be moved unless at least fourteen days’ notice has been given of the intention to move the resolution.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how the office of the Deputy Chairman of the Council of States can become vacant.

Also, the Deputy Chairman must vacate the office if he ceases to be a member of the Council.

In addition, the Deputy Chairman may resign by writing to the Chairman.

The Deputy Chairman may also be removed by a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the Council. However, at least fourteen days notice must be given before moving such a resolution.

Ex : If the Rajya Sabha passes a proper resolution after notice, the Deputy Chairman can be removed.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies how the office of the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha can become vacant.
The Deputy Chairman must vacate the office if they cease to be a member of the Rajya Sabha.
They can resign by writing to the Chairman (the Vice-President).
They can be removed by a resolution passed by the Rajya Sabha with an 'effective majority' (a majority of all the then members of the Council).
Historical_Context: This article outlines the procedures for ensuring accountability and transition in the office of the Deputy Chairman, mirroring similar provisions for other presiding officers.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article governing the functioning of the Rajya Sabha.
Related_Articles: Article 94 (Vacation and resignation of... Speaker and Deputy Speaker)

Title: Power of the Deputy Chairman or other person to perform the duties of the office of, or to act as, Chairman
Original_Description:
(1) While the office of Chairman is vacant, or during any period when the Vice-President is acting as, or discharging the functions of, President, the duties of the office shall be performed by the Deputy Chairman, or, if the office of Deputy Chairman is also vacant, by such member of the Council of States as the President may appoint for the purpose.

 

(2) During the absence of the Chairman from any sitting of the Council of States the Deputy Chairman, or, if he is also absent, such person as may be determined by the rules of procedure of the Council, or, if no such person is present, such other person as may be determined by the Council, shall act as Chairman.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains who will perform the duties of the Chairman of the Council of States in certain situations.

Also, if the office of Chairman is vacant or if the Vice-President is acting as President, the Deputy Chairman will perform the duties of Chairman.

If the office of Deputy Chairman is also vacant, a member appointed by the President will perform those duties.

In addition, during the absence of the Chairman from a sitting, the Deputy Chairman or another person as determined by the rules or by the Council will act as Chairman.

Ex : If the Chairman is absent from a meeting, the Deputy Chairman will preside.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: The Deputy Chairman performs the duties of the Chairman when the Chairman's office is vacant.
The Deputy Chairman also performs the Chairman's duties when the Vice-President is acting as the President.
The Deputy Chairman presides over the Rajya Sabha during the temporary absence of the Chairman.
If the Deputy Chairman is also absent, a member from the panel of Vice-Chairpersons (determined by the rules of the House) presides.
Historical_Context: This article ensures that the proceedings of the Rajya Sabha can continue uninterrupted even in the absence or vacancy of the Chairman's office.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural provision for the internal functioning of Parliament.
Related_Articles: Article 65 (The Vice-President to act as President...)

Title: The Chairman or the Deputy Chairman not to preside while a resolution for his removal from office is under consideration
Original_Description:
(1) At any sitting of the Council of States, while any resolution for the removal of the Vice-President from his office is under consideration, the Chairman, or while any resolution for the removal of the Deputy Chairman from his office is under consideration, the Deputy Chairman, shall not, though he is present, preside, and the provisions of clause (2) of article 91 shall apply in relation to every such sitting as they apply in relation to a sitting from which the Chairman, or, as the case may be, the Deputy Chairman, is absent.

 

(2) The Chairman shall have the right to speak in, and otherwise to take part in the proceedings of, the Council of States while any resolution for the removal of the Vice-President from his office is under consideration in the Council, but, notwithstanding anything in article 100, shall not be entitled to vote at all on such resolution or on any other matter during such proceedings.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Chairman or Deputy Chairman cannot preside over the Council of States when a resolution for their removal is under consideration.

Also, during such a sitting, another person will preside according to the rules.

In addition, the Chairman has the right to speak and take part in the proceedings when a resolution for removal from office is being considered. However, he cannot vote on that resolution or on any other matter during those proceedings.

Ex : If the Rajya Sabha is discussing removal of the Vice-President as Chairman, he cannot preside over that discussion.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Prohibits the Chairman (Vice-President) from presiding over the Rajya Sabha when a resolution for his removal is being considered.
Similarly, the Deputy Chairman cannot preside when a resolution for their removal is under consideration.
The person facing removal can be present, speak, and participate in the proceedings.
