The Chairman (Vice-President) is not entitled to vote at all on the resolution for their removal.
Historical_Context: This provision is based on the principles of natural justice, ensuring that a person does not act as a judge in their own cause. It guarantees a fair process when the removal of a presiding officer is being debated.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural rule of Parliament and has not been the subject of court cases.
Related_Articles: Article 67 (Term of office of Vice-President, including removal)
Article 90 (Removal from the office of Deputy Chairman)
Article 96 (Similar provision for the Speaker and Deputy Speaker)

Title: The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of the People
Original_Description: The House of the People shall, as soon as may be, choose two members of the House to be respectively Speaker and Deputy Speaker thereof and, so often as the office of Speaker or Deputy Speaker becomes vacant, the House shall choose another member to be Speaker or Deputy Speaker, as the case may be.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the House of the People must choose two of its members to be the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker.

Also, this choice must be made as soon as possible.

Whenever the office of Speaker or Deputy Speaker becomes vacant, the House must choose another member to fill that position.

Ex : After general elections, the Lok Sabha elects a Speaker from among its members.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Mandates that the Lok Sabha must choose two of its members to be the Speaker and Deputy Speaker.
The election of the Speaker is one of the first acts of a newly constituted Lok Sabha.
When the office of Speaker or Deputy Speaker becomes vacant, the House must elect another member to fill the vacancy.
Historical_Context: The offices of the Speaker and Deputy Speaker are crucial for the functioning of the Lok Sabha. The Speaker is the presiding officer and the custodian of the powers and privileges of the House. By convention, the Speaker is usually from the ruling party, and the post of Deputy Speaker is offered to the opposition, although this is not always followed.
Landmark_Cases: This is a foundational article for the structure of the Lok Sabha.
Related_Articles: Article 89 (The Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Council of States)

Title: Vacation and resignation of, and removal from, the offices of Speaker and Deputy Speaker
Original_Description:
A member holding office as Speaker or Deputy Speaker of the House of the People —

 

(a) shall vacate his office if he ceases to be a member of the House of the People;

 

(b) may at any time, by writing under his hand addressed, if such member is the Speaker, to the Deputy Speaker, and if such member is the Deputy Speaker, to the Speaker, resign his office; and

 

(c) may be removed from his office by a resolution of the House of the People passed by a majority of all the then members of the House:

 

Provided that no resolution for the purpose of clause (c) shall be moved unless at least fourteen days' notice has been given of the intention to move the resolution: Provided further that, whenever the House of the People is dissolved, the Speaker shall not vacate his office until immediately before the first meeting of the House of the People after the dissolution.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how the offices of Speaker and Deputy Speaker can become vacant.

Also, a person holding either office must vacate it if he ceases to be a member of the House of the People.

In addition, the Speaker may resign by writing to the Deputy Speaker, and the Deputy Speaker may resign by writing to the Speaker.

They may also be removed by a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the House, but at least fourteen days notice must be given before moving such a resolution.

However, when the House of the People is dissolved, the Speaker continues in office until just before the first meeting of the new House.

Ex : Even after the Lok Sabha is dissolved, the Speaker continues until the new House meets.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies how the offices of Speaker and Deputy Speaker become vacant.
They must vacate their office if they cease to be a member of the Lok Sabha.
The Speaker can resign by writing to the Deputy Speaker, and vice versa.
They can be removed by a resolution passed by an 'effective majority' (majority of all the then members) of the Lok Sabha.
The Speaker does not vacate their office upon dissolution of the Lok Sabha but continues until the first meeting of the new House.
Historical_Context: The provision for the Speaker to continue in office until the new Lok Sabha meets ensures that there is no vacuum in the office. This is important as the Speaker has administrative duties and represents the House even when it is dissolved.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article governing the functioning of the Lok Sabha.
Related_Articles: Article 90 (Vacation and resignation of... Deputy Chairman)

Title: Power of the Deputy Speaker or other person to perform the duties of the office of, or to act as, Speaker
Original_Description:
(1) While the office of Speaker is vacant, the duties of the office shall be performed by the Deputy Speaker or, if the office of Deputy Speaker is also vacant, by such member of the House of the People as the President may appoint for the purpose.

 

(2) During the absence of the Speaker from any sitting of the House of the People the Deputy Speaker or, if he is also absent, such person as may be determined by the rules of procedure of the House, or, if no such person is present, such other person as may be determined by the House, shall act as Speaker.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains who will perform the duties of the Speaker of the House of the People in certain situations.

Also, if the office of Speaker is vacant, the Deputy Speaker will perform the duties.

If the office of Deputy Speaker is also vacant, a member appointed by the President will perform those duties.

In addition, during the absence of the Speaker from a sitting, the Deputy Speaker or another person determined by the rules or by the House will act as Speaker.

Ex : If the Speaker is absent from a meeting, the Deputy Speaker will preside.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: The Deputy Speaker performs the duties of the Speaker when the Speaker's office is vacant.
The Deputy Speaker presides over the Lok Sabha during the temporary absence of the Speaker.
If the office of the Deputy Speaker is also vacant, the President can appoint a member of the Lok Sabha to perform the Speaker's duties.
If both are absent from a sitting, a member from the panel of Chairpersons presides.
Historical_Context: This article ensures the uninterrupted functioning of the Lok Sabha. The recent vacancy in the post of Deputy Speaker for an extended period has led to debates about the mandatory nature ('shall be performed') of this article.
Landmark_Cases: A petition has been filed in the Supreme Court concerning the non-election of a Deputy Speaker, which may lead to a judicial interpretation of this article.

Title: The Speaker or the Deputy Speaker not to preside while a resolution for his removal from office is under consideration
Original_Description:
(1) At any sitting of the House of the People, while any resolution for the removal of the Speaker from his office is under consideration, the Speaker, or while any resolution for the removal of the Deputy Speaker from his office is under consideration, the Deputy Speaker, shall not, though he is present, preside, and the provisions of clause (2) of article 95 shall apply in relation to every such sitting as they apply in relation to a sitting from which the Speaker, or, as the case may be, the Deputy Speaker, is absent.

 

(2) The Speaker shall have the right to speak in, and otherwise to take part in the proceedings of, the House of the People while any resolution for his removal from office is under consideration in the House and shall, notwithstanding anything in article 100, be entitled to vote only in the first instance on such resolution or on any other matter during such proceedings but not in the case of an equality of votes
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Speaker or Deputy Speaker cannot preside over the House of the People when a resolution for their removal is under consideration.

Also, during such proceedings, another person will preside according to the rules.

In addition, the Speaker has the right to speak and take part in the discussion during consideration of the resolution for removal.

However, during such proceedings, the Speaker can vote only in the first instance and cannot use a casting vote in case of equality of votes.

Ex : If the Lok Sabha is discussing removal of the Speaker, the Speaker cannot preside over that discussion.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Prohibits the Speaker or Deputy Speaker from presiding over the Lok Sabha when a resolution for their removal is being considered.
The person facing removal has the right to be present, speak, and participate in the proceedings.
When such a resolution is being debated, the Speaker can vote in the first instance like any other member, but loses their casting vote in case of a tie.
Historical_Context: This provision is based on the principles of natural justice, ensuring fairness in the process of removing a presiding officer. The change in voting rights (from casting vote to ordinary vote) reflects their temporary status as an ordinary member during the proceedings.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural rule of Parliament and has not been a subject of court cases.
Related_Articles: Article 92 (Similar provision for the Chairman and Deputy Chairman)
Article 94 (Removal from the offices of Speaker and Deputy Speaker)

Title: Salaries and allowances of the Chairman and Deputy Chairman and the Speaker and Deputy Speaker
Original_Description: There shall be paid to the Chairman and the Deputy Chairman of the Council of States, and to the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker of the House of the People, such salaries and allowances as may be respectively fixed by Parliament by law and, until provision in that behalf is so made, such salaries and allowances as are specified in the Second Schedule.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that salaries and allowances of the Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Council of States and the Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of the People are fixed by Parliament by law.

Also, until Parliament makes such a law, their salaries and allowances are as specified in the Second Schedule.

This means that Parliament decides how much these presiding officers are paid.

Ex : Parliament can pass a law to increase the salary of the Speaker.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to determine the salaries and allowances of the presiding officers of both Houses.
This includes the Chairman & Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha and the Speaker & Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha.
Until Parliament makes a law, their salaries are as specified in the Second Schedule.
These salaries are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India and are not subject to the annual vote of Parliament.
Historical_Context: By charging the salaries of presiding officers on the Consolidated Fund, the Constitution ensures their independence from the executive and from the political pressures of the annual budget process.
Landmark_Cases: This is a financial and procedural article.
Related_Articles: Article 112(3) (Expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India)

Title: Secretariat of Parliament
Original_Description:
(1) Each House of Parliament shall have a separate secretarial staff:

 

Provided that nothing in this clause shall be construed as preventing the creation of posts common to both Houses of Parliament.

 

(2) Parliament may by law regulate the recruitment, and the conditions of service of persons appointed, to the secretarial staff of either House of Parliament.

 

(3) Until provision is made by Parliament under clause (2), the President may, after consultation with the Speaker of the House of the People or the Chairman of the Council of States, as the case may be, make rules regulating the recruitment, and the conditions of service of persons appointed, to the secretarial staff of the House of the People or the Council of States, and any rules so made shall have effect subject to the provisions of any law made under the said clause.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that each House of Parliament shall have its own separate secretarial staff.

Also, posts common to both Houses may be created if needed.

In addition, Parliament may make laws to regulate recruitment and service conditions of the secretarial staff of each House.

Until Parliament makes such laws, the President may make rules after consulting the Speaker or the Chairman, and those rules will apply.

Ex : The Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha have their own administrative staff.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Mandates a separate secretarial staff for each House of Parliament.
Allows for the creation of posts common to both Houses.
Empowers Parliament to regulate the recruitment and service conditions of the secretarial staff.
Until Parliament does so, the President makes rules in consultation with the Speaker or the Chairman.
Historical_Context: An independent secretariat for each House is crucial for maintaining the independence of the legislature from the executive. The secretariats of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha function under the overall control of the Speaker and Chairman respectively, not under any government ministry.
Landmark_Cases: This article ensures the administrative independence of Parliament.

Title: Oath or affirmation by members
Original_Description: Every member of either House of Parliament shall, before taking his seat, make and subscribe before the President, or some person appointed in that behalf by him, an oath or affirmation according to the form set out for the purpose in the Third Schedule.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every member of either House of Parliament must take an oath or make an affirmation before taking their seat.

Also, this oath is taken before the President or a person appointed by the President.

The form of the oath is given in the Third Schedule.

This means that a member cannot participate in the proceedings until the oath is taken.

Ex : A newly elected Member of Parliament must take oath before sitting in the House.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Requires every Member of Parliament (MP) to take an oath or make an affirmation before taking their seat in the House.
The oath is administered by the President or a person appointed by the President (usually the pro-tem Speaker).
The form of the oath is prescribed in the Third Schedule.
The oath is to bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution and to uphold the sovereignty and integrity of India.
Historical_Context: The oath-taking ceremony is a solemn procedure that marks the official commencement of an MP's term. It binds them to the principles of the Constitution. A member cannot participate in the proceedings of the House or vote without taking the oath.
Landmark_Cases: This is a mandatory procedural requirement.
Related_Articles: Article 104 (Penalty for sitting and voting before making oath...)
Third Schedule (Forms of Oaths or Affirmations)

Title: Voting in Houses, power of Houses to act notwithstanding vacancies and quorum
Original_Description:
(1) Save as otherwise provided in this Constitution, all questions at any sitting of either House or joint sitting of the Houses shall be determined by a majority of votes of the members present and voting, other than the Speaker or person acting as Chairman or Speaker.

 

The Chairman or Speaker, or person acting as such, shall not vote in the first instance, but shall have and exercise a casting vote in the case of an equality of votes.

 

(2) Either House of Parliament shall have power to act notwithstanding any vacancy in the membership thereof, and any proceedings in Parliament shall be valid notwithstanding that it is discovered subsequently that some person who was not entitled so to do sat or voted or otherwise took part in the proceedings.

 

(3) Until Parliament by law otherwise provides, the quorum to constitute a meeting of either House of Parliament shall be one-tenth of the total number of members of the House. (4) If at any time during a meeting of a House there is no quorum, it shall be the duty of the Chairman or Speaker, or person acting as such, either to adjourn the House or to suspend the meeting until there is a quorum.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how voting takes place in the Houses of Parliament and about quorum.

Also, unless otherwise provided in the Constitution, decisions in either House or in a joint sitting are made by a majority of members present and voting, excluding the presiding officer.

The Chairman or Speaker does not vote in the first instance but has a casting vote in case of equality of votes.

In addition, the House can continue its work even if there are vacancies among its members. Proceedings remain valid even if later it is found that someone who was not entitled took part.

Until Parliament provides otherwise by law, the quorum for a meeting is one-tenth of the total number of members of the House.

If at any time there is no quorum, the presiding officer must either adjourn the House or suspend the meeting until quorum is present.

Ex : If a vote results in equal numbers on both sides, the Speaker can use a casting vote to decide.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Decisions in the Houses are made by a majority of members present and voting.
The presiding officer (Speaker/Chairman) does not vote in the first instance but has a 'casting vote' in case of a tie.
The quorum (minimum number of members required to be present) for a meeting is one-tenth of the total membership of the House.
The proceedings of a House are valid even if there are vacancies in its membership.
Historical_Context: This article lays down the basic rules for decision-making in Parliament. The concept of a casting vote for the presiding officer is a common feature in Westminster-style parliaments, meant to be used to maintain the status quo. The quorum requirement ensures that a small number of members cannot take major decisions.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article governing parliamentary business.
Related_Articles: Article 96 (Voting by Speaker during removal resolution)

Title: Vacation of seats
Original_Description:
(1) No person shall be a member of both Houses of Parliament and provision shall be made by Parliament by law for the vacation by a person who is chosen a member of both Houses of his seat in one House or the other.

 

(2) No person shall be a member both of Parliament and of a House of the Legislature of a State and if a person is chosen a member both of Parliament and of a House of the Legislature of a State, then, at the expiration of such period as may be specified in rules made by the President, that person's seat in Parliament shall become vacant, unless he has previously resigned his seat in the Legislature of the State.

 

(3) If a member of either House of Parliament —

 

(a) becomes subject to any of the disqualifications mentioned in clause (1) or clause (2) of article 102, or 5

 

(b) resigns his seat by writing under his hand addressed to the Chairman or the Speaker, as the case may be, and his resignation is accepted by the Chairman or the Speaker, as the case may be, his seat shall thereupon become vacant:

 

his seat shall thereupon become vacant:

 

Provided that in the case of any resignation referred to in sub-clause (b), if from information received or otherwise and after making such inquiry as he thinks fit, the Chairman or the Speaker, as the case may be, is satisfied that such resignation is not voluntary or genuine, he shall not accept such resignation.

 

(4) If for a period of sixty days a member of either House of Parliament is without permission of the House absent from all meetings thereof, the House may declare his seat vacant:

 

Provided that in computing the said period of sixty days no account shall be taken of any period during which the House is prorogued or is adjourned for more than four consecutive days.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains when a seat in Parliament becomes vacant.

Also, no person can be a member of both Houses of Parliament at the same time. If a person is chosen as a member of both Houses, he must give up one seat according to the law made by Parliament.

In addition, a person cannot be a member of Parliament and also a member of a State Legislature at the same time. If someone is elected to both, his seat in Parliament will become vacant after a specified period unless he resigns from the State Legislature first.

A seat will also become vacant if a member becomes disqualified under the Constitution or resigns by writing to the Chairman or Speaker and the resignation is accepted.

However, if the Chairman or Speaker finds that the resignation is not voluntary or genuine, it will not be accepted.

If a member is absent from all meetings of the House for sixty days without permission, the House may declare the seat vacant. The period when the House is not meeting for long breaks is not counted in these sixty days.

Ex : If a Member of Parliament does not attend the House for sixty days without permission, the House can declare his seat vacant.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Lists the circumstances under which a Member of Parliament's seat becomes vacant.
A person cannot be a member of both Houses of Parliament simultaneously.
A person cannot be a member of both Parliament and a State Legislature simultaneously.
A seat becomes vacant if a member resigns, is disqualified, or is absent from the sittings of the House for 60 days without permission.
Historical_Context: These provisions ensure that members are committed to a single legislative body and are actively participating in its proceedings. The rule against dual membership is fundamental to the clear division of roles in a federal structure. The rule on absence is to ensure accountability and regular participation.
Landmark_Cases: The process of accepting resignations and the grounds for disqualification have been subject to judicial scrutiny.

Title: Disqualifications for membership
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the disqualifications for being a member of Parliament.

Also, a person will be disqualified if he holds an office of profit under the Government of India or a State Government, unless Parliament has declared that office not to cause disqualification.

In addition, a person is disqualified if he is declared by a court to be of unsound mind, if he is an undischarged insolvent, or if he is not a citizen of India or has voluntarily taken citizenship of another country.

A person may also be disqualified under any law made by Parliament.

However, being a Minister does not count as holding an office of profit for this purpose.

A person is also disqualified if he is disqualified under the Tenth Schedule.

Ex : If a Member of Parliament becomes a citizen of another country, he will lose his membership.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Lists the grounds for disqualification of a Member of Parliament.
These include: holding an office of profit, being of unsound mind, being an undischarged insolvent, not being an Indian citizen, and being disqualified under any law made by Parliament.
This last point (1e) allows Parliament to add more grounds for disqualification, which it has done through the Representation of the People Act, 1951.
Disqualification under the Tenth Schedule (anti-defection law) is also a ground.
Historical_Context: These disqualifications are meant to ensure that legislators are of sound character, are not under conflicting obligations (office of profit), and maintain allegiance to the country. The Representation of the People Act adds further disqualifications, such as conviction for certain criminal offenses.
Landmark_Cases: Jaya Bachchan vs Union of India (2006): A key case that clarified the meaning of 'office of profit'.
Lily Thomas vs Union of India (2013): The Supreme Court struck down a provision in the Representation of the People Act that allowed convicted MPs/MLAs to continue in office if they filed an appeal.
Related_Articles: Article 103 (Decision on questions as to disqualifications)

Title: Decision on questions as to disqualifications of members
Original_Description:
(1) If any question arises as to whether a member of either House of Parliament has become subject to any of the disqualifications mentioned in clause (1) of article 102, the question shall be referred for the decision of the President and his decision shall be final.

 

(2) Before giving any decision on any such question, the President shall obtain the opinion of the Election Commission and shall act according to such opinion.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains who decides if a member of Parliament has become disqualified.

Also, if any question arises about disqualification under the Constitution, the matter is referred to the President.

Before making a decision, the President must obtain the opinion of the Election Commission and must act according to that opinion.

This means the President cannot decide the matter alone and must follow the advice given by the Election Commission.

Ex : If there is a doubt that a Member of Parliament holds an office of profit, the President decides the matter after taking the Election Commission’s opinion.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies the authority to decide on disqualification of an MP under Article 102(1).
The decision is made by the President of India.
Before making the decision, the President must obtain the opinion of the Election Commission of India.
The President is bound to act according to the Election Commission's opinion.
This article does not apply to disqualification on grounds of defection (which is decided by the Presiding Officer under the Tenth Schedule).
Historical_Context: This article provides a clear mechanism for adjudicating disqualification cases (other than defection). By involving the Election Commission, a quasi-judicial body, and making its advice binding, the process is intended to be fair and based on expert opinion, rather than being a purely political decision.
Landmark_Cases: This process has been followed in several 'office of profit' cases and other disqualification matters referred to the President.

Title: Penalty for sitting and voting before making oath or affirmation under article 99 or when not qualified or when disqualified
Original_Description: If a person sits or votes as a member of either House of Parliament before he has complied with the requirements of article 99, or when he knows that he is not qualified or that he is disqualified for membership thereof, or that he is prohibited from so doing by the provisions of any law made by Parliament, he shall be liable in respect of each day on which he so sits or votes to a penalty of five hundred rupees to be recovered as a debt due to the Union.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the penalty for sitting or voting in Parliament without proper qualification.

Also, if a person sits or votes before taking the required oath, or if he knows that he is not qualified or has been disqualified, he must pay a penalty.

The penalty is five hundred rupees for each day he sits or votes in the House in such a situation.

This amount is recovered as a debt due to the Union.

Ex : If a newly elected member votes before taking oath, he must pay a fine for each day he did so.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Imposes a penalty for sitting and voting in Parliament under certain circumstances.
The penalty is ₹500 per day.
It applies if a person participates without taking the oath (under Article 99).
It also applies if a person participates knowing they are not qualified or have been disqualified.
Historical_Context: This article acts as a deterrent and enforcement mechanism for the rules regarding oath-taking and qualification for membership in Parliament, ensuring that only duly qualified and sworn-in members participate in legislative proceedings.
Landmark_Cases: This is a penal provision and has been invoked in rare instances.

Title: Powers, privileges, etc, of the Houses of Parliament and of the members and committees thereof
Original_Description:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution and to the rules and standing orders regulating the procedure of Parliament, there shall be freedom of speech in Parliament.

 

(2) No member of Parliament shall be liable to any proceedings in any court in respect of any thing said or any vote given by him in Parliament or any committee thereof, and no person shall be so liable in respect of the publication by or under the authority of either House of Parliament of any report, paper, votes or proceedings.

 

(3) In other respects, the powers, privileges and immunities of each House of Parliament, and of the members and the committees of each House, shall be such as may from time to time be defined by Parliament by law, and, until so defined, shall be those of that House and of its members and committees immediately before the coming into force of section 15 of the Constitution (Forty-fourth Amendment) Act, 1978.

 

(4) The provisions of clauses (1), (2) and (3) shall apply in relation to persons who by virtue of this Constitution have the right to speak in, and otherwise to take part in the proceedings of, a House of Parliament or any committee thereof as they apply in relation to members of Parliament.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the powers and privileges of the Houses of Parliament and their members.

Also, there is freedom of speech in Parliament, subject to the Constitution and the rules of the House.

In addition, no member can be taken to court for anything said or any vote given in Parliament or its committees.

No person can be taken to court for publishing reports or proceedings of Parliament if it is done under authority of the House.

Other powers and privileges of each House and its members are defined by Parliament by law. Until such law is made, they continue as they existed earlier.

These protections also apply to persons who have the right to speak in Parliament even if they are not members.

Ex : A Member of Parliament cannot be sued in court for something he said during a debate in the House.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Guarantees freedom of speech for Members of Parliament within the House.
Provides absolute immunity to MPs from any court proceedings for anything said or any vote cast in Parliament or its committees.
States that other powers, privileges, and immunities of the Houses and their members are to be defined by Parliament by law.
This includes the power to punish for contempt or breach of privilege.
Historical_Context: Parliamentary privileges are essential for the independence and effectiveness of the legislature. They allow MPs to debate freely without fear of legal consequences. The scope of these privileges, particularly the power to punish for contempt and their relationship with fundamental rights, has been a recurring point of tension between the Parliament and the judiciary.
Landmark_Cases: Keshav Singh's Case (1965): A landmark case that dealt with the conflict between legislative privileges under Article 105 and an individual's fundamental rights, particularly Article 21.

P.V. Narasimha Rao vs State (CBI) (1998): The Supreme Court held that the immunity under Article 105(2) extends to bribery cases, i.e., an MP cannot be prosecuted for taking a bribe to vote or speak in Parliament. (This was later overruled in part in 2024).
Related_Articles: Article 19(1)(a) (Freedom of speech for citizens, which is not absolute)
Article 194 (Powers, privileges, etc., of the Houses of Legislatures of States)

Title: Salaries and allowances of members
Original_Description: Members of either House of Parliament shall be entitled to receive such salaries and allowances as may from time to time be determined by Parliament by law and, until provision in that respect is so made, allowances at such rates and upon such conditions as were immediately before the commencement of this Constitution applicable in the case of members of the Constituent Assembly of the Dominion of India.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that members of Parliament are entitled to salaries and allowances.

Also, Parliament decides the amount of salaries and allowances by making a law.

Until such a law is made, the allowances continue as they were before the Constitution came into force.

This means Parliament has the authority to fix how much its members are paid.

Ex : Parliament can pass a law to revise the salary of Members of Parliament.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to determine the salaries and allowances of its own members.
This is done by enacting a law.
Until such a law is made, the allowances were to be the same as for members of the Constituent Assembly.
Historical_Context: This article gives the legislature autonomy in determining the financial compensation for its members. The salaries and allowances of MPs are periodically revised by Parliament through legislation, which has sometimes been a subject of public debate.
Landmark_Cases: This is a financial and procedural article.
Related_Articles: Article 195 (Salaries and allowances of members of State Legislatures)

Title: Provisions as to introduction and passing of Bills
Original_Description:
(1) Subject to the provisions of articles 109 and 117 with respect to Money Bills and other financial Bills, a Bill may originate in either House of Parliament.

 

(2) Subject to the provisions of articles 108 and 109, a Bill shall not be deemed to have been passed by the Houses of Parliament unless it has been agreed to by both Houses, either without amendment or with such amendments only as are agreed to by both Houses.

 

(3) A Bill pending in Parliament shall not lapse by reason of the prorogation of the Houses.

 

(4) A Bill pending in the Council of States which has not been passed by the House of the People shall not lapse on a dissolution of the House of the People.

 

(5) A Bill which is pending in the House of the People, or which having been passed by the House of the People is pending in the Council of States, shall, subject to the provisions of article 108, lapse on a dissolution of the House of the People.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how Bills are introduced and passed in Parliament.

Also, except for Money Bills and certain financial Bills, a Bill can be introduced in either House of Parliament.

A Bill is considered passed only when both Houses agree to it, either without changes or with amendments agreed by both.

If a session ends, a pending Bill does not lapse.

However, if the House of the People is dissolved, a Bill pending in that House or passed by it but pending in the Council of States will lapse.

A Bill pending only in the Council of States does not lapse on dissolution of the House of the People.

Ex : If a Bill is still pending in the Lok Sabha and the Lok Sabha is dissolved, that Bill will lapse.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: An ordinary bill can be introduced in either the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha.
A bill must be passed by both Houses to become law.
Prorogation (ending a session) of the House does not lead to the lapsing of a bill.
Dissolution of the Lok Sabha leads to the lapsing of all bills pending in the Lok Sabha.
Bills passed by the Lok Sabha but pending in the Rajya Sabha also lapse on dissolution.
A bill pending in the Rajya Sabha which has not been passed by the Lok Sabha does not lapse.
Historical_Context: This article lays down the fundamental rules of the legislative process, particularly the life cycle of a bill. The rules about the lapsing of bills are important for understanding the impact of parliamentary sessions and general elections on pending legislation.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article central to the legislative process.
Related_Articles: Article 109 (Special procedure in respect of Money Bills)

Title: Joint sitting of both Houses in certain cases
Original_Description:
(1) If after a Bill has been passed by one House and transmitted to the other House —

 

(a) the Bill is rejected by the other House; or

 

(b) the Houses have finally disagreed as to the amendments to be made in the Bill; or

 

(c) more than six months elapse from the date of the reception of the Bill by the other House without the Bill being passed by it,

 

the President may, unless the Bill has elapsed by reason of a dissolution of the House of the People, notify to the Houses by message if they are sitting or by public notification if they are not sitting, his intention to summon them to meet in a joint sitting for the purpose of deliberating and voting on the Bill:

 

Provided that nothing in this clause shall apply to a Money Bill.

 

(2) In reckoning any such period of six months as is referred to in clause (1), no account shall be taken of any period during which the House referred to in sub-clause (c) of that clause is prorogued or adjourned for more than four consecutive days.

 

(3) Where the President has under clause (1) notified his intention of summoning the Houses to meet in a joint sitting, neither House shall proceed further with the Bill, but the President may at any time after the date of his notification summon the Houses to meet in a joint sitting for the purpose specified in the notification and, if he does so, the Houses shall meet accordingly.

 

(4) If at the joint sitting of the two Houses the Bill, with such amendments, if any, as are agreed to in joint sitting, is passed by a majority of the total number of members of both Houses present and voting, it shall be deemed for the purposes of this Constitution to have been passed by both Houses:

 

Provided that at a joint sitting —

 

(a) if the Bill, having been passed by one House, has not been passed by the other House with amendments and returned to the House in which it originated, no amendment shall be proposed to the Bill other than such amendments (if any) as are made necessary by the delay in the passage of the Bill;

 

(b) if the Bill has been so passed and returned, only such amendments as aforesaid shall be proposed to the Bill and such other amendments as are relevant to the matters with respect to which the Houses have not agreed;

 

and the decision of the person presiding as to the amendments which are admissible under this clause shall be final.

 

(5) A joint sitting may be held under this article and a Bill passed thereat, notwithstanding that a dissolution of the House of the People has intervened since the President notified his intention to summon the Houses to meet therein.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament in certain cases.

Also, if one House passes a Bill and the other House rejects it, disagrees on amendments, or does not pass it for more than six months, the President may call a joint sitting of both Houses.

However, this rule does not apply to Money Bills.

In a joint sitting, members of both Houses meet together and vote on the Bill. If the Bill is passed by a majority of members present and voting, it is treated as passed by both Houses.

Certain rules apply about what amendments can be proposed during the joint sitting, and the presiding officer’s decision on this is final.

Even if the House of the People is dissolved after the President has announced a joint sitting, the joint sitting can still take place.

Ex : If the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha cannot agree on a Bill, the President can call a joint meeting to decide it.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Provides a mechanism for resolving a deadlock between the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha on an ordinary bill.
The President can summon a joint sitting of both Houses if a bill is rejected by one House, if they disagree on amendments, or if more than six months pass.
The Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over the joint sitting.
Decisions are taken by a simple majority of the total number of members of both Houses present and voting.
This provision does not apply to Money Bills or Constitutional Amendment Bills.
Historical_Context: The joint sitting mechanism tends to favor the will of the Lok Sabha, as it has a much larger membership than the Rajya Sabha. This is a tool to overcome legislative obstruction by the upper house. It has been used only three times in India's history: for the Dowry Prohibition Act (1961), the Banking Service Commission (Repeal) Act (1978), and the Prevention of Terrorism Act (2002).
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article for resolving legislative deadlocks.
Related_Articles: Article 107 (Provisions as to introduction and passing of Bills)

Title: Special procedure in respect of Money Bills
Original_Description:
(1) A Money Bill shall not be introduced in the Council of States.

 

(2) After a Money Bill has been passed by the House of the People it shall be transmitted to the Council of States for its recommendations and the Council of States shall within a period of fourteen days from the date of its receipt of the Bill return the Bill to the House of the People with its recommendations and the House of the People may thereupon either accept or reject all or any of the recommendations of the Council of States.

 

(3) If the House of the People accepts any of the recommendations of the Council of States, the Money Bill shall be deemed to have been passed by both Houses with the amendments recommended by the Council of States and accepted by the House of the People.

 

(4) If the House of the People does not accept any of the recommendations of the Council of States, the Money Bill shall be deemed to have been passed by both Houses in the form in which it was passed by the House of the People without any of the amendments recommended by the Council of States.

 

(5) If a Money Bill passed by the House of the People and transmitted to the Council of States for its recommendations is not returned to the House of the People within the said period of fourteen days, it shall be deemed to have been passed by both Houses at the expiration of the said period in the form in which it was passed by the House of the People.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the special procedure for Money Bills.

Also, a Money Bill can only be introduced in the House of the People.

After it is passed by the House of the People, it is sent to the Council of States for its recommendations. The Council of States must return it within fourteen days.

The House of the People may accept or reject the recommendations of the Council of States.

If the Council of States does not return the Bill within fourteen days, it is treated as passed by both Houses in the form passed by the House of the People.

Ex : If the Rajya Sabha suggests changes to a Money Bill, the Lok Sabha can accept or reject those suggestions.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: A Money Bill can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha.
After being passed by the Lok Sabha, it is sent to the Rajya Sabha.
The Rajya Sabha has limited powers; it cannot reject or amend a Money Bill.
It must return the bill within 14 days, with or without recommendations.
The Lok Sabha can either accept or reject the Rajya Sabha's recommendations.
If the Rajya Sabha does not return the bill in 14 days, it is deemed to have been passed by both Houses.
Historical_Context: This special procedure ensures the financial supremacy of the Lok Sabha, which is the directly elected house and represents the will of the people. It prevents the indirectly elected Rajya Sabha from blocking the government's financial agenda.
Landmark_Cases: K.S. Puttaswamy vs Union of India (2018) (Aadhaar case): The case involved a major debate on whether the Aadhaar Bill was correctly classified as a Money Bill to bypass the Rajya Sabha.
Related_Articles: Article 117 (Special provisions as to financial Bills)

Title: Definition of “Money Bills”
Simplified_Description:
This article explains what a Money Bill is.

Also, a Bill is called a Money Bill if it deals only with matters like taxes, government borrowing, custody and withdrawal of money from the Consolidated Fund of India, or related financial matters.

A Bill is not a Money Bill only because it includes fines, fees, or taxes imposed by local authorities.

If there is any question about whether a Bill is a Money Bill, the decision of the Speaker of the House of the People is final.

Every Money Bill must carry the certificate of the Speaker stating that it is a Money Bill.

Ex : A Bill that deals only with imposition of a new central tax is a Money Bill.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Defines a Money Bill as a bill containing 'only' provisions related to taxation, government borrowing, appropriation of funds, and other specified financial matters.
Declares that the decision of the Speaker of the Lok Sabha on whether a bill is a Money Bill is final.
Requires the Speaker to certify a bill as a Money Bill before it is sent to the Rajya Sabha or presented to the President.
Historical_Context: The restrictive definition ('only provisions') is meant to prevent the government from including non-financial matters in a Money Bill to bypass the Rajya Sabha's scrutiny. However, the finality of the Speaker's decision has been a contentious issue, leading to accusations of misuse of the provision.
Landmark_Cases: K.S. Puttaswamy vs Union of India (2018) (Aadhaar case): The Supreme Court examined the scope of 'finality' of the Speaker's decision and held that it is subject to judicial review, though it upheld the certification of the Aadhaar Bill in that specific instance.
Related_Articles: Article 109 (Special procedure in respect of Money Bills)

Title: Assent to Bills
Original_Description:
When a Bill has been passed by the Houses of Parliament, it shall be presented to the President, and the President shall declare either that he assents to the Bill, or that he withholds assent therefrom:

 

      Provided that the President may, as soon as possible after the presentation to him of a Bill for assent, return the Bill if it is not a Money Bill to the Houses with a message requesting that they will reconsider the Bill or any specified provisions thereof and, in particular, will consider the desirability of introducing any such amendments as he may recommend in his message, and when a Bill is so returned, the Houses shall reconsider the Bill accordingly, and if the Bill is passed again by the Houses with or without amendment and presented to the President for assent, the President shall not withhold assent therefrom.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the President’s role in giving assent to Bills.

Also, when a Bill is passed by both Houses, it is presented to the President.

The President may give assent or withhold assent.

However, if the Bill is not a Money Bill, the President may return it to Parliament for reconsideration.

If Parliament passes the Bill again, with or without changes, the President must give assent.

Ex : If the President sends a Bill back and Parliament passes it again, the President must approve it.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Describes the President's role after a bill is passed by both Houses of Parliament.
The President has three options for an ordinary bill: give assent, withhold assent (absolute veto), or return the bill for reconsideration (suspensive veto).
If the Parliament passes the bill again (with or without amendments) after reconsideration, the President must give their assent.
In the case of a Money Bill, the President can only give or withhold assent; they cannot return it for reconsideration.
The Constitution does not prescribe a time limit for the President to act, giving rise to the 'pocket veto'.
Historical_Context: The President's veto power is a check on the legislature. However, in India's parliamentary system, it is limited. The absolute veto is generally exercised only on the advice of the cabinet (e.g., for a private member's bill). The suspensive veto is a more direct presidential check, but it can be overridden. The pocket veto (indefinitely delaying a decision) was famously used by President Zail Singh in 1986 on the Indian Post Office (Amendment) Bill.
Landmark_Cases: The exercise of veto power is a political process, guided by constitutional conventions.
Related_Articles: Article 74 (Council of Ministers to aid and advise President)
Article 200 (Assent to Bills by the Governor)

Title: Annual financial statement
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the annual financial statement of the Government of India.

Also, every financial year the President causes a statement to be laid before both Houses showing the estimated receipts and expenditure of the government.

The statement must clearly show expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India and other expenditure.

Certain expenditures, such as the salaries of the President, judges of the Supreme Court, and the Comptroller and Auditor General, are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India.

These charged expenditures are not subject to vote, though they can be discussed.

Ex : The Union Budget presented every year is the annual financial statement.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Mandates the presentation of an 'Annual Financial Statement', popularly known as the Union Budget, before Parliament for every financial year.
The President is responsible for causing it to be laid before both Houses.
The statement must show the estimated receipts and expenditures of the government.
It must distinguish between expenditure 'charged' on the Consolidated Fund of India and expenditure 'made' from it.
Expenditure 'charged' (e.g., salaries of President, Supreme Court judges) is not subject to a vote, though it can be discussed.
Historical_Context: This article establishes the constitutional requirement for the annual budget, which is a fundamental aspect of parliamentary control over public finances. It ensures that the government cannot raise or spend money without the approval of the people's representatives.
Landmark_Cases: This is a core financial and procedural article of the Constitution.
Related_Articles: Article 113 (Procedure in Parliament with respect to estimates)

Title: Procedure in Parliament with respect to estimates
Original_Description:
(1) So much of the estimates as relates to expenditure charged upon the Consolidated Fund of India shall not be submitted to the vote of Parliament, but nothing in this clause shall be construed as preventing the discussion in either House of Parliament of any of those estimates.

 

(2) So much of the said estimates as relates to other expenditure shall be submitted in the form of demands for grants to the House of the People, and the House of the People shall have power to assent, or to refuse to assent, to any demand, or to assent to any demand subject to a reduction of the amount specified therein.

 

(3) No demand for a grant shall be made except on the recommendation of the President.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how Parliament deals with expenditure estimates.

Also, expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India is not put to vote, but it can be discussed.

Other expenditure is presented to the House of the People as Demands for Grants.

The House of the People may accept, reject, or reduce these demands.

No demand for a grant can be made without the recommendation of the President.

Ex : The Lok Sabha can reduce the amount requested by a ministry in a Demand for Grant.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India can be discussed but not voted upon by Parliament.
All other expenditure is presented to the Lok Sabha in the form of 'Demands for Grants'.
The Lok Sabha has the power to assent, refuse, or reduce a Demand for Grant.
No demand for a grant can be made except on the recommendation of the President.
Historical_Context: This article operationalizes parliamentary control over government spending. By giving the Lok Sabha the power to vote on Demands for Grants, it makes the government financially accountable to the directly elected house. The process of voting on these demands is a key part of the budget session.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article central to the budget process.

Title: Appropriation Bills
Original_Description:
(1) As soon as may be after the grants under article 113 have been made by the House of the People, there shall be introduced a Bill to provide for the appropriation out of the Consolidated Fund of India of all moneys required to meet —

 

(a) the grants so made by the House of the People; and

 

(b) the expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India but not exceeding in any case the amount shown in the statement previously laid before Parliament.

 

(2) No amendment shall be proposed to any such Bill in either House of Parliament which will have the effect of varying the amount or altering the destination of any grant so made or of varying the amount of any expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India, and the decision of the person presiding as to whether an amendment is inadmissible under this clause shall be final.

 

(3) Subject to the provisions of articles 115 and 116, no money shall be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund of India except under appropriation made by law passed in accordance with the provisions of this article.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the Appropriation Bill.

Also, after the House of the People passes the Demands for Grants, a Bill is introduced to allow withdrawal of money from the Consolidated Fund of India.

This Bill covers both voted grants and charged expenditure.

No amendment can change the amount or purpose of the grants in this Bill.

No money can be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund of India except under a law passed for this purpose.

Ex : After the budget is approved, an Appropriation Act allows the government to spend the approved money.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Mandates that after the Demands for Grants are passed by the Lok Sabha, an 'Appropriation Bill' must be introduced.
This bill authorizes the government to withdraw money from the Consolidated Fund of India.
It includes both the voted grants and the expenditure charged on the Fund.
Crucially, it states that no money can be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund except under an appropriation made by law.
No amendment can be proposed to this bill that would alter the amount or destination of any grant.
Historical_Context: This article embodies the principle of 'no taxation without representation' and extends it to 'no expenditure without authorization'. The Appropriation Act is the legal authority for the government to spend money. Its passage is essential for the government to function.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article central to the budget process.
Related_Articles: Article 113 (Procedure in Parliament with respect to estimates)

Title: Supplementary, additional or excess grants
Original_Description:
(1) The President shall —

 

(a) if the amount authorised by any law made in accordance with the provisions of article 114 to be expended for a particular service for the current financial year is found to be insufficient for the purposes of that year or when a need has arisen during the current financial year for supplementary or additional expenditure upon some new service not contemplated in the annual financial statement for that year, or

 

(b) if any money has been spent on any service during a financial year in excess of the amount granted for that service and for that year,

 

cause to be laid before both the Houses of Parliament another statement showing the estimated amount of that expenditure or cause to be presented to the House of the People a demand for such excess, as the case may be.

 

(2) The provisions of articles 112, 113 and 114 shall have effect in relation to any such statement and expenditure or demand and also to any law to be made authorising the appropriation of moneys out of the Consolidated Fund of India to meet such expenditure or the grant in respect of such demand as they have effect in relation to the annual financial statement and the expenditure mentioned therein or to a demand for a grant and the law to be made for the authorisation of appropriation of moneys out of the Consolidated Fund of India to meet such expenditure or grant.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains supplementary, additional, and excess grants.

Also, if the money approved for a service is not enough during a financial year, or if new expenditure is needed, the President causes another statement to be laid before Parliament.

If more money was spent than approved, a demand for excess grant is presented.

The same procedure as the annual budget applies to these grants.

Ex : If extra funds are needed during the year for disaster relief, a supplementary grant can be requested.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Provides a mechanism for the government to get parliamentary approval for extra expenditure during a financial year.
Supplementary Grant: When funds for a particular service are found to be insufficient.
Additional Grant: When there is a need for expenditure on a new service not contemplated in the budget.
Excess Grant: When money has been spent in excess of the amount granted. This is regularized after the expenditure, post-scrutiny by the Public Accounts Committee.
The same procedure of Demands for Grants and Appropriation Bill applies to these grants.
Historical_Context: This article provides the necessary flexibility for government finances, allowing it to deal with unforeseen expenditures that were not part of the annual budget, while still maintaining the principle of parliamentary accountability.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article related to public finance.

Title: Votes on account, votes of credit and exceptional grants
Simplified_Description:
This article explains special types of grants.

Also, the House of the People can make a Vote on Account to allow the government to spend money for a short period before the full budget is passed.

It can also make a Vote of Credit to meet an unexpected demand where full details cannot be given.

An exceptional grant may be made for a special purpose not part of the usual services of the year.

Parliament must pass a law to authorize withdrawal of money for these purposes.

Ex : Before the full budget is passed, Parliament may pass a Vote on Account to allow the government to function.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Provides for three types of special grants by the Lok Sabha:
1. Vote on Account: An advance grant made to enable the government to meet its expenses for a part of the new financial year, pending the full passage of the budget.
2. Vote of Credit: A blank cheque granted to the executive to meet an unexpected demand where the details cannot be stated.
3. Exceptional Grant: Granted for a special purpose which does not form part of the ordinary expenditure of a financial year.
Historical_Context: The 'Vote on Account' is a routine practice, especially in an election year when a full budget cannot be presented. 'Votes of Credit' and 'Exceptional Grants' are extraordinary financial powers and are very rarely used.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article related to public finance.
Related_Articles: Article 113 (Procedure in Parliament with respect to estimates)

Title: Special provisions as to financial Bills
Original_Description:
(1) A Bill or amendment making provision for any of the matters specified in sub-clauses (a) to (f) of clause (1) of article 110 shall not be introduced or moved except on the recommendation of the President and a Bill making such provision shall not be introduced in the Council of States:

 

Provided that no recommendation shall be required under this clause for the moving of an amendment making provision for the reduction or abolition of any tax.

 

(2) A Bill or amendment shall not be deemed to make provision for any of the matters aforesaid by reason only that it provides for the imposition of fines or other pecuniary penalties, or for the demand or payment of fees for licences or fees for services rendered, or by reason that it provides for the imposition, abolition, remission, alteration or regulation of any tax by any local authority or body for local purposes.

 

(3) A Bill which, if enacted and brought into operation, would involve expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India shall not be passed by either House of Parliament unless the President has recommended to that House the consideration of the Bill.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains special provisions for financial Bills.

Also, certain Bills dealing with financial matters cannot be introduced without the recommendation of the President.

Some financial Bills can only be introduced in the House of the People.

A Bill involving expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India cannot be passed unless the President has recommended its consideration.

However, an amendment to reduce or abolish a tax does not require such recommendation.

Ex : A Bill proposing new central expenditure must have the President’s recommendation before being passed.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Deals with 'Financial Bills', which are different from Money Bills.

Financial Bills (Category I): Contain provisions of Article 110 but also other general matters. They can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha on the President's recommendation. Otherwise, they follow the procedure of an ordinary bill.

Financial Bills (Category II): Contain provisions involving expenditure from the Consolidated Fund. They can be introduced in either House but require the President's recommendation for consideration.
A Money Bill is a specific type of Financial Bill.
Historical_Context: This article creates categories of financial legislation that are not as restrictive as Money Bills but still require executive pre-approval (President's recommendation) due to their financial implications. This ensures executive control over public finances.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article defining types of financial legislation.
Related_Articles: Article 109 (Special procedure in respect of Money Bills)

Title: Rules of procedure
Original_Description:
(1) Each House of Parliament may make rules for regulating, subject to the provisions of this Constitution, its procedure and the conduct of its business.

 

(2) Until rules are made under clause (1), the rules of procedure and standing orders in force immediately before the commencement of this Constitution with respect to the Legislature of the Dominion of India shall have effect in relation to Parliament subject to such modifications and adaptations as may be made therein by the Chairman of the Council of States or the Speaker of the House of the People, as the case may be.

 

(3) The President, after consultation with the Chairman of the Council of States and the Speaker of the House of the People, may make rules as to the procedure with respect to joint sittings of, and communications between, the two Houses.

 

(4) At a joint sitting of the two Houses the Speaker of the House of the People, or in his absence such person as may be determined by rules of procedure made under clause (3), shall preside.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that each House of Parliament may make its own rules of procedure.

Also, until new rules are made, earlier rules continue with necessary changes.

The President may make rules for joint sittings and communication between the two Houses after consulting the Speaker and the Chairman.

At a joint sitting, the Speaker of the House of the People presides.

Ex : The Lok Sabha makes its own rules for debates and voting.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Empowers each House of Parliament to make its own rules of procedure and conduct of business.
The President, after consulting the Speaker and Chairman, makes rules for joint sittings.
The Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting.
Historical_Context: This article gives autonomy to the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha to manage their internal affairs. The 'Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business' for each House are detailed documents that govern every aspect of parliamentary functioning, from asking questions to debating bills.
Landmark_Cases: Courts generally do not interfere with the internal proceedings of the House, which are governed by these rules, unless there is a gross violation of the Constitution.
Related_Articles: Article 122 (Courts not to inquire into proceedings of Parliament)

Title: Regulation by law of procedure in Parliament in relation to financial business
Original_Description: Parliament may, for the purpose of the timely completion of financial business, regulate by law the procedure of, and the conduct of business in, each House of Parliament in relation to any financial matter or to any Bill for the appropriation of moneys out of the Consolidated Fund of India, and, if and so far as any provision of any law so made is inconsistent with any rule made by a House of Parliament under clause (1) of article 118 or with any rule or standing order having effect in relation to Parliament under clause (2) of that article, such provision shall prevail.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament may make a law to regulate its procedure for financial business.

Also, this is done to ensure that financial matters are completed in time.

If such a law is made and it conflicts with the rules of a House, the law will prevail.

This means Parliament can pass a law to speed up financial procedures.

Ex : Parliament can pass a law to ensure the budget is completed within a fixed time.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Allows Parliament to make a law to regulate its own procedure for financial business.
This is done to ensure the 'timely completion' of financial matters, like the passing of the budget.
If a law is made under this article, its provisions will override any rules made by the House under Article 118.
Historical_Context: This article provides an additional tool to streamline the budget process. It recognizes that financial business is time-sensitive and gives Parliament the power to create a binding legal framework for its own procedures to avoid delays.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article.

Title: Language to be used in Parliament
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in Part XVII, but subject to the provisions of article 348, business in Parliament shall be transacted in Hindi or in English:

 

       Provided that the Chairman of the Council of States or Speaker of the House of the People, or person acting as such, as the case may be, may permit any member who cannot adequately express himself in Hindi or in English to address the House in his mother-tongue.

 

(2) Unless Parliament by law otherwise provides, this article shall, after the expiration of a period of fifteen years from the commencement of this Constitution, have effect as if the words "or in English" were omitted therefrom.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the language to be used in Parliament.

Also, business in Parliament is conducted in Hindi or English.

However, the presiding officer may allow a member to speak in his mother tongue if he cannot express himself properly in Hindi or English.

Unless Parliament provides otherwise by law, English was originally meant to stop after fifteen years, but Parliament allowed its continued use.

Ex : A Member of Parliament may speak in Hindi, English, or with permission in his mother tongue.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Specifies that the business in Parliament shall be transacted in Hindi or English.
The presiding officer of a House can permit a member to speak in their mother tongue if they cannot express themselves in Hindi or English.
The Constitution originally provided for the discontinuation of English after 15 years, but this was overridden by the Official Languages Act, 1963.
Historical_Context: The language issue was one of the most contentious in the Constituent Assembly. This article represented a compromise. While Hindi was designated the official language, the continuation of English was permitted. The Official Languages Act, 1963, allowed for the continued use of English alongside Hindi indefinitely, addressing the concerns of non-Hindi speaking states.
Landmark_Cases: This article governs the internal functioning of Parliament.

Title: Restriction on discussion in Parliament
Original_Description: No discussion shall take place in Parliament with respect to the conduct of any Judge of the Supreme Court or of a High Court in the discharge of his duties except upon a motion for presenting an address to the President praying for the removal of the Judge as hereinafter provided.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament cannot discuss the conduct of a Judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court while the judge is performing official duties.

Also, no discussion about a judge’s behavior in court is allowed during normal proceedings in Parliament.

However, this restriction does not apply when a motion is brought for removing a judge from office.

This means that the conduct of a judge can only be discussed in Parliament when a formal process for removal is started according to the Constitution.

Ex : Members of Parliament cannot debate how a judge decided a case, unless they are discussing a motion to remove that judge.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Prohibits any discussion in Parliament about the conduct of a Supreme Court or High Court judge in the discharge of their duties.
The only exception is when Parliament is considering a motion for the impeachment (removal) of a judge.
This restriction applies to both Houses of Parliament.
Historical_Context: This article is a crucial provision for protecting the independence of the judiciary. It prevents the legislature from using its floor to exert political pressure on judges or to criticize their judicial conduct, thereby insulating them from political attacks and allowing them to perform their duties without fear or favor.
Landmark_Cases: In the matter of Keshav Singh (1965): The court emphasized the importance of mutual respect between the judiciary and the legislature, and the role of provisions like Article 121 in maintaining that balance.
Related_Articles: Article 124(4) (Procedure for removal of a Judge)

Title: Courts not to inquire into proceedings of Parliament
Original_Description:
(1) The validity of any proceedings in Parliament shall not be called in question on the ground of any alleged irregularity of procedure.

 

(2) No officer or member of Parliament in whom powers are vested by or under this Constitution for regulating procedure or the conduct of business, or for maintaining order, in Parliament shall be subject to the jurisdiction of any court in respect of the exercise by him of those powers.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that courts cannot question the validity of parliamentary proceedings on the ground of procedural irregularity.

Also, if Parliament makes a small mistake in procedure while conducting its business, courts cannot declare its proceedings invalid only for that reason.

In addition, any officer or member of Parliament who has powers to regulate procedure or maintain order in Parliament cannot be questioned in court for exercising those powers.

This means that Parliament controls its own internal working without interference from courts in matters of procedure.

Ex : If there is a minor procedural mistake during a debate in Parliament, a court cannot cancel the decision only because of that mistake.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Bars courts from inquiring into the validity of parliamentary proceedings on the grounds of procedural irregularity.
Provides immunity to the officers and members of Parliament responsible for conducting business and regulating procedure from the jurisdiction of courts.
This upholds the principle of the separation of powers and the autonomy of the legislature.
Historical_Context: This article protects the internal autonomy of Parliament. It ensures that the judiciary cannot interfere with the day-to-day proceedings of the legislature. However, this immunity is not absolute. The courts can intervene if the procedure is unconstitutional or violates a mandatory provision of the Constitution, as opposed to a mere 'irregularity'.
Landmark_Cases: Raja Ram Pal vs Hon'ble Speaker, Lok Sabha (2007) (Cash for Query case): The Supreme Court held that while courts cannot review procedural irregularities, they can review proceedings for illegality or unconstitutionality. The court upheld the expulsion of the MPs in this case.

Title: Power of President to promulgate Ordinances during recess of Parliament
Original_Description:
(1) If at any time, except when both Houses of Parliament are in session, the President is satisfied that circumstances exist which render it necessary for him to take immediate action, he may promulgate such Ordinances as the circumstances appear to him to require.

 

(2) An Ordinance promulgated under this article shall have the same force and effect as an Act of Parliament, but every such Ordinance —

 

(a) shall be laid before both Houses of Parliament and shall cease to operate at the expiration of six weeks from the reassembly of Parliament, or, if before the expiration of that period resolutions disapproving it are passed by both Houses, upon the passing of the second of those resolutions; and

 

(b) may be withdrawn at any time by the President.

 

      Explanation.—Where the Houses of Parliament are summoned to reassemble on different dates, the period of six weeks shall be reckoned from the later of those dates for the purposes of this clause.

 

(3) If and so far as an Ordinance under this article makes any provision which Parliament would not under this Constitution be competent to enact, it shall be void.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can issue an Ordinance when Parliament is not in session.

Also, this power can be used only when both Houses are not meeting and when immediate action is necessary.

An Ordinance has the same force as a law made by Parliament.

However, it must be placed before both Houses when they reassemble. It will stop operating six weeks after Parliament meets again, unless it is approved earlier.

The President can also withdraw an Ordinance at any time.

If an Ordinance contains something that Parliament has no power to make as law, that part will be invalid.

Ex : If an urgent law is needed when Parliament is not sitting, the President can issue an Ordinance to deal with the situation.
Subject: The Union
Key_Points: Gives the President the legislative power to issue Ordinances when both Houses of Parliament are not in session.
This power can only be used if the President is satisfied that immediate action is necessary.
An Ordinance has the same force as an Act of Parliament but is temporary in nature.
It must be laid before Parliament when it reassembles and ceases to operate six weeks after reassembly, unless it is approved or disapproved earlier.
The President can withdraw an ordinance at any time.
Historical_Context: This power was provided as a mechanism to deal with unforeseen and urgent matters when Parliament is not available to legislate. However, it has been frequently criticized for its overuse by the executive to bypass parliamentary debate and scrutiny. The re-promulgation of ordinances without seeking parliamentary approval has been particularly condemned by the Supreme Court.
Landmark_Cases: R.C. Cooper vs Union of India (1970): The Supreme Court held that the President's 'satisfaction' is not immune from judicial review.
D.C. Wadhwa vs State of Bihar (1987): The court strongly condemned the practice of re-promulgating ordinances, calling it a 'fraud on the Constitution' and an abuse of power.
Related_Articles: Article 213 (Power of Governor to promulgate Ordinances)

Title: Establishment and constitution of Supreme Court
Original_Description:
(1) There shall be a Supreme Court of India consisting of a Chief Justice of India and, until Parliament by law prescribes a larger number, of not more than seven other Judges.

 

(2) Every Judge of the Supreme Court shall be appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal after consultation with such of the Judges of the Supreme Court and of the High Courts in the States as the President may deem necessary for the purpose and shall hold office until he attains the age of sixty-five years:

 

     Provided that in the case of appointment of a Judge other than the Chief Justice, the Chief Justice of India shall always be consulted:

 

Provided further that — 

(a) a Judge may, by writing under his hand addressed to the President, resign his office;

 

(b) a Judge may be removed from his office in the manner provided in clause (4).

 

(2A) The age of a Judge of the Supreme Court shall be determined by such authority and in such manner as Parliament may by law provide.

 

(3) A person shall not be qualified for appointment as a Judge of the Supreme Court unless he is a citizen of India and —

 

(a) has been for at least five years a Judge of a High Court or of two or more such Courts in succession; or

 

(b) has been for at least ten years an advocate of a High Court or of two or more such Courts in succession; or

 

(c) is, in the opinion of the President, a distinguished jurist.

 

Explanation I.—In this clause "High Court'' means a High Court which exercises, or which at any time before the commencement of this Constitution exercised, jurisdiction in any part of the territory of India.

 

Explanation II.—In computing for the purpose of this clause the period during which a person has been an advocate, any period during which a person has held judicial office not inferior to that of a district judge after he became an advocate shall be included.

 

(4) A Judge of the Supreme Court shall not be removed from his office except by an order of the President passed after an address by each House of Parliament supported by a majority of the total membership of that House and by a majority of not less than two - thirds of the members of that House present and voting has been presented to the President in the same session for such removal on the ground of proved misbehaviour or incapacity.

 

(5) Parliament may by law regulate the procedure for the presentation of an address and for the investigation and proof of the misbehaviour or incapacity of a Judge under clause (4).

 

(6) Every person appointed to be a Judge of the Supreme Court shall, before he enters upon his office, make and subscribe before the President, or some person appointed in that behalf by him, an oath or affirmation according to the form set out for the purpose in the Third Schedule.
