Related_Articles: Article 15 (Prohibition of discrimination and special provisions)

Title: Duration of Panchayats, etc
Original_Description:
(1) Every Panchayat, unless sooner dissolved under any law for the time being in force, shall continue for five years from the date appointed for its first meeting and no longer.

 

(2) No amendment of any law for the time being in force shall have the effect of causing dissolution of a Panchayat at any level, which is functioning immediately before such amendment, till the expiration of its duration specified in clause (1).

 

(3) An election to constitute a Panchayat shall be completed —

 

(a) before the expiry of its duration specified in clause (1);

 

(b) before the expiration of a period of six months from the date of its dissolution:

 

   Provided that where the remainder of the period for which the dissolved Panchayat would have continued is less than six months, it shall not be necessary to hold any election under this clause for constituting the Panchayat for such period.

 

(4) A Panchayat constituted upon the dissolution of a Panchayat before the expiration of its duration shall continue only for the remainder of the period for which the dissolved Panchayat would have continued under clause (1) had it not been so dissolved.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every Panchayat will continue for five years from the date of its first meeting unless it is dissolved earlier according to law.

Also, if any law is changed, it cannot end the term of a Panchayat that is already working until its five-year period is over.

In addition, elections for a new Panchayat must be completed before the five-year term ends. If a Panchayat is dissolved earlier, elections must be held within six months. However, if the remaining period is less than six months, it is not necessary to hold elections for that short time.

If a new Panchayat is formed after early dissolution, it will serve only for the remaining time of the original term.

Ex : If a Panchayat is dissolved after three years, the new Panchayat will serve only for the remaining two years.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Provides for a fixed five-year term for every Panchayat.
A Panchayat can be dissolved earlier, but it must be given a reasonable opportunity of being heard.
If a Panchayat is dissolved, a fresh election must be held within six months.
A Panchayat reconstituted after dissolution only serves for the remainder of the original term.
Historical_Context: Before the 73rd Amendment, state governments could dissolve panchayats arbitrarily and postpone elections indefinitely. This article provided security of tenure and mandated timely elections, making panchayats stable and durable institutions of self-government.
Landmark_Cases: The Supreme Court has emphasized the importance of timely elections for local bodies as a constitutional mandate.

Title: Disqualifications for membership
Original_Description:
(1) A person shall be disqualified for being chosen as, and for being, a member of a Panchayat —

 

(a) if he is so disqualified by or under any law for the time being in force for the purposes of elections to the Legislature of the State concerned: Provided that no person shall be disqualified on the ground that he is less than twenty-five years of age, if he has attained the age of twenty-one years;

 

(b) if he is so disqualified by or under any law made by the Legislature of the State.

 

(2) If any question arises as to whether a member of a Panchayat has become subject to any of the disqualifications mentioned in clause (1), the question shall be referred for the decision of such authority and in such manner as the Legislature of a State may, by law, provide.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains when a person cannot become or continue as a member of a Panchayat.

A person will be disqualified if they are disqualified under the laws that apply to State Legislature elections. However, a person can contest Panchayat elections if they are at least twenty-one years old, even though State elections require twenty-five years.

Also, a State law may provide other reasons for disqualification.

If there is a question about whether a member has become disqualified, the matter will be decided by an authority chosen according to State law.

Ex : If a person is declared guilty of certain crimes under State law, they may not be allowed to contest Panchayat elections.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Specifies the grounds for disqualification for membership of a Panchayat.
A person is disqualified if they are disqualified under any law applicable to elections for the State Legislature.
It lowers the minimum age for contesting elections to 21 years (as against 25 for the State Assembly).
State legislatures can prescribe additional grounds for disqualification by law.
Historical_Context: This article links the disqualification criteria for panchayat members to those of state legislators, ensuring a basic standard of eligibility. The reduction of the minimum age to 21 was a deliberate step to encourage youth participation in local governance.
Landmark_Cases: State laws imposing disqualifications like a two-child norm or minimum educational qualifications have been challenged and judged by the courts under this article, for instance, in Javed vs State of Haryana (2003).
Related_Articles: Article 191 (Disqualifications for membership of State Legislature)

Title: Powers, authority and responsibilities of Panchayats
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that State Legislatures can give powers and responsibilities to Panchayats so that they can function as self-government institutions.

Also, State laws may allow Panchayats to prepare plans for economic development and social justice.

In addition, Panchayats may be given responsibility to implement development schemes, including subjects listed in the Eleventh Schedule.

This means that Panchayats can play an important role in local development if the State gives them proper authority.

Ex : A Panchayat may be given power to manage village roads and drinking water projects.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Empowers State Legislatures to endow Panchayats with powers and authority.
The objective is to enable them to function as 'institutions of self-government'.
This includes devolution of powers for preparing plans for economic development and social justice.
It also covers the implementation of schemes, including those related to the 29 matters listed in the Eleventh Schedule.
Historical_Context: This is a crucial article that determines the actual functioning and autonomy of panchayats. While the 73rd Amendment created the structure, it left the devolution of powers, functions, and funds to the discretion of the State Legislatures. The extent of devolution varies significantly from state to state, which is a key challenge in the functioning of Panchayati Raj.
Landmark_Cases: This article is at the heart of debates and advocacy for greater devolution of 'Functions, Funds, and Functionaries' (the 3 Fs) to local governments.
Related_Articles: Eleventh Schedule (Lists 29 functional items for Panchayats)

Title: Powers to impose taxes by, and Funds of, the Panchayats
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that State Legislatures can allow Panchayats to collect taxes, duties, tolls, and fees according to rules and limits set by law.

Also, the State Government can assign certain taxes collected by it to Panchayats.

In addition, the State may provide grants to Panchayats from its main fund. The law may also create special funds for Panchayats to manage their money.

This means Panchayats can receive money and manage funds to carry out their duties.

Ex : A Panchayat may collect a small fee for local market facilities if allowed by State law.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Empowers State Legislatures to authorize Panchayats to levy and collect taxes, duties, tolls, and fees.
Allows the State Government to assign its own collected taxes to Panchayats.
Provides for grants-in-aid to Panchayats from the Consolidated Fund of the State.
This article is the basis for the financial empowerment of Panchayats.
Historical_Context: Financial autonomy is critical for the functioning of self-governing institutions. This article enables State Legislatures to create a financial base for Panchayats, allowing them to raise their own resources and receive funds from the state government. The actual extent of financial devolution is determined by the State Finance Commission's recommendations and the state's own laws.
Landmark_Cases: The implementation of this article's provisions is a key area of focus for strengthening local governance.
Related_Articles: Article 243I (Constitution of Finance Commission to review financial position)

Title: Constitution of Finance Commission to review financial position
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Governor of a State must form a Finance Commission within one year of the start of this system and then every five years.

The Finance Commission will review the financial condition of Panchayats and give recommendations to the Governor.

Also, it will suggest how taxes should be shared between the State and Panchayats, what taxes may be given to Panchayats, and how grants should be provided. It may also suggest ways to improve the financial condition of Panchayats.

The State Legislature may decide the composition and powers of the Commission. The Governor must place its report before the State Legislature.

Ex : Every five years, a State Finance Commission reviews how much money Panchayats should receive.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Mandates the Governor to constitute a State Finance Commission (SFC) every five years.
The primary function of the SFC is to review the financial position of the Panchayats.
It makes recommendations on the principles for the distribution of taxes between the state and panchayats, the assignment of taxes to panchayats, and grants-in-aid.
The Governor must lay the SFC's report and an action-taken memorandum before the State Legislature.
Historical_Context: This article creates a constitutional mechanism, similar to the Union Finance Commission (Article 280), to ensure a predictable and rule-based transfer of funds from the state government to the panchayats. It is a critical institutional safeguard for the financial viability and autonomy of local bodies.
Landmark_Cases: The timely constitution of SFCs and the implementation of their recommendations have been subjects of judicial scrutiny and public advocacy.

Title: Audit of accounts of Panchayats
Original_Description: The Legislature of a State may, by law, make provisions with respect to the maintenance of accounts by the Panchayats and the auditing of such accounts.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the State Legislature can make laws about how Panchayats must maintain their accounts and how those accounts should be audited.

This means Panchayats must properly record their income and expenses and follow the rules set by the State.

Ex : A Panchayat must maintain records of money collected and spent, and these records can be checked by an auditor.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Empowers the State Legislature to make laws regarding the maintenance and auditing of Panchayat accounts.
This is essential for financial accountability and transparency in local governance.
Historical_Context: This provision ensures that there is a legal framework for financial oversight of Panchayats. Effective auditing helps in preventing corruption, ensuring proper utilization of funds, and building public trust in local institutions.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article related to financial accountability.
Related_Articles: Article 148-151 (Provisions related to the Comptroller and Auditor-General)

Title: Elections to the Panchayats
Original_Description:
(1) The superintendence, direction and control of the preparation of electoral rolls for, and the conduct of, all elections to the Panchayats shall be vested in a State Election Commission consisting of a State Election Commissioner to be appointed by the Governor.

 

(2) Subject to the provisions of any law made by the Legislature of a State, the conditions of service and tenure of office of the State Election Commissioner shall be such as the Governor may by rule determine:

 

   Provided that the State Election Commissioner shall not be removed from his office except in like manner and on the like grounds as a Judge of a High Court and the conditions of service of the State Election Commissioner shall not be varied to his disadvantage after his appointment.

 

(3) The Governor of a State shall, when so requested by the State Election Commission, make available to the State Election Commission such staff as may be necessary for the discharge of the functions conferred on the State Election Commission by clause (1).

 

(4) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, the Legislature of a State may, by law, make provision with respect to all matters relating to, or in connection with, elections to the Panchayats.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that elections to Panchayats will be conducted by a State Election Commission.

The State Election Commissioner will be appointed by the Governor. The Commissioner cannot be removed easily and has protection similar to a High Court Judge.

Also, the State Election Commission will prepare voter lists and conduct elections. The Governor must provide necessary staff when requested.

The State Legislature can make laws regarding Panchayat elections.

Ex : The State Election Commission organizes and supervises elections for village Panchayats.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Mandates the creation of an independent State Election Commission (SEC) in every state.
The SEC is responsible for the superintendence, direction, and control of all elections to the Panchayats.
The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Governor.
Provides for security of tenure: the Commissioner can only be removed in the same manner as a High Court judge.
Historical_Context: This article creates an independent constitutional body to conduct free and fair elections for local bodies, mirroring the role of the Election Commission of India (Article 324) for Parliament and state legislatures. This was a crucial step to ensure that panchayat elections are held regularly and impartially, free from the control of the state government.
Landmark_Cases: Kishan Singh Tomar vs Municipal Corporation of the City of Ahmedabad (2006): The Supreme Court held that the powers of the State Election Commission are akin to the Election Commission of India and that state governments are obligated to cooperate with it to ensure timely elections.

Title: Application to Union territories
Original_Description:
The provisions of this Part shall apply to the Union territories and shall, in their application to a Union territory, have effect as if the references to the Governor of a State were references to the Administrator of the Union territory appointed under article 239 and references to the Legislature or the Legislative Assembly of a State were references, in relation to a Union territory having a Legislative Assembly, to that Legislative Assembly:

 

   Provided that the President may, by public notification, direct that the provisions of this Part shall apply to any Union territory or part thereof subject to such exceptions and modifications as he may specify in the notification.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the rules about Panchayats also apply to Union Territories.

In Union Territories, references to the Governor will mean the Administrator appointed under the Constitution. If a Union Territory has a Legislative Assembly, references to State Legislature will mean that Assembly.

However, the President can decide to apply these provisions with certain changes or exceptions.

Ex : In a Union Territory with an Assembly, that Assembly can make laws about Panchayats.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Applies the provisions of Part IX (The Panchayats) to Union Territories.
References to the Governor are to be read as references to the Administrator of the UT.
Gives the President the power to direct that the provisions of this Part shall apply to a UT with certain exceptions and modifications.
Historical_Context: This article extends the Panchayati Raj system to Union Territories, promoting grassroots democracy in these centrally administered areas. It also provides the flexibility for the President to adapt the provisions to the specific administrative context of each UT.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article for applying Part IX to UTs.

Title: Part not to apply to certain areas
Original_Description:
(1) Nothing in this Part shall apply to the Scheduled Areas referred to in clause (1), and the tribal areas referred to in clause (2), of article 244.

 

(2) Nothing in this Part shall apply to —

 

(a) the States of Nagaland, Meghalaya and Mizoram; 

 

(b) the hill areas in the State of Manipur for which District Councils exist under any law for the time being in force.

 

(3) Nothing in this Part —

 

(a) relating to Panchayats at the district level shall apply to the hill areas of the District of Darjeeling in the State of West Bengal for which Darjeeling Gorkha Hill Council exists under any law for the time being in force;

 

(b) shall be construed to affect the functions and powers of the Darjeeling Gorkha Hill Council constituted under such law.

 

(3A) Nothing in article 243D, relating to reservation of seats for the Scheduled Castes, shall apply to the State of Arunachal Pradesh. 

 

(4) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, —

 

(a) the Legislature of a State referred to in subclause (a) of clause (2) may, by law, extend this part to that State, except the areas, if any, referred to in clause (1), if the Legislative Assembly of that State passes a resolution to that effect by a majority of the total membership of that House and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of that House present and voting;

 

(b) Parliament may, by law, extend the provisions of this Part to the Scheduled Areas and the tribal areas referred to in clause (1) subject to such exceptions and modifications as may be specified in such law, and no such law shall be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for the purposes of article 368.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Panchayat provisions do not apply automatically to certain Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas.

Also, these provisions do not apply to certain States and specific hill areas mentioned in the Constitution.

However, Parliament can extend these provisions to Scheduled or Tribal Areas with modifications. A State Legislature of certain States may also choose to apply these provisions if it passes a special resolution.

This means some areas with special systems of governance are treated differently.

Ex : In certain tribal areas, special local systems may continue instead of regular Panchayats.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Specifies areas where Part IX (The Panchayats) does not automatically apply.
This includes Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas under Article 244.
It also excludes the states of Nagaland, Meghalaya, and Mizoram, as well as certain hill areas of Manipur and Darjeeling.
Empowers Parliament to extend the provisions of this Part to Scheduled and Tribal areas with modifications.
Historical_Context: This article recognizes that the tribal areas and certain states in the North-East have their own traditional systems of local governance and self-government. A uniform application of the Panchayati Raj system was deemed inappropriate. This led to the enactment of the Panchayats (Extension to Scheduled Areas) Act, 1996 (PESA), which extended Part IX to Scheduled Areas with modifications to recognize the powers of the Gram Sabha and traditional customs.
Landmark_Cases: Cases related to the implementation of the PESA Act fall under the ambit of this article.
Related_Articles: Article 244 (Administration of Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas)

Title: Continuance of existing laws and Panchayats
Original_Description:
Notwithstanding anything in this Part, any provision of any law relating to Panchayats in force in a State immediately before the commencement of the Constitution (Seventy-third Amendment) Act, 1992, which is inconsistent with the provisions of this Part, shall continue to be in force until amended or repealed by a competent Legislature or other competent authority or until the expiration of one year from such commencement, whichever is earlier:

 

   Provided that all the Panchayats existing immediately before such commencement shall continue till the expiration of their duration, unless sooner dissolved by a resolution passed to that effect by the Legislative Assembly of that State or, in the case of a State having a Legislative Council, by each House of the Legislature of that State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that any State law about Panchayats that existed before this Part started will continue for up to one year, even if it does not fully match the new rules.

Also, existing Panchayats will continue until their term ends unless dissolved earlier by the State Legislature.

This means States were given time to update their laws to match the new system.

Ex : A State had one year to change its Panchayat law to follow the Constitution.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: This is a transitional provision.
It provided that all existing state laws on Panchayats that were inconsistent with the new Part IX would continue for a maximum of one year.
States were required to amend their laws to bring them in conformity with the 73rd Amendment within this one-year period.
Existing panchayats were allowed to continue for their full term unless dissolved sooner by the state legislature.
Historical_Context: This article ensured a smooth transition to the new constitutional framework for Panchayati Raj. It gave state governments a one-year window (from April 24, 1993, to April 23, 1994) to pass new Panchayati Raj Acts or amend existing ones to align them with the mandatory provisions of the 73rd Amendment.
Landmark_Cases: This was a transitional article with relevance for a limited period after the 73rd Amendment came into force.
Related_Articles: Article 372 (Continuance in force of existing laws)

Title: Bar to interference by courts in electoral matters
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that courts cannot question laws related to dividing areas for Panchayat elections or allotting seats.

Also, no Panchayat election can be challenged in court except through an election petition filed in the manner provided by State law.

This means elections cannot be stopped by court cases during the process.

Ex : If someone wants to challenge a Panchayat election result, they must file an election petition after the election.
Subject: The Panchayats
Key_Points: Places a bar on judicial interference in certain electoral matters related to Panchayats.
The validity of laws related to delimitation of constituencies and allotment of seats cannot be questioned in any court.
An election to a Panchayat can only be challenged through an election petition presented to an authority prescribed by state law.
This bars courts from interfering in the electoral process once it has started.
Historical_Context: This article mirrors the provisions of Article 329 for Parliament and State Legislature elections. It is intended to ensure that electoral processes are conducted smoothly and without interruption. The remedy for any grievance is not to stop the election but to file an election petition after the results are declared.
Landmark_Cases: Kishan Singh Tomar vs Municipal Corporation of the City of Ahmedabad (2006): While interpreting the parallel provision for municipalities, the SC affirmed this principle but also stated that the bar does not prevent High Courts from intervening under Article 226 in extraordinary cases where the election is not being conducted at all.
Related_Articles: Article 329 (Bar to interference by courts in electoral matters)

Title: Definitions
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the meaning of certain important words used in this Part about Municipalities.

It defines a Committee as a body formed under the rules of this Part. It defines a district as a district in a State. It defines a Metropolitan area as an area with a population of ten lakh or more, which may include more than one district and consists of two or more Municipalities or nearby areas declared by the Governor.

Also, it explains that a Municipal area means the area of a Municipality as declared by the Governor. A Municipality means a local self-government body for urban areas. A Panchayat means a rural local body formed under the Constitution. Population means the number of people counted in the latest published census.

This means these definitions help in understanding how urban local government works.

Ex : A large city with more than ten lakh people may be declared a Metropolitan area.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Provides definitions for terms used in Part IX-A (The Municipalities).
Defines 'Municipality' as an institution of self-government for urban areas.
Defines 'Metropolitan area' as an area with a population of 10 lakhs or more.
Also defines 'Committee', 'district', 'Municipal area', and 'Panchayat' for the purpose of this part.
Historical_Context: This article, and the entire Part IX-A, was inserted by the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992. These definitions are essential for understanding the structure and different types of Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) that the amendment established.
Landmark_Cases: These definitions are foundational to all litigation related to urban local governance.

Title: Constitution of Municipalities
Original_Description:
(1) There shall be constituted in every State, —

 

(a) a Nagar Panchayat (by whatever name called) for a transitional area, that is to say, an area in transition from a rural area to an urban area;

 

(b) a Municipal Council for a smaller urban area; and

 

(c) a Municipal Corporation for a larger urban area, in accordance with the provisions of this Part:

 

   Provided that a Municipality under this clause may not be constituted in such urban area or part thereof as the Governor may, having regard to the size of the area and the municipal services being provided or proposed to be provided by an industrial establishment in that area and such other factors as he may deem fit, by public notification, specify to be an industrial township.

 

(2) In this article, “a transitional area”, “a smaller urban area” or “a larger urban area” means such area as the Governor may, having regard to the population of the area, the density of the population therein, the revenue generated for local administration, the percentage of employment in non-agricultural activities, the economic importance or such other factors as he may deem fit, specify by public notification for the purposes of this Part
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every State must create different types of Municipalities for urban areas.

There must be a Nagar Panchayat for an area that is changing from rural to urban. There must be a Municipal Council for a smaller urban area. There must be a Municipal Corporation for a larger urban area.

However, the Governor may decide not to create a Municipality in an area declared as an industrial township if certain services are provided by an industrial establishment.

The Governor decides which area is transitional, smaller urban, or larger urban based on factors like population, revenue, and economic importance.

Ex : A small town may have a Municipal Council, while a big city will have a Municipal Corporation.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Mandates the constitution of a three-tier system of urban local bodies.
A Nagar Panchayat for a transitional area (an area in transition from rural to urban).
A Municipal Council for a smaller urban area.
A Municipal Corporation for a larger urban area.
The Governor specifies these areas based on factors like population, revenue, and economic importance.
Historical_Context: The 74th Amendment brought constitutional uniformity to the structure of urban local governance. Prior to this, the creation and structure of municipalities were entirely at the discretion of state governments. This article made the three-tier structure a constitutional requirement, strengthening urban self-government.
Landmark_Cases: This is a foundational structural article for urban local bodies.

Title: Composition of Municipalities
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how members of a Municipality are chosen.

All seats in a Municipality must be filled by direct election from different areas called wards. Each Municipal area must be divided into wards.

Also, State law may allow certain persons to be represented in the Municipality, such as persons with special knowledge, Members of Parliament, and Members of the State Legislature. However, persons with special knowledge may not have the right to vote in meetings.

The State Legislature may also decide how the Chairperson of a Municipality is elected.

Ex : People living in each ward vote to choose their ward representative in the Municipality.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Mandates that all seats in a Municipality shall be filled by direct election.
The municipal area is to be divided into territorial constituencies called 'wards'.
Allows State Legislatures to provide for the representation of persons with special knowledge, MPs, and MLAs in the Municipality (though they may not have the right to vote).
Historical_Context: This article ensures that urban local bodies are democratically elected representative bodies. It establishes the principle of direct election from wards as the basis of municipal governance, ensuring accountability to local citizens.
Landmark_Cases: This is a structural article defining the democratic composition of municipalities.

Title: Constitution and composition of Wards Committees, etc
Original_Description:
(1) There shall be constituted Wards Committees, consisting of one or more wards, within the territorial area of a Municipality having a population of three lakhs or more.

 

(2) The Legislature of a State may, by law, make provision with respect to —

 

(a) the composition and the territorial area of a Wards Committee;

 

(b) the manner in which the seats in a Wards Committee shall be filled.

 

(3) A member of a Municipality representing a ward within the territorial area of the Wards Committee shall be a member of that Committee.

 

(4) Where a Wards Committee consists of —

 

(a) one ward, the member representing that ward in the Municipality; or

 

(b) two or more wards, one of the members representing such wards in the Municipality elected by the members of the Wards Committee, shall be the Chairperson of that Committee.

 

(5) Nothing in this article shall be deemed to prevent the Legislature of a State from making any provision for the constitution of Committees in addition to the Wards Committees.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Wards Committees must be formed in every Municipality that has a population of three lakh or more.

A Wards Committee may consist of one or more wards. The State Legislature can decide the composition and area of these Committees and how members are chosen.

Also, a member of the Municipality representing a ward will be part of the Wards Committee for that area. If the Committee has more than one ward, one member will be chosen as Chairperson.

In addition, the State Legislature may create other Committees apart from Wards Committees.

Ex : In a large city, each group of wards may have a Committee to handle local issues like sanitation.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Mandates the constitution of Wards Committees in all municipalities with a population of three lakhs or more.
A Wards Committee consists of one or more wards.
The State Legislature is empowered to make laws regarding the composition, territorial area, and manner of filling seats in these committees.
Historical_Context: The concept of Wards Committees was introduced by the 74th Amendment to bring governance even closer to the people in larger cities. They are meant to be a sub-municipal structure to deal with local issues at the ward level, enhancing citizen participation and accountability.
Landmark_Cases: The effectiveness and functioning of Wards Committees vary widely across different cities and states.

Title: Reservation of seats
Original_Description:
(1) Seats shall be reserved for the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes in every Municipality and the number of seats so reserved shall bear, as nearly as may be, the same proportion to the total number of seats to be filled by direct election in that Municipality as the population of the Scheduled Castes in the Municipal area or of the Scheduled Tribes in the Municipal area bears to the total population of that area and such seats may be allotted by rotation to different constituencies in a Municipality.

 

(2) Not less than one-third of the total number of seats reserved under clause (1) shall be reserved for women belonging to the Scheduled Castes or, as the case may be, the Scheduled Tribes.

 

(3) Not less than one-third (including the number of seats reserved for women belonging to the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes) of the total number of seats to be filled by direct election in every Municipality shall be reserved for women and such seats may be allotted by rotation to different constituencies in a Municipality.

 

(4) The offices of Chairpersons in the Municipalities shall be reserved for the Scheduled Castes, the Scheduled Tribes and women in such manner as the Legislature of a State may, by law, provide.

 

(5) The reservation of seats under clauses (1) and (2) and the reservation of offices of Chairpersons (other than the reservation for women) under clause (4) shall cease to have effect on the expiration of the period specified in article 334.

 

(6) Nothing in this Part shall prevent the Legislature of a State from making any provision for reservation of seats in any Municipality or offices of Chairpersons in the Municipalities in favour of backward class of citizens.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that seats in every Municipality must be reserved for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes according to their population in that area.

Also, at least one-third of the seats reserved for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes must be reserved for women of those groups.

In addition, at least one-third of the total seats in every Municipality must be reserved for women. These seats may be rotated among different wards in different elections.

The positions of Chairpersons must also be reserved for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and women as decided by State law.

States may also provide reservation for backward classes.

Ex : Some wards in a city may be reserved only for women candidates.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Mandates reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs) in every Municipality, in proportion to their population.
Mandates that not less than one-third of the total seats must be reserved for women.
Provides for the reservation of the offices of Chairpersons for SCs, STs, and women.
Allows state legislatures to make reservation provisions for Backward Classes (OBCs).
Historical_Context: This article is the urban counterpart to Article 243D for panchayats. It was a landmark provision of the 74th Amendment that constitutionally guaranteed political representation for marginalized groups and women in urban local governance, aiming to make city councils more inclusive and representative.
Landmark_Cases: K. Krishna Murthy (Dr.) vs Union of India (2010): Upheld the constitutionality of reservations for OBCs in municipal elections, subject to the 'triple test' criteria.
Related_Articles: Article 15 (Prohibition of discrimination and special provisions)

Title: Duration of Municipalities, etc
Original_Description:
(1) Every Municipality, unless sooner dissolved under any law for the time being in force, shall continue for five years from the date appointed for its first meeting and no longer:

 

   Provided that a Municipality shall be given a reasonable opportunity of being heard before its dissolution.

 

(2) No amendment of any law for the time being in force shall have the effect of causing dissolution of a Municipality at any level, which is functioning immediately before such amendment, till the expiration of its duration specified in clause (1). 

 

(3) An election to constitute a Municipality shall be completed, —

 

(a) before the expiry of its duration specified in clause (1);

 

(b) before the expiration of a period of six months from the date of its dissolution:

 

   Provided that where the remainder of the period for which the dissolved Municipality would have continued is less than six months, it shall not be necessary to hold any election under this clause for constituting the Municipality for such period.

 

(4) A Municipality constituted upon the dissolution of a Municipality before the expiration of its duration shall continue only for the remainder of the period for which the dissolved Municipality would have continued under clause (1) had it not been so dissolved.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every Municipality will continue for five years from its first meeting unless it is dissolved earlier.

However, before dissolving a Municipality, it must be given a reasonable chance to explain its position.

Elections must be completed before the five-year term ends. If a Municipality is dissolved early, elections must be held within six months. If the remaining term is less than six months, elections are not necessary for that short period.

If a new Municipality is formed after early dissolution, it will serve only for the remaining part of the original term.

Ex : If a Municipal Council is dissolved after four years, new elections must be held within six months.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Provides for a fixed five-year term for every Municipality.
A Municipality must be given a reasonable opportunity to be heard before it is dissolved.
If a Municipality is dissolved, fresh elections must be held within six months.
A Municipality reconstituted after a mid-term dissolution will only serve for the remainder of the original five-year period.
Historical_Context: This article provided crucial security of tenure for urban local bodies. Before the 74th Amendment, state governments frequently superseded municipalities for long periods, undermining local democracy. This provision made regular, timely elections a constitutional compulsion.
Landmark_Cases: This article has been instrumental in ensuring the regularity of municipal elections across the country.

Title: Disqualifications for membership
Original_Description:
(1) A person shall be disqualified for being chosen as, and for being, a member of a Municipality —

 

(a) if he is so disqualified by or under any law for the time being in force for the purposes of elections to the Legislature of the State concerned:

 

   Provided that no person shall be disqualified on the ground that he is less than twenty-five years of age, if he has attained the age of twenty-one years;

 

(b) if he is so disqualified by or under any law made by the Legislature of the State.

 

(2) If any question arises as to whether a member of a Municipality has become subject to any of the disqualifications mentioned in clause (1), the question shall be referred for the decision of such authority and in such manner as the Legislature of a State may, by law, provide.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains when a person cannot become or remain a member of a Municipality.

A person will be disqualified if they are disqualified under laws related to State Legislature elections. However, a person can contest municipal elections if they are at least twenty-one years old.

Also, the State Legislature may make additional rules for disqualification.

If there is a question about disqualification, it will be decided by an authority as provided by State law.

Ex : A person declared bankrupt under law may not be allowed to contest municipal elections.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Specifies the disqualification criteria for membership of a Municipality.
It links the disqualifications to those applicable for elections to the State Legislature.
It sets the minimum age for contesting municipal elections at 21 years.
State legislatures can prescribe additional disqualifications by law.
Historical_Context: Similar to Article 243F for panchayats, this article sets the eligibility standards for candidates in urban local elections, aligning them with state-level norms while also lowering the age barrier to encourage youth participation.
Landmark_Cases: State laws passed under this article, prescribing conditions like a two-child norm or minimum educational qualifications, have been a subject of litigation.
Related_Articles: Article 191 (Disqualifications for membership of State Legislature)

Title: Powers, authority and responsibilities of Municipalities, etc
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that State Legislatures can give powers and responsibilities to Municipalities so they can function as self-government institutions.

Also, Municipalities may be given the responsibility to prepare plans for economic development and social justice.

In addition, they may be allowed to implement schemes related to matters listed in the Twelfth Schedule.

This means Municipalities can manage important local services if the State gives them proper authority.

Ex : A Municipal Corporation may be given power to manage city roads and waste management.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Empowers State Legislatures to give powers and authority to Municipalities.
The aim is to enable them to function as 'institutions of self-government'.
This includes powers related to preparing plans for economic development and social justice.
It also covers the implementation of schemes related to the 18 matters listed in the Twelfth Schedule.
Historical_Context: This article is the cornerstone for the functional autonomy of urban local bodies. However, like its rural counterpart (Article 243G), it leaves the actual devolution of powers to the discretion of state legislatures. The limited and often reluctant transfer of functions, funds, and functionaries from state governments remains a major challenge for effective urban governance.
Landmark_Cases: This article is central to the ongoing debate about empowering city governments in India.
Related_Articles: Twelfth Schedule (Lists 18 functional items for Municipalities)

Title: Power to impose taxes by, and Funds of, the Municipalities
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that State Legislatures can allow Municipalities to collect taxes, duties, tolls, and fees according to rules set by law.

Also, the State Government may assign certain taxes collected by it to Municipalities.

In addition, grants may be given to Municipalities from the State’s main fund. Laws may also create special funds for Municipalities to manage their money.

This means Municipalities can receive and manage funds for city services.

Ex : A Municipality may collect property tax if allowed by State law.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Empowers State Legislatures to authorize Municipalities to levy and collect taxes, duties, tolls, and fees (e.g., property tax).
Allows the State Government to assign its own collected taxes to Municipalities.
Provides for grants-in-aid to Municipalities from the Consolidated Fund of the State.
Historical_Context: Financial independence is critical for urban local bodies to deliver civic services. This article provides the constitutional basis for municipalities to raise their own revenue and receive funds from the state. The recommendations of the State Finance Commission are meant to guide this process of financial devolution.
Landmark_Cases: The scope of municipal taxation powers is a frequent subject of litigation.

Title: Finance Commission
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Finance Commission formed under Article 243I will also review the financial condition of Municipalities.

The Commission will recommend how taxes should be shared between the State and Municipalities, what taxes may be assigned to them, and how grants should be given.

It may also suggest ways to improve the financial condition of Municipalities.

The Governor must place the recommendations before the State Legislature.

Ex : The State Finance Commission may recommend giving a fixed share of certain taxes to city governments.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Mandates that the State Finance Commission (SFC), constituted under Article 243-I for panchayats, shall also review the financial position of the Municipalities.
The SFC will make recommendations on the distribution of taxes between the state and municipalities, assignment of taxes, and grants-in-aid.
This creates a single, unified body to look at the financial needs of both rural and urban local bodies.
Historical_Context: By tasking the same State Finance Commission with reviewing the finances of both panchayats and municipalities, the Constitution provides for an integrated approach to local government finance. The goal is to ensure a stable and predictable flow of funds to urban local bodies based on objective criteria.
Landmark_Cases: The functioning of SFCs and the implementation of their recommendations are crucial for the financial health of cities.
Related_Articles: Article 243I (Constitution of Finance Commission for Panchayats)

Title: Audit of accounts of Municipalities
Original_Description: The Legislature of a State may, by law, make provisions with respect to the maintenance of accounts by the Municipalities and the auditing of such accounts.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the State Legislature can make laws about how Municipalities must maintain their accounts and how those accounts should be audited.

This means Municipalities must properly record their income and expenses and follow auditing rules.

Ex : A Municipal Corporation must keep records of its spending on public services and these records can be checked by auditors.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Empowers the State Legislature to make laws regarding the maintenance and auditing of the accounts of Municipalities.
This is essential for ensuring financial discipline, transparency, and accountability in urban local governance.
Historical_Context: This provision is the urban counterpart to Article 243J for panchayats. It provides the legal basis for state-level audit mechanisms to oversee the financial operations of municipalities, which handle significant public funds.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article related to financial accountability.

Title: Elections to the Municipalities
Original_Description:
(1) The superintendence, direction and control of the preparation of electoral rolls for, and the conduct of, all elections to the Municipalities shall be vested in the State Election Commission referred to in article 243K.

 

(2) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, the Legislature of a State may, by law, make provision with respect to all matters relating to, or in connection with, elections to the Municipalities.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that elections to Municipalities will be conducted by the State Election Commission mentioned in the Constitution.

The State Election Commission will prepare voter lists and conduct all municipal elections.

Also, the State Legislature may make laws about matters related to these elections.

This means municipal elections are supervised by an independent body.

Ex : The State Election Commission organizes elections for city councils.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Vests the responsibility for conducting all elections to Municipalities in the State Election Commission (SEC).
The same SEC constituted under Article 243K for panchayat elections is responsible for municipal elections.
This includes the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of elections.
Historical_Context: This article ensures that municipal elections are conducted by an independent constitutional body, free from the control of the state executive. It makes the State Election Commission the single authority for all local body elections in a state, ensuring uniformity and impartiality.
Landmark_Cases: Kishan Singh Tomar vs Municipal Corporation of the City of Ahmedabad (2006): The Supreme Court affirmed the independence of the SEC and the constitutional mandate to hold timely municipal elections.

Title: Application to Union territories
Original_Description:
The provisions of this Part shall apply to the Union territories and shall, in their application to a Union territory, have effect as if the references to the Governor of a State were references to the Administrator of the Union territory appointed under article 239 and references to the Legislature or the Legislative Assembly of a State were references in relation to a Union territory having a Legislative Assembly, to that Legislative Assembly:

 

   Provided that the President may, by public notification, direct that the provisions of this Part shall apply to any Union territory or part thereof subject to such exceptions and modifications as he may specify in the notification.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the rules about Municipalities also apply to Union Territories.

In Union Territories, references to the Governor will mean the Administrator. If the Union Territory has a Legislative Assembly, references to State Legislature will mean that Assembly.

However, the President may apply these rules with certain changes or exceptions.

Ex : In a Union Territory with an Assembly, that Assembly may make laws about Municipalities.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Applies the provisions of Part IX-A (The Municipalities) to Union Territories.
Gives the President the power to apply these provisions with such exceptions and modifications as necessary for a particular UT.
Historical_Context: This article extends the constitutional framework for urban local self-government to the Union Territories, ensuring that the principles of the 74th Amendment are also implemented in centrally administered urban areas.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article.
Related_Articles: Article 243L (Application of Panchayat provisions to UTs)

Title: Part not to apply to certain areas
Original_Description:
(1) Nothing in this Part shall apply to the Scheduled Areas referred to in clause (1), and the tribal areas referred to in clause (2), of article 244.

 

(2) Nothing in this Part shall be construed to affect the functions and powers of the Darjeeling Gorkha Hill Council constituted under any law for the time being in force for the hill areas of the district of Darjeeling in the State of West Bengal.

 

(3) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, Parliament may, by law, extend the provisions of this Part to the Scheduled Areas and the tribal areas referred to in clause (1) subject to such exceptions and modifications as may be specified in such law, and no such law shall be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for the purposes of article 368.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the rules about Municipalities do not automatically apply to certain Scheduled and Tribal Areas.

It also protects the powers of certain special local councils mentioned in the Constitution.

However, Parliament may extend these rules to such areas with modifications.

This means special areas with unique systems of governance are treated differently.

Ex : A tribal area may continue with its special local council instead of regular municipal rules.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Specifies areas where Part IX-A (The Municipalities) does not automatically apply.
This includes Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas under Article 244, which have their own governance structures.
It also protected the functions and powers of the Darjeeling Gorkha Hill Council.
Parliament is empowered to extend these provisions to Scheduled and Tribal areas with modifications.
Historical_Context: Similar to Article 243M for panchayats, this article acknowledges the special governance arrangements in tribal areas and certain other specified regions. It prevents a uniform model of urban governance from being imposed on areas with distinct administrative and cultural contexts.
Landmark_Cases: The governance structures in these excluded areas are a complex mix of traditional and modern institutions.
Related_Articles: Article 243M (Part not to apply to certain areas - for Panchayats)
Article 244 (Administration of Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas)

Title: Committee for district planning
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every State must create a District Planning Committee at the district level.

The main duty of this Committee is to combine the development plans prepared by Panchayats and Municipalities in the district and prepare one draft development plan for the entire district.

Also, the State Legislature can decide how this Committee will be formed and how its members will be chosen. At least four-fifths of the total members must be elected from among the elected members of Panchayats and Municipalities in the district. Their number must reflect the population of rural and urban areas in that district.

While preparing the district plan, the Committee must consider matters of common interest like land use planning, sharing of water and other resources, development of infrastructure, and protection of the environment. It must also consider the available financial and other resources.

After preparing the plan, the Chairperson of the Committee must send it to the State Government.

Ex : A district plan may include both village road improvements and city drainage projects in one combined development plan.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Mandates the constitution of a District Planning Committee (DPC) in every state at the district level.
The DPC's primary function is to consolidate the development plans prepared by both Panchayats and Municipalities within the district.
It prepares a draft development plan for the district as a whole.
Not less than four-fifths of the DPC members must be elected from among the elected members of the district panchayat and municipalities.
Historical_Context: The 74th Amendment introduced the DPC as a critical institution for integrated and decentralized planning. It is designed to bridge the rural-urban divide in planning by creating a single platform where representatives of both panchayats and municipalities can come together to create a holistic development plan for the entire district.
Landmark_Cases: The effective functioning of DPCs remains a challenge in many states, and their role is a key topic in the discourse on decentralized governance.

Title: Committee for Metropolitan planning
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every Metropolitan area must have a Metropolitan Planning Committee.

The duty of this Committee is to prepare a draft development plan for the whole Metropolitan area.

Also, the State Legislature can decide how this Committee will be formed and how members will be selected. At least two-thirds of the members must be elected from among the elected members of Municipalities and Chairpersons of Panchayats in that Metropolitan area. Their number must reflect the population of urban and rural areas in that area.

While preparing the plan, the Committee must consider the plans made by Municipalities and Panchayats, matters of common interest like coordinated planning, sharing of water and natural resources, development of infrastructure, and protection of the environment. It must also consider the priorities of the Central and State Governments and the likely investments in that area.

After preparing the draft plan, the Chairperson must send it to the State Government.

Ex : A large metropolitan city may prepare a common plan covering traffic systems, housing, and water supply for the entire region.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Mandates the constitution of a Metropolitan Planning Committee (MPC) in every metropolitan area (population of 10 lakhs or more).
The MPC's function is to prepare a draft development plan for the entire metropolitan area.
It must consolidate plans prepared by the Municipalities and Panchayats within the area.
Not less than two-thirds of the MPC members must be elected from among the elected members of the municipalities and chairpersons of the panchayats in the area.
Historical_Context: The 74th Amendment Act, 1992, introduced the MPC to address the planning challenges of large urban agglomerations that often span multiple municipalities and rural areas. It aims to ensure integrated, region-wide planning for infrastructure, land use, and environmental conservation in major urban centres.
Landmark_Cases: The functioning and empowerment of MPCs are critical issues in urban planning and governance, though they have not been the subject of major Supreme Court litigation.

Title: Continuance of existing laws and Municipalities
Original_Description:
Notwithstanding anything in this Part, any provision of any law relating to Municipalities in force in a State immediately before the commencement of the Constitution (Seventy-fourth Amendment) Act, 1992, which is inconsistent with the provisions of this Part, shall continue to be in force until amended or repealed by a competent Legislature or other competent authority or until the expiration of one year from such commencement, whichever is earlier:

 

   Provided that all the Municipalities existing immediately before such commencement shall continue till the expiration of their duration, unless sooner dissolved by a resolution passed to that effect by the Legislative Assembly of that State or, in the case of a State having a Legislative Council, by each House of the Legislature of that State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that when the new constitutional rules about Municipalities came into force, existing laws about Municipalities that were inconsistent with these rules were allowed to continue only for one year or until they were changed, whichever happened first.

Also, Municipalities that were already functioning before the new rules came into force were allowed to continue for their full term unless they were dissolved earlier by the State Legislature.

This means States were given time to update their municipal laws to match the new constitutional provisions.

Ex : If a State had an old municipal law that did not follow the new rules, it had one year to change that law.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: This is a transitional provision related to the 74th Amendment.
It stipulated that all existing state laws on municipalities inconsistent with the new Part IX-A would continue for a maximum of one year.
It required states to amend their municipal laws to conform with the 74th Amendment within one year of its commencement.
Existing municipalities were allowed to complete their term unless dissolved earlier.
Historical_Context: This article provided a one-year grace period (from June 1, 1993, to May 31, 1994) for state governments to align their urban local body legislation with the new constitutional framework, ensuring a smooth transition to the new system of municipal governance.
Landmark_Cases: This was a transitional article with relevance for a limited period after the 74th Amendment came into force.

Title: Bar to interference by courts in electoral matters
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that courts cannot interfere in certain matters related to Municipal elections.

The validity of any law related to division of wards or allocation of seats cannot be questioned in any court.

Also, an election to a Municipality cannot be challenged in court except through an election petition filed before an authority as provided by State law.

This means once the election process starts, it should not be stopped by court cases. Any complaint must be made after the election through the proper legal process.

Ex : If someone believes there was a mistake in counting votes, they must file an election petition instead of stopping the election midway.
Subject: The Municipalities
Key_Points: Bars courts from interfering in certain electoral matters related to municipalities.
The validity of laws on delimitation and seat allotment cannot be challenged in court.
An election to a municipality can only be challenged through an election petition after the election is over.
This prevents judicial intervention during the electoral process.
Historical_Context: Mirroring the provisions for Parliament, State Legislatures, and Panchayats, this article aims to ensure that municipal elections are completed without being hindered by litigation. The proper legal remedy for electoral grievances is an election petition, not a writ petition to stop the process midway.
Landmark_Cases: Kishan Singh Tomar vs Municipal Corporation of the City of Ahmedabad (2006): The Supreme Court clarified that while courts cannot interfere in the election process once started, they can intervene to direct the State Election Commission to conduct elections if they are not being held on time.
Related_Articles: Article 329 (Bar to interference by courts in electoral matters of Parliament/State Legislatures)

Title: Definitions
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the meaning of certain words used in this Part about Co-operative Societies.

It defines an authorised person as a person referred to in the Constitution. It defines a board as the group of directors or governing body that manages a co-operative society. A co-operative society means a society registered under a law relating to co-operative societies in a State.

It also defines a multi-State co-operative society as a society operating in more than one State and registered under the relevant law. An office bearer means a President, Chairperson, Secretary, Treasurer, or similar person in the society. Registrar means the official appointed by the Government to oversee co-operative societies.

These definitions help in clearly understanding how co-operative societies are structured and managed.

Ex : A dairy co-operative registered under State law is called a co-operative society under this Part.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Provides definitions for terms used in Part IX-B (The Co-operative Societies).
Defines 'co-operative society', 'board', 'office bearer', 'multi-State co-operative society', and other relevant terms.
Historical_Context: This article, and the entire Part IX-B, was added by the 97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011. The amendment was aimed at promoting and professionalizing co-operative societies by giving them a constitutional status and setting out principles for their democratic and autonomous functioning.
Landmark_Cases: Union of India vs Rajendra N. Shah (2021): The Supreme Court struck down certain provisions of Part IX-B, holding that the amendment required ratification by state legislatures as 'co-operative societies' is a State List subject, and this procedure was not followed.
Related_Articles: Article 19(1)(c) (which includes the right to form co-operative societies)
Article 43B (DPSP for promotion of co-operative societies)

Title: Incorporation of co-operative societies
Original_Description: Subject to the provisions of this Part, the Legislature of a State may, by law, make provisions with respect to the incorporation, regulation and winding up of co-operative societies based on the principles of voluntary formation, democratic membercontrol, member-economic participation and autonomous functioning.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the State Legislature can make laws about the creation, regulation, and closing of co-operative societies.

However, such laws must follow certain basic principles. These include voluntary formation, democratic control by members, participation of members in economic activities, and independent functioning.

This means co-operative societies should be formed willingly by members and managed in a democratic and fair manner.

Ex : A group of farmers may voluntarily form a co-operative society to sell their crops together.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Empowers State Legislatures to make laws for the incorporation, regulation, and winding up of co-operative societies.
Mandates that such laws must be based on principles of voluntary formation, democratic member control, member economic participation, and autonomous functioning.
Historical_Context: This provision was introduced by the 97th Amendment to provide a constitutional guideline for state laws governing co-operatives, aiming to free them from excessive government control and promote them as genuine, member-driven democratic enterprises.
Landmark_Cases: The Supreme Court's judgment in Union of India vs Rajendra N. Shah (2021) impacted the applicability of this part on state-level co-operative societies, as the required state ratification was not obtained for the amendment.

Title: Number and term of members of board and its office bearers
Original_Description:
(1) The board shall consist of such number of directors as may be provided by the Legislature of a State, by law:

 

Provided that the maximum number of directors of a co-operative society shall not exceed twenty-one:

 

Provided further that the Legislature of a State shall, by law, provide for the reservation of one seat for the Scheduled Castes or the Scheduled Tribes and two seats for women on board of every co-operative society consisting of individuals as members and having members from such class of category of persons.

 

(2) The term of office of elected members of the board and its office bearers shall be five years from the date of election and the term of office bearers shall be coterminous with the term of the board:Provided that the board may fill a casual vacancy on the board by nomination out of the same class of members in respect of which the casual vacancy has arisen, if the term of office of the board is less than half of its original term.

 

(3) The Legislature of a State shall, by law, make provisions for co-option of persons to be members of the board having experience in the field of banking, management, finance or specialisation in any other field relating to the objects and activities undertaken by the co-operative society, as members of the board of such society:

 

Provided that the number of such co-opted members shall not exceed two in addition to twenty-one directors specified in the first proviso to clause (1):

 

Provided further that such co-opted members shall not have the right to vote in any election of the cooperative society in their capacity as such member or to be eligible to be elected as office bearers of the board:

 

Provided also that the functional directors of a co-operative society shall also be the members of the board and such members shall be excluded for the purpose of counting the total number of directors specified in the first proviso to clause (1).
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the number and term of members of the board of a co-operative society.

The State Legislature may decide the number of directors, but the maximum number cannot exceed twenty-one.

Also, State law must provide for reservation of one seat for Scheduled Castes or Scheduled Tribes and two seats for women in certain co-operative societies.

The term of elected members and office bearers of the board is five years. The term of office bearers ends along with the term of the board.

The law may also allow co-option of up to two persons with special knowledge or experience. However, such co-opted members cannot vote in elections or become office bearers.

Ex : A co-operative society board may have fifteen elected directors who serve for five years.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Sets the maximum number of directors on the board of a co-operative society at twenty-one.
Mandates a fixed five-year term for elected members of the board and its office bearers.
Requires state laws to provide for the reservation of one seat for SCs/STs and two seats for women on the board of certain co-operatives.
Historical_Context: Part of the 97th Amendment, this article aimed to bring uniformity and democratic principles to the governance structure of co-operative societies, preventing indefinitely long terms for office bearers and ensuring representation for women and marginalized communities.
Landmark_Cases: This provision was one of the parts of the 97th Amendment that the Supreme Court found to be invalid for want of state ratification in the Union of India vs Rajendra N. Shah (2021) case, in its application to state co-operative societies.

Title: Election of members of board
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in any law made by the Legislature of a State, the election of a board shall be conducted before the expiry of the term of the board so as to ensure that the newly elected members of the board assume office immediately on the expiry of the term of the office of members of the outgoing board.

 

(2) The superintendence, direction and control of the preparation of electoral rolls for, and the conduct of, all elections to a co-operative society shall vest in such an authority or body, as may be provided by the Legislature of a State, by law:

 

Provided that the Legislature of a State may, by law, provide for the procedure and guidelines for the conduct of such elections.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how elections to the board of a co-operative society must be conducted.

The responsibility of conducting, supervising, and controlling these elections is given to an authority or body as decided by State law.

Also, elections must be completed before the term of the existing board ends. If the board is dissolved earlier, elections must be completed within six months.

However, if the remaining period of the board is less than six months, it is not necessary to hold elections for that short period.

This means that co-operative societies must have regular and timely elections so that democratic control is maintained.

Ex : If the board of a co-operative society finishes its five-year term, new elections must be conducted before the term ends.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Mandates that elections to the board of a co-operative society must be conducted before the expiry of the current board's term.
Vests the responsibility for conducting these elections in an authority or body as provided by state law.
Historical_Context: A key objective of the 97th Amendment was to ensure regular and timely elections in co-operative societies, which were often plagued by indefinite postponements and the appointment of administrators by the government. This article aimed to secure the democratic functioning of these bodies.
Landmark_Cases: This provision was also affected by the Supreme Court's 2021 judgment on the 97th Amendment.
Related_Articles: Article 243ZJ (Number and term of members of board)

Title: Supersession and suspension of board and interim management
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force, no board shall be superseded or kept under supersession for a period exceeding six months:

 

Provided that the board may be superseded or kept under suspension in a case—

 

(i) of its persistent default; or

 

(ii) of negligence in the performance of its duties; or

 

(iii) the board has committed any act prejudicial to the interests of the co-operative society or its members; or

 

(iv) there is stalemate in the constitution or functions of the board; or (v) the authority or body as provided by the Legislature of a State, by law, under clause (2) of article 243ZK, has failed to conduct elections in accordance with the provisions of the State Act:

 

Provided further that the board of any such co-operative society shall not be superseded or kept under suspension where there is no Government shareholding or loan or financial assistance or any guarantee by the Government:

 

Provided also that in case of a co-operative society carrying on the business of banking, the provisions of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 shall also apply:

 

Provided also that in case of a co-operative society, other than a multi- State co-operative society, carrying on the business of banking, the provisions of this clause shall have the effect as if for the words ―six months‖, the words "one year" had been substituted.

 

(2) In case of supersession of a board, the administrator appointed to manage the affairs of such cooperative society shall arrange for conduct of elections within the period specified in clause (1) and hand over the management to the elected board.

 

(3) The Legislature of a State may, by law, make provisions for the conditions of service of the administrator.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains the rules about suspension or dissolution of the board of a co-operative society.

The board can be suspended or dissolved only for specific reasons such as persistent default, negligence in duties, acts that harm the interests of the society, or if there is a failure to hold elections.

Also, the board cannot be kept under suspension or dissolution for more than six months.

However, in the case of co-operative societies that do not receive government support, the board cannot be suspended or dissolved unless the society itself agrees.

This means that the government cannot easily remove the board of a co-operative society without proper reasons.

Ex : If a co-operative society board fails to conduct elections on time, it may be dissolved for that reason.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Restricts the power of the government to supersede (dissolve) the elected board of a co-operative society.
Supersession is not allowed for a period exceeding six months.
Specifies the grounds on which a board can be superseded, such as persistent default or negligence.
An administrator appointed during supersession must arrange for elections within the six-month period.
Historical_Context: This provision was a direct response to the widespread practice of state governments superseding elected boards of co-operatives for long periods and appointing their own administrators, which undermined their autonomy. The 97th Amendment sought to severely curtail this power.
Landmark_Cases: This was another key provision of Part IX-B that the Supreme Court held would not apply to state co-operative societies due to the lack of ratification.
Related_Articles: Article 243ZJ, 243ZK (dealing with board's term and elections)

Title: Audit of accounts of co-operative societies
Original_Description:
(1) The Legislature of a State may, by law, make provisions with respect to the maintenance of accounts by the co-operative societies and the auditing of such accounts at least once in each financial year. 

 

(2) The Legislature of a State shall, by law, lay down the minimum qualifications and experience of auditors and auditing firms that shall be eligible for auditing accounts of the co-operative societies.

 

(3) Every co-operative society shall cause to be audited by an auditor or auditing firms referred to in clause (2) appointed by the general body of the co-operative society:

 

Provided that such auditors or auditing firms shall be appointed from a panel approved by a State Government or an authority authorised by the State Government in this behalf.

 

(4) The accounts of every co-operative society shall be audited within six months of the close of the financial year to which such accounts relate.

 

(5) The audit report of the accounts of an apex co-operative society, as may be defined by the State Act, shall be laid before the State Legislature in the manner, as may be provided by the State Legislature, by law.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the accounts of every co-operative society must be audited.

The audit must be conducted by an auditor or auditing firm chosen from a panel approved by the State Government.

Also, the audit must be completed within six months from the end of the financial year.

This means that co-operative societies must maintain proper financial records and ensure that their accounts are checked regularly.

Ex : After the financial year ends in March, the society must complete its audit by September.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Requires state laws to provide for the maintenance of accounts by co-operatives and their audit at least once a year.
The audit must be completed within six months of the close of the financial year.
State law is to lay down the minimum qualifications for auditors.
Historical_Context: The 97th Amendment introduced this article to promote financial discipline, transparency, and accountability in the functioning of co-operative societies.
Landmark_Cases: This provision was also affected by the Supreme Court's 2021 judgment on the 97th Amendment.
Related_Articles: Article 243ZP (Returns to be filed by co-operatives)

Title: Convening of general body meetings
Original_Description: The Legislature of a State may, by law, make provisions that the annual general body meeting of every co-operative society shall be convened within a period of six months of close of the financial year to transact the business as may be provided in such law.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every co-operative society must hold a general body meeting of its members within six months from the end of each financial year.

In this meeting, the society must present important matters such as the annual report, audited accounts, plans for the next year, and other required information.

This means that members of the society must be informed about how the society is functioning and how money is being used.

Ex : Members of a housing co-operative meet once a year to review the accounts and future plans.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Mandates that state laws should ensure that every co-operative society holds its annual general body meeting.
The meeting must be convened within six months of the close of the financial year.
Historical_Context: This provision was introduced by the 97th Amendment to ensure that the supreme authority of a co-operative, the general body of its members, meets regularly to review its functioning, approve accounts, and hold the elected board accountable.
Landmark_Cases: This provision was also affected by the Supreme Court's 2021 judgment on the 97th Amendment.
Related_Articles: Article 243ZO (Right of a member to get information)

Title: Right of a member to get information
