Original_Description:
(1) The Legislature of a State may, by law, provide for access to every member of a co-operative society to the books, information and accounts of the cooperative society kept in regular transaction of its business with such members.

 

(2) The Legislature of a State may, by law, make provisions to ensure the participation of members of the management of the co-operative society providing minimum requirement of attending meetings by the members and utilising the minimum level of services as may be provided in such law.

 

(3) The Legislature of a State may, by law, provide for co-operative education and training for its members.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every co-operative society has the right to get information about its functioning.

Members must have access to important documents such as accounts, meeting minutes, and other records as provided by law.

Also, the society must file returns and reports with the authority as required by State law.

This means there must be transparency in the working of co-operative societies.

Ex : A member can ask to see the financial records of the co-operative society.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Directs state legislatures to make laws to give members of a co-operative society access to its books, accounts, and information.
Also directs states to make provisions for co-operative education and training for members.
Historical_Context: Introduced by the 97th Amendment, this article aims to empower the members of a co-operative society by ensuring their right to information about its functioning. This promotes transparency and strengthens member control over the management.
Landmark_Cases: This provision was also affected by the Supreme Court's 2021 judgment on the 97th Amendment.
Related_Articles: Article 19(1)(a) (Right to freedom of speech and expression, which includes the right to information)

Title: Returns
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the provisions of this Part will apply to multi-State co-operative societies, but Parliament has the power to make laws for them.

This means that societies working in more than one State are mainly governed by laws made by Parliament instead of State laws.

Ex : A co-operative society operating in both Maharashtra and Gujarat will follow the central law made by Parliament.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Mandates every co-operative society to file annual returns within six months of the close of the financial year.
The returns should include an annual report, audited accounts, and other specified information.
The returns are to be filed with an authority designated by the state government.
Historical_Context: This provision of the 97th Amendment aims to ensure regulatory oversight and create a public record of the activities and financial health of co-operative societies, thereby enhancing accountability.
Landmark_Cases: This provision was also affected by the Supreme Court's 2021 judgment on the 97th Amendment.

Title: Offences and Penalties
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the provisions of this Part do not apply to certain types of co-operative societies.

It does not apply to co-operative societies that deal with banking, as they are governed by separate banking laws.

Also, it does not apply to societies that operate only for specific purposes as defined by law.

This means that some special types of co-operative societies are governed by other specific laws.

Ex : A co-operative bank follows banking laws instead of these provisions.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Empowers state legislatures to make laws specifying offences and penalties related to co-operative societies.
Lists certain acts that must be included as offences, such as wilfully making a false return, disobeying summons, and adopting corrupt practices during elections.
Historical_Context: This part of the 97th Amendment provides a framework for state laws to create a deterrent against mismanagement and malpractices in co-operative societies, thereby protecting the interests of the members and the public.
Landmark_Cases: This provision was also affected by the Supreme Court's 2021 judgment on the 97th Amendment.
Related_Articles: This article provides the penal framework for Part IX-B.

Title: Application to multi-State co-operative societies
Original_Description: The provisions of this Part shall apply to the multi-State co-operative societies subject to the modification that any reference to "Legislature of a State", "State Act" or State Government" shall be construed as a reference to "Parliament", "Central Act" or "the Central Government" respectively.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that if there is any inconsistency between the provisions of this Part and any other law, the provisions of this Part will prevail.

However, this applies only to matters covered under this Part.

This means that the constitutional rules about co-operative societies are stronger than ordinary laws in case of conflict.

Ex : If a State law contradicts these constitutional provisions about board elections, the constitutional provision will apply.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Applies the provisions of Part IX-B to multi-State co-operative societies.
For multi-State co-operatives, references to 'State Legislature' are to be read as 'Parliament', and 'State Government' as 'Central Government'.
Historical_Context: While state-level co-operatives are a state subject, multi-State co-operatives (which operate in more than one state) are a Union subject. The 97th Amendment aimed to reform both. The Supreme Court in 2021, while striking down the part of the amendment concerning state co-operatives, upheld its validity for multi-State co-operative societies.
Landmark_Cases: Union of India vs Rajendra N. Shah (2021): This judgment is directly relevant as it upheld the validity of Part IX-B in relation to multi-State co-operative societies.

Title: Application to Union territories
Original_Description:
The provisions of this Part shall apply to the Union territories and shall, in their application to a Union territory, having no Legislative Assembly as if the references to the Legislature of a State were a reference to the administrator thereof appointed under article 239 and, in relation to a Union territory having a Legislative Assembly, to that Legislative Assembly:

 

Provided that the President may, by notification in the Official Gazette, direct that the provisions of this Part shall not apply to any Union territory or part thereof as he may specify in the notification.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that existing laws related to co-operative societies will continue to operate until they are amended or repealed, but only for one year from the commencement of this Part.

Also, after that period, such laws must be made consistent with these constitutional provisions.

This means States were given time to update their co-operative society laws to match the new constitutional rules.

Ex : If a State had an old co-operative law that did not follow these new rules, it had one year to correct it.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: Applies the provisions of Part IX-B to Union Territories.
Gives the President the power to direct that these provisions shall not apply to a particular UT or part thereof.
Historical_Context: This article extends the constitutional framework for co-operative societies, as introduced by the 97th Amendment, to the Union Territories, while providing flexibility for the President to make exceptions as needed for specific UTs.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article.

Title: Continuance of existing laws
Original_Description: Notwithstanding anything in this Part, any provision of any law relating to co-operative societies in force in a State immediately before the commencement of the Constitution (Ninety-seventh Amendment) Act, 2011, which is inconsistent with the provisions of this Part, shall continue to be in force until amended or repealed by a competent Legislature or other competent authority or until the expiration of one year from such commencement, whichever is less.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that any amendment made to the Constitution regarding co-operative societies can be challenged in court only on limited grounds.

It also clarifies that certain earlier actions taken under old laws will not automatically become invalid just because of these new provisions.

This means that the change in constitutional provisions does not automatically cancel past valid actions.

Ex : If a board was legally elected under an old law before these changes, it does not become invalid only because new rules were introduced.
Subject: Co-operative Societies
Key_Points: This is a transitional provision for the 97th Amendment.
It provided a one-year window for existing laws on co-operative societies to be brought into conformity with the new constitutional provisions.
Inconsistent laws would cease to have effect after one year from the commencement of the amendment.
Historical_Context: Similar to the transitional provisions for Panchayats and Municipalities, this article was intended to ensure a smooth transition by giving state legislatures time to amend their existing co-operative laws to align with the new constitutional mandate of Part IX-B.
Landmark_Cases: This article was relevant for the one-year period following the commencement of the 97th Amendment in 2012.

Title: Administration of Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas
Original_Description:
(1) The provisions of the Fifth Schedule shall apply to the administration and control of the Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in any State other than the States of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.

 

(2) The provisions of the Sixth Schedule shall apply to the administration of the tribal areas in the States of Assam Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that special rules apply for the administration and control of certain tribal areas in India.

In most States of India, the areas that are declared as Scheduled Areas and the people who are Scheduled Tribes are governed according to the Fifth Schedule.

However, in the States of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram, the tribal areas are governed according to the Sixth Schedule instead of the Fifth Schedule.

This means that there are two different systems to manage tribal areas in India, depending on the State.

Ex : A tribal area in Maharashtra will follow the Fifth Schedule system, but a tribal area in Assam will follow the Sixth Schedule system.
Subject: Scheduled and Tribal Areas
Key_Points: Provides for a special system of administration for certain designated 'Scheduled Areas' and 'Tribal Areas'.
Applies the provisions of the Fifth Schedule to Scheduled Areas in various states across India (except four northeastern states).
Applies the provisions of the Sixth Schedule to the Tribal Areas in the states of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram.
This creates two different schemes for the administration of tribal areas.
Historical_Context: The framers recognized the need to protect the distinct culture, customs, and land rights of the tribal population from exploitation. This article, along with the Fifth and Sixth Schedules, creates a protective legal and administrative framework. The Sixth Schedule provides for a higher degree of autonomy through the creation of Autonomous District Councils.
Landmark_Cases: Samatha vs State of Andhra Pradesh (1997): A landmark judgment that interpreted the Fifth Schedule to protect tribal lands from being transferred to non-tribals for private mining operations.
Related_Articles: Article 243M, 243ZC (Exclusion of these areas from Part IX and IX-A)

Title: Formation of an autonomous State comprising certain tribal areas in Assam and creation of local Legislature or Council of Ministers or both therefor
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, Parliament may, by law, form within the State of Assam an autonomous State (comprising whether wholly or in part) all or any of the tribal areas specified in Part I of the table appended to paragraph 20 of the Sixth Schedule and create therefor —

 

(a) a body, whether elected or partly nominated and partly elected, to function as a Legislature for the autonomous State, or

 

(b) a Council of Ministers,

 

or both with such constitution, powers and functions, in each case, as may be specified in the law.

 

(2) Any such law as is referred to in clause (1) may, in particular, —

 

(a) specify the matters enumerated in the State List or the Concurrent List with respect to which the Legislature of the autonomous State shall have power to make laws for the whole or any part thereof, whether to the exclusion of the Legislature of the State of Assam or otherwise;

 

(b) define the matters with respect to which the executive power of the autonomous State shall extend;

 

(c) provide that any tax levied by the State of Assam shall be assigned to the autonomous State in so far as the proceeds thereof are attributable to the autonomous State;

 

(d) provide that any reference to a State in any article of this Constitution shall be construed as including a reference to the autonomous State; and

 

(e) make such supplemental, incidental and consequential provisions as may be deemed necessary.

 

(3) An amendment of any such law as aforesaid in so far as such amendment relates to any of the matters specified in sub-clause (a) or sub-clause (b) of clause (2) shall have no effect unless the amendment is passed in each House of Parliament by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.

 

(4) Any such law as is referred to in this article shall not be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for the purposes of article 368 notwithstanding that it contains any provision which amends or has the effect of amending this Constitution.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament has the power to create an autonomous State within the State of Assam by law.

This autonomous State can include all or some of the tribal areas mentioned in the Sixth Schedule.

Parliament can create a separate Legislature for that autonomous State, or a Council of Ministers, or both. It can decide their structure, powers and functions through law.

Also, the law can clearly mention which subjects the autonomous State can make laws on, and how far its executive power will extend.

In addition, the law can provide that certain taxes collected by Assam may be given to the autonomous State if they are related to that area.

It can also clarify that whenever the Constitution refers to a State, it may include this autonomous State.

However, if any change is made later to the powers related to law-making or executive matters, such change must be passed by a two-thirds majority in both Houses of Parliament.

This means that Parliament can give a higher level of self-governance to certain tribal areas in Assam through a special law.

Ex : If certain tribal areas in Assam want more control over their local matters, Parliament can create an autonomous State with its own Legislature to manage those matters.
Subject: Scheduled and Tribal Areas
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to create an 'autonomous state' within the State of Assam.
This autonomous state can comprise some or all of the tribal areas specified in the Sixth Schedule.
Parliament can create a local Legislature or a Council of Ministers, or both, for such an autonomous state.
This is a special provision applicable only to Assam.
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 22nd Amendment Act, 1969. It was a response to the political aspirations of the tribal communities in the hill areas of Assam. The provision was used to create the autonomous state of Meghalaya in 1970, which was later granted full statehood in 1972. It provides a constitutional mechanism for granting a higher degree of autonomy than available under the Sixth Schedule.
Landmark_Cases: This is a special power-conferring article that has been used in the past for state formation in the North-East.
Related_Articles: Article 244 (Administration of Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas)

Title: Extent of laws made by Parliament and by the Legislatures of States
Original_Description:
(1) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, Parliament may make laws for the whole or any part of the territory of India, and the Legislature of a State may make laws for the whole or any part of the State.

 

(2) No law made by Parliament shall be deemed to be invalid on the ground that it would have extra-territorial operation.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament can make laws for the whole of India or for any part of India.

Also, a State Legislature can make laws for the whole State or for any part of that State.

However, Parliament can even make a law that has effect outside India. Such a law will not become invalid just because it affects matters beyond Indian territory.

This means that Parliament has wider law-making power compared to State Legislatures.

Ex : Parliament can make a law that applies to Indian citizens working abroad, while a State like Karnataka can make a law that applies only within Karnataka.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Defines the territorial limits of the legislative powers of Parliament and State Legislatures.
Parliament can legislate for the entire territory of India or any part of it.
A State Legislature can legislate for the whole state or any part of it.
Laws made by Parliament can have extra-territorial application (apply to Indian citizens and their property anywhere in the world).
State laws generally cannot have extra-territorial operation, unless there is a sufficient 'nexus' or connection between the state and the subject of the law.
Historical_Context: This is the first article of Part XI, which deals with Union-State relations. It establishes the foundational principle of legislative jurisdiction based on territory, a fundamental aspect of a federal structure.
Landmark_Cases: Wallace Bros. & Co. Ltd. vs Commissioner of Income Tax (1948): The Privy Council, in a pre-constitution case, established the doctrine of 'territorial nexus', which holds that a state can legislate on an extra-territorial object if there is a sufficient connection between the state and that object.

Title: Subject-matter of laws made by Parliament and by the Legislatures of States
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in clauses (2) and (3), Parliament has exclusive power to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in List I in the Seventh Schedule (in this Constitution referred to as the “Union List”).

 

(2) Notwithstanding anything in clause (3), Parliament, and, subject to clause (1), the Legislature of any State also, have power to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in List III in the Seventh Schedule (in this Constitution referred to as the “Concurrent List”).

 

(3) Subject to clauses (1) and (2), the Legislature of any State has exclusive power to make laws for such State or any part thereof with respect to any of the matters enumerated in List II in the Seventh Schedule (in this Constitution referred to as the “State List”).

 

(4) Parliament has power to make laws with respect to any matter for any part of the territory of India not included in a State notwithstanding that such matter is a matter enumerated in the State List.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how law-making subjects are divided between Parliament and State Legislatures.

Parliament has exclusive power to make laws on matters listed in the Union List.

Also, both Parliament and State Legislatures can make laws on matters listed in the Concurrent List. However, Parliament’s power is stronger if there is any conflict.

However, State Legislatures have exclusive power to make laws on matters listed in the State List, but this is subject to the powers given to Parliament in certain situations.

In addition, Parliament can make laws on any subject for areas that are not part of any State, even if that subject is normally in the State List.

This means that subjects are clearly divided into three lists so that both the Union and the States know their areas of power.

Ex : Parliament makes laws on defence because it is in the Union List, a State makes laws on police because it is in the State List, and both can make laws on education because it is in the Concurrent List.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Distributes legislative power between the Union and the States based on subject matter.
It establishes the three lists of the Seventh Schedule:
List I (Union List): Parliament has exclusive power (e.g., Defence, Foreign Affairs, Currency).
List II (State List): State Legislatures have exclusive power (e.g., Public Order, Police, Health).
List III (Concurrent List): Both Parliament and State Legislatures can make laws (e.g., Education, Forests, Criminal Law).
Historical_Context: This article is the bedrock of India's legislative federalism. The detailed three-fold distribution of subjects, adopted from the Government of India Act, 1935, is a unique feature designed to minimize jurisdictional conflicts. In case of any conflict between the lists, the Union List prevails over the Concurrent and State Lists, and the Concurrent List prevails over the State List.
Landmark_Cases: State of Bombay vs F.N. Balsara (1951): The Supreme Court applied the 'Doctrine of Pith and Substance' to determine the true nature of a law when it seems to transgress into the other legislature's domain.
Gujarat University vs Krishna Ranganath Mudholkar (1963): Dealt with conflicts between Union and State laws on the subject of education.

Title: Special provision with respect to goods and services tax
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in articles 246 and 254, Parliament, and, subject to clause (2), the Legislature of every State, have power to make laws with respect to goods and services tax imposed by the Union or by such State.

(2) Parliament has exclusive power to make laws with respect to goods and services tax where the supply of goods, or of services, or both takes place in the course of inter-State trade or commerce.

Explanation.—The provisions of this article, shall, in respect of goods and services tax referred to in clause (5) of article 279A, take effect from the date recommended by the Goods and Services Tax Council.]
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that both Parliament and State Legislatures have the power to make laws about goods and services tax.

However, when goods or services move from one State to another, only Parliament has the exclusive power to make laws for that tax.

This means that for local sales within a State, both the Union and the State can make GST laws, but for inter-State trade, only Parliament can make the law.

Also, these provisions take effect from the date recommended by the Goods and Services Tax Council.

Ex : If a shop in Maharashtra sells goods within Maharashtra, both State and Union GST laws apply, but if goods are sold from Maharashtra to Gujarat, only Parliament’s law governs that inter-State tax.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Gives concurrent power to both Parliament and State Legislatures to make laws on Goods and Services Tax (GST).
Gives Parliament the exclusive power to make laws on GST for inter-state trade and commerce (IGST).
This article overrides the traditional separation of powers in Articles 246 and 254 for the purpose of GST.
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2016, which introduced the GST regime. GST replaced a multitude of indirect taxes levied by the Centre and states. Article 246A created a unique concurrent power for both levels of government to legislate on this single tax, a fundamental shift in Indian fiscal federalism.
Landmark_Cases: Union of India vs Mohit Minerals Pvt. Ltd. (2022): The Supreme Court held that the recommendations of the GST Council are not binding on the Union and State legislatures, emphasizing the importance of cooperative federalism.
Related_Articles: Article 269A (Levy and collection of GST in course of inter-State trade)

Title: Power of Parliament to provide for the establishment of certain additional courts
Original_Description: Notwithstanding anything in this Chapter, Parliament may by law provide for the establishment of any additional courts for the better administration of laws made by Parliament or of any existing laws with respect to a matter enumerated in the Union List.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament can create additional courts by law for better administration of laws.

These courts can be created to properly implement laws made by Parliament or laws related to subjects in the Union List.

This means that if needed, Parliament can set up special courts to handle certain types of cases more effectively.

Ex : Parliament can create a special court to deal only with cases related to income tax laws made by it.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to establish additional courts.
The purpose of these courts is the better administration of laws made by Parliament or laws related to subjects in the Union List.
Historical_Context: This article provides the constitutional basis for Parliament to create specialized courts or tribunals to deal with matters falling under its legislative competence. This allows for the creation of expert judicial bodies to handle specific areas of law, supplementing the work of the regular judiciary.
Landmark_Cases: This is a power-conferring article.

Title: Residuary powers of legislation
Original_Description:
(1) Parliament has exclusive power to make any law with respect to any matter not enumerated in the Concurrent List or State List.

 

(2) Such power shall include the power of making any law imposing a tax not mentioned in either of those Lists.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament has exclusive power to make laws on subjects that are not mentioned in the Union List, State List or Concurrent List.

This means that if a new subject comes up which is not written in any of the three lists, only Parliament can make a law about it.

Also, Parliament can impose a tax on such matters even if that tax is not mentioned in any of the lists.

This ensures that there is no gap in law-making power.

Ex : If a new type of digital activity arises that is not mentioned in any list, Parliament can make a law to regulate it.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Vests the 'residuary powers' of legislation exclusively with the Parliament.
This means that if a subject matter is not mentioned in any of the three lists (Union, State, or Concurrent), only Parliament has the power to make a law on it.
This includes the power to impose a tax not mentioned in any list.
Historical_Context: This provision reflects the strong-centre bias of the Indian federation. Unlike other federations like the USA, where residuary powers are with the states, the Indian Constitution allocates them to the Union. This ensures that the central legislature has the authority to legislate on any new subject that may emerge in the future.
Landmark_Cases: Union of India vs H.S. Dhillon (1972): The Supreme Court upheld Parliament's power to enact the wealth tax on agricultural land by relying on its residuary powers under this article.

Title: Power of Parliament to legislate with respect to a matter in the State List in the national interest
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in the foregoing provisions of this Chapter, if the Council of States has declared by resolution supported by not less than twothirds of the members present and voting that it is necessary or expedient in the national interest that Parliament should make laws with respect to any matter enumerated in the State List specified in the resolution, it shall be lawful for Parliament to make laws for the whole or any part of the territory of India with respect to that matter while the resolution remains in force.

 

(2) A resolution passed under clause (1) shall remain in force for such period not exceeding one year as may be specified therein:

 

   Provided that, if and so often as a resolution approving the continuance in force of any such resolution is passed in the manner provided in clause (1), such resolution shall continue in force for a further period of one year from the date on which under this clause it would otherwise have ceased to be in force.

 

(3) A law made by Parliament which Parliament would not but for the passing of a resolution under clause (1) have been competent to make shall, to the extent of the incompetency, cease to have effect on the expiration of a period of six months after the resolution has ceased to be in force, except as respects things done or omitted to be done before the expiration of the said period.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament can make laws on matters in the State List if it is necessary in the national interest.

However, this can happen only if the Council of States passes a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of the members present and voting.

Such a resolution remains valid for one year, but it can be extended again for another year in the same manner.

Also, any law made by Parliament under this power will stop being effective six months after the resolution ends, except for actions already taken under it.

This means that Parliament can temporarily take over State subjects when it is important for the whole country.

Ex : If it is necessary for national security to regulate a matter normally handled by States, the Council of States can allow Parliament to make a law on it for a limited time.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Allows Parliament to make laws on a subject in the State List.
This can only be done if the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution declaring it necessary in the 'national interest'.
The resolution must be supported by a special majority of two-thirds of the members present and voting.
Such a resolution is valid for one year at a time but can be renewed.
The law made by Parliament ceases to have effect six months after the resolution expires.
Historical_Context: This is a significant provision that allows the Union to encroach upon the legislative domain of the states under specific circumstances. The requirement of a resolution by the Rajya Sabha (the Council of States) is a key federal safeguard, as it signifies the consent of the representatives of the states themselves for this temporary centralization of power.
Landmark_Cases: This is a power-conferring article that highlights the flexible nature of Indian federalism.
Related_Articles: Article 250 (Parliament's power to legislate on State List during emergency)

Title: Power of Parliament to legislate with respect to any matter in the State List if a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in this Chapter, Parliament shall, while a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, have power to make laws for the whole or any part of the territory of India with respect to any of the matters enumerated in the State List.

 

(2) A law made by Parliament which Parliament would not but for the issue of a Proclamation of Emergency have been competent to make shall, to the extent of the incompetency, cease to have effect on the expiration of a period of six months after the Proclamation has ceased to operate, except as respects things done or omitted to be done before the expiration of the said period.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that during a national emergency, Parliament can make laws on any matter in the State List.

This power continues only while the emergency is in operation.

However, such laws will stop being effective six months after the emergency ends, except for actions already taken under them.

This means that during a serious national crisis, the central government can take control over State subjects to handle the situation properly.

Ex : During a national emergency, Parliament can make laws related to public order in all States if needed to manage the crisis.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to legislate on any subject in the State List during a National Emergency (under Article 352).
This power extends to the whole of India or any part of it.
The laws made under this provision become inoperative six months after the emergency has ceased to operate.
Historical_Context: This provision transforms the Indian federal system into a unitary one during a national emergency. It allows the central government to assume supreme legislative power to deal with the crisis, overriding the normal distribution of powers between the Union and the States.
Landmark_Cases: This is a key emergency provision.

Title: Inconsistency between laws made by Parliament under articles 249 and 250 and laws made by the Legislatures of States
Original_Description: Nothing in articles 249 and 250 shall restrict the power of the Legislature of a State to make any law which under this Constitution it has power to make, but if any provision of a law made by the Legislature of a State is repugnant to any provision of a law made by Parliament which Parliament has under either of the said articles power to make, the law made by Parliament, whether passed before or after the law made by the Legislature of the State, shall prevail, and the law made by the Legislature of the State shall to the extent of the repugnancy, but so long only as the law made by Parliament continues to have effect, be inoperative.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that even when Parliament makes a law under special powers given in certain situations, State Legislatures can still make laws on their normal subjects.

However, if there is any conflict between a State law and a law made by Parliament under those special powers, the law made by Parliament will prevail.

The State law will become ineffective only to the extent of the conflict and only while the Parliamentary law remains in force.

This means that in case of conflict during such special situations, the central law will have priority.

Ex : If a State makes a law on a subject but Parliament has already made a conflicting law under special national interest power, the central law will apply until it remains valid.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Deals with inconsistency between a law made by Parliament (under Art 249 or 250) and a law made by a State Legislature on the same State List subject.
It clarifies that the law made by Parliament will prevail.
The state law is not completely void but becomes 'inoperative' to the extent of its repugnancy, for as long as the parliamentary law is in force.
Historical_Context: This article establishes a clear rule of precedence for the temporary and extraordinary situations covered by Articles 249 and 250. It reinforces the supremacy of Parliament's law when it is legislating on a state subject in the national interest or during an emergency.
Landmark_Cases: This is a rule of interpretation to resolve legislative conflicts.
Related_Articles: Article 249 (Parliament's power on state list in national interest)
Article 250 (Parliament's power on state list during emergency)

Title: Power of Parliament to legislate for two or more States by consent and adoption of such legislation by
Original_Description:
(1) If it appears to the Legislatures of two or more States to be desirable that any of the matters with respect to which Parliament has no power to make laws for the States except as provided in articles 249 and 250 should be regulated in such States by Parliament by law, and if resolutions to that effect are passed by all the Houses of the Legislatures of those States, it shall be lawful for Parliament to pass an Act for regulating that matter accordingly, and any Act so passed shall apply to such States and to any other State by which it is adopted afterwards by resolution passed in that behalf by the House or, where there are two Houses, by each of the Houses of the Legislature of that State.

 

(2) Any Act so passed by Parliament may be amended or repealed by an Act of Parliament passed or adopted in like manner but shall not, as respects any State to which it applies, be amended or repealed by an Act of the Legislature of that State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that if two or more States feel that Parliament should make a law on a matter that normally belongs to the State List, they can pass resolutions requesting Parliament to do so.

Once such resolutions are passed, Parliament can make a law for those States on that matter.

Also, any other State can later choose to adopt that same law by passing a similar resolution.

However, once such a law is made, only Parliament can amend or repeal it. The State Legislatures cannot change it on their own.

This means that States can voluntarily give Parliament the power to make a common law for them on certain matters.

Ex : If two States want the same rule for controlling wildlife, they can request Parliament to make one common law for them.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Allows Parliament to make a law on a State List subject if two or more states pass resolutions requesting it.
Such a law will apply only to the states that made the request.
Other states can adopt the same law later by passing a similar resolution.
Only Parliament can amend or repeal such a law, not the state legislatures.
Historical_Context: This is a mechanism for achieving uniformity in legislation on state subjects when desired by the states themselves, without having to amend the Constitution. It is a feature of cooperative federalism. Several laws have been passed under this provision, such as the Prize Competition Act, 1955, and the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972.
Landmark_Cases: This is a power-conferring article.

Title: Legislation for giving effect to international agreements
Original_Description: Notwithstanding anything in the foregoing provisions of this Chapter, Parliament has power to make any law for the whole or any part of the territory of India for implementing any treaty, agreement or convention with any other country or countries or any decision made at any international conference, association or other body
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament can make laws to implement any international treaty, agreement or decision taken at an international meeting.

This power applies even if the subject normally belongs to the State List.

This means that to fulfill international commitments, Parliament can make laws for the whole or any part of India regardless of the normal division of subjects.

Ex : If India signs an international agreement on environmental protection, Parliament can make a law to follow that agreement even if the subject relates to State matters.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Gives Parliament the power to make laws on any subject (including those in the State List) to implement international treaties, agreements, or conventions.
This power overrides the normal distribution of legislative powers between the Union and the States.
It enables the central government to fulfill its international obligations.
Historical_Context: In international law, the national government is responsible for fulfilling the country's treaty obligations. This article ensures that the Indian Parliament has the necessary legislative power to do so, even if the subject matter of the treaty falls within the legislative domain of the states. This provision has been used to enact laws on subjects like environment (e.g., Environment Protection Act, 1986) and intellectual property.
Landmark_Cases: The enactment of laws to comply with WTO obligations and various international environmental conventions relies on the power derived from this article.
Related_Articles: Article 51 (Promotion of international peace and security)

Title: Inconsistency between laws made by Parliament and laws made by the Legislatures of States
Original_Description:
(1) If any provision of a law made by the Legislature of a State is repugnant to any provision of a law made by Parliament which Parliament is competent to enact, or to any provision of an existing law with respect to one of the matters enumerated in the Concurrent List, then, subject to the provisions of clause (2), the law made by Parliament, whether passed before or after the law made by the Legislature of such State, or, as the case may be, the existing law, shall prevail and the law made by the Legislature of the State shall, to the extent of the repugnancy, be void.

 

(2) Where a law made by the Legislature of a State with respect to one of the matters enumerated in the Concurrent List contains any provision repugnant to the provisions of an earlier law made by Parliament or an existing law with respect to that matter, then, the law so made by the Legislature of such State shall, if it has been reserved for the consideration of the President and has received his assent, prevail in that State:

 

   Provided that nothing in this clause shall prevent Parliament from enacting at any time any law with respect to the same matter including a law adding to, amending, varying or repealing the law so made by the Legislature of the State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains what happens when a State law conflicts with a law made by Parliament on a subject in the Concurrent List.

If there is any inconsistency between the two, the law made by Parliament will prevail, and the State law will become void to the extent of the conflict.

However, if a State law on a Concurrent subject is reserved for the President and receives his assent, then that State law will prevail in that State even if it conflicts with an earlier law made by Parliament.

In addition, Parliament still has the power to make a new law later to change, amend or cancel that State law.

This means that normally the central law is stronger in case of conflict, but a State law with Presidential approval can temporarily prevail in that State.

Ex : If both Parliament and a State make different laws on education and they conflict, the central law will apply unless the State law has received Presidential assent.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Establishes the 'Doctrine of Repugnancy' for laws on the Concurrent List.
If a state law conflicts with a central law on a concurrent subject, the central law prevails.
The state law, to the extent of the conflict (repugnancy), becomes void.

Exception: If the state law was reserved for and has received the President's assent, it will prevail in that state. However, Parliament can still override this state law by enacting a later law on the same subject.
Historical_Context: This article provides a clear rule to resolve conflicts that are bound to arise in the Concurrent List, where both levels of government have legislative competence. It establishes the ultimate supremacy of Parliament in the concurrent field, a key unitary feature of the Indian Constitution.
Landmark_Cases: M. Karunanidhi vs Union of India (1979): The Supreme Court laid down the conditions for when a state law can be considered 'repugnant' to a central law.
Zaverbhai Amaidas vs State of Bombay (1954): Dealt with the issue of implied repeal and repugnancy in the concurrent field.

Title: Requirements as to recommendations and previous sanctions to be regarded as matters of procedure only
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that a law will not become invalid only because a required recommendation or previous sanction was not taken before passing it.

However, this is valid only if the final assent to the law is given by the same authority whose recommendation or sanction was required.

This means that if a procedural step was missed but the proper authority later gives approval, the law will still remain valid.

Ex : If a Bill required the Governor’s recommendation before introduction but later receives the Governor’s assent, it will not become invalid just because the earlier recommendation was not taken.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: This is a 'curing' provision.
It states that a law will not be considered invalid merely because the required prior recommendation or sanction (from the President or Governor) was not obtained.
This is valid only if the bill subsequently receives the assent of that very authority (President or Governor).
It treats the lack of prior sanction as a procedural irregularity that can be cured by subsequent assent.
Historical_Context: This article is designed to prevent laws from being struck down on purely technical or procedural grounds, especially when the authority whose prior sanction was needed has ultimately given their assent to the final Act. It prioritizes the substance of the legislative act over a procedural lapse.
Landmark_Cases: This procedural article is sometimes invoked to save legislation from being invalidated on technical grounds.
Related_Articles: Article 304 (Bills restricting trade requiring prior sanction)

Title: Obligation of States and the Union
Original_Description: The executive power of every State shall be so exercised as to ensure compliance with the laws made by Parliament and any existing laws which apply in that State, and the executive power of the Union shall extend to the giving of such directions to a State as may appear to the Government of India to be necessary for that purpose.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that every State must use its executive power in a way that follows the laws made by Parliament and any existing laws that apply in that State.

Also, the Union government has the power to give directions to a State if it feels that such directions are necessary to make sure that these laws are properly followed.

This means that States cannot ignore or act against laws made by Parliament, and the Union can guide them to ensure proper implementation.

Ex : If Parliament makes a law about environmental protection, a State government must properly implement it, and the Union can give instructions if the State is not following it correctly.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Imposes an obligation on every state government to exercise its executive power in a way that ensures compliance with laws made by Parliament.
Empowers the Union executive to give directions to a state government as necessary to ensure such compliance.
This establishes the supremacy of Union laws in the executive sphere.
Historical_Context: This article provides the constitutional basis for the Union's administrative control over the states. It ensures that state governments, which are responsible for implementing most laws (including central laws), do so effectively. Failure to comply with such directions can have serious consequences, potentially leading to the imposition of President's Rule under Article 365.
Landmark_Cases: The scope of this power and its implications for federalism were extensively discussed in S.R. Bommai vs Union of India (1994).
Related_Articles: Article 257 (Control of the Union over States in certain cases)
Article 365 (Effect of failure to comply with directions given by the Union)

Title: Control of the Union over States in certain cases
Original_Description:
(1) The executive power of every State shall be so exercised as not to impede or prejudice the exercise of the executive power of the Union, and the executive power of the Union shall extend to the giving of such directions to a State as may appear to the Government of India to be necessary for that purpose.

 

(2) The executive power of the Union shall also extend to the giving of directions to a State as to the construction and maintenance of means of communication declared in the direction to be of national or military importance:

 

   Provided that nothing in this clause shall be taken as restricting the power of Parliament to declare highways or waterways to be national highways or national waterways or the power of the Union with respect to the highways or waterways so declared or the power of the Union to construct and maintain means of communication as part of its functions with respect to naval, military and air force works.

 

(3) The executive power of the Union shall also extend to the giving of directions to a State as to the measures to be taken for the protection of the railways within the State.

 

(4) Where in carrying out any direction given to a State under clause (2) as to the construction or maintenance of any means of communication or under clause (3) as to the measures to be taken for the protection of any railway, costs have been incurred in excess of those which would have been incurred in the discharge of the normal duties of the State if such direction had not been given, there shall be paid by the Government of India to the State such sum as may be agreed, or, in default of agreement, as may be determined by an arbitrator appointed by the Chief Justice of India, in respect of the extra costs so incurred by the State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that a State must use its executive power in a way that does not create problems for the Union in using its own executive power.

Also, the Union can give directions to a State to make sure that the State’s actions do not interfere with the Union’s work.

In addition, the Union can give directions to a State about building and maintaining roads, waterways, or other means of communication if they are declared to be of national or military importance.

The Union can also give directions to a State about steps to protect railways within that State.

However, if a State spends extra money while following such directions, and this cost is more than its normal duties, the Union must pay the State the extra amount. If there is disagreement about the amount, it will be decided by an arbitrator appointed by the Chief Justice of India.

This means that national interests like defence, communication and railway safety are given special importance, and the Union can ensure proper action.

Ex : If the Union declares a highway important for national security, it can direct the State to maintain it properly and pay for any extra cost involved.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Obligates state governments not to impede or prejudice the executive power of the Union.
Empowers the Union executive to give directions to states in specific matters:
Construction and maintenance of communications of national or military importance.
Measures for the protection of railways within the state.
The Union is required to pay for the extra costs incurred by the state in carrying out these directions.
Historical_Context: This article further elaborates on the administrative control of the Union over the states. It is aimed at ensuring that national interests, particularly in strategic areas like communications and railways, are not hampered by the actions or inactions of state governments.
Landmark_Cases: This article defines specific areas of Union's administrative control over states.
Related_Articles: Article 256 (Obligation of States and the Union)
Article 365 (Effect of failure to comply with directions)

Title: Power of the Union to confer powers, etc, on States in certain cases
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, the President may, with the consent of the Government of a State, entrust either conditionally or unconditionally to that Government or to its officers functions in relation to any matter to which the executive power of the Union extends.

 

(2) A law made by Parliament which applies in any State may, notwithstanding that it relates to a matter with respect to which the Legislature of the State has no power to make laws, confer powers and impose duties, or authorise the conferring of powers and the imposition of duties, upon the State or officers and authorities thereof.

 

(3) Where by virtue of this article powers and duties have been conferred or imposed upon a State or officers or authorities thereof, there shall be paid by the Government of India to the State such sum as may be agreed, or, in default of agreement, as may be determined by an arbitrator appointed by the Chief Justice of India, in respect of any extra costs of administration incurred by the State in connection with the exercise of those powers and duties.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can give certain executive functions of the Union to a State government or its officers.

However, this can be done only with the consent of that State government, and it may be done with or without conditions.

Also, Parliament can make a law that gives powers or duties to a State government or its officers, even if the State itself does not have the power to make laws on that subject.

In addition, if a State spends extra money while carrying out these Union functions, the Government of India must pay the State for the extra cost. If there is no agreement on the amount, it will be decided by an arbitrator appointed by the Chief Justice of India.

This means that the Union can use the State machinery to carry out its work in an efficient way.

Ex : The Union may ask State officers to implement a central scheme, and if the State spends extra money for this work, the Union will compensate it.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Allows for the delegation of the Union's executive functions to a state government.
Delegation by President: The President can entrust Union functions to a state government, but only with the consent of that state government.
Delegation by Parliament: A law made by Parliament can confer powers and impose duties on a state government or its officers, even without the state's consent.
When functions are delegated, the Union must pay the state for any extra costs of administration.
Historical_Context: This article facilitates cooperative federalism and administrative efficiency. It allows the central government to use the administrative machinery of the states to implement its own functions, which is often more practical and effective. It provides for both consensual and statutory delegation of powers.
Landmark_Cases: This article provides the framework for administrative delegation between the Union and states.
Related_Articles: Article 258A (Power of the States to entrust functions to the Union)

Title: Power of the States to entrust functions to the Union
Original_Description: Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, the Governor of a State may, with the consent of the Government of India, entrust either conditionally or unconditionally to that Government or to its officers functions in relation to any matter to which the executive power of the State extends.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that a State government can give some of its executive functions to the Union government.

However, this can be done only with the consent of the Government of India, and it may be done with or without conditions.

This means that just like the Union can give work to a State, a State can also request the Union to handle certain executive functions on its behalf.

Ex : If a State feels that the Union is better equipped to handle a particular administrative task, it can entrust that task to the Union with its consent.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Allows a state government to delegate its executive functions to the Union government.
This can only be done with the consent of the Government of India.
The delegation is done by the Governor.
This is the reverse of the delegation provided for in Article 258.
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 7th Amendment Act, 1956. It creates a reciprocal arrangement for the delegation of functions, allowing states to entrust their executive powers to the Centre when it is administratively convenient or necessary. This further promotes inter-governmental cooperation.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article facilitating cooperative federalism.
Related_Articles: Article 258 (Power of the Union to confer powers on States)

Title: Jurisdiction of the Union in relation to territories outside India
Original_Description: The Government of India may by agreement with the Government of any territory not being part of the territory of India undertake any executive, legislative or judicial functions vested in the Government of such territory, but every such agreement shall be subject to, and governed by, any law relating to the exercise of foreign jurisdiction for the time being in force.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the Government of India can make an agreement with the government of a territory outside India to perform certain executive, legislative or judicial functions for that territory.

However, such an agreement must follow the laws in force about exercising authority outside India.

This means that India can help administer a foreign territory if there is a proper agreement and legal basis.

Ex : If a small foreign territory asks India to manage certain administrative functions through an agreement, India can do so according to law.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Empowers the Government of India to undertake executive, legislative, or judicial functions of a foreign territory.
This can only be done through an agreement with the government of that territory.
Any such exercise of power is governed by laws related to foreign jurisdiction.
Historical_Context: This article was designed to provide a legal framework for India to administer territories outside its borders by agreement, possibly in a protectorate or trust capacity. It is based on the British Foreign Jurisdiction Act and has not been significantly used in practice.
Landmark_Cases: This is an enabling provision related to foreign relations.

Title: Public acts, records and judicial proceedings
Original_Description:
(1) Full faith and credit shall be given throughout the territory of India to public acts, records and judicial proceedings of the Union and of every State.

 

(2) The manner in which and the conditions under which the acts, records and proceedings referred to in clause (1) shall be proved and the effect thereof determined shall be as provided by law made by Parliament.

 

(3) Final judgments or orders delivered or passed by civil courts in any part of the territory of India shall be capable of execution anywhere within that territory according to law.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that public acts, official records and court decisions of the Union and of every State must be respected throughout India.

Also, Parliament can make laws to decide how such acts, records and court decisions should be proved and what effect they will have.

In addition, final judgments or orders given by civil courts in any part of India can be executed anywhere else in India according to law.

This means that a legal decision given in one State is valid and enforceable in another State.

Ex : If a civil court in Delhi passes a final order, that order can be enforced in Maharashtra without starting a new case.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: This is the 'Full Faith and Credit' clause of the Constitution.
It mandates that public acts (like laws), records, and judicial proceedings of the Centre and every state must be given full faith and credit throughout India.
Final judgments of civil courts in any part of India are executable anywhere else in the country.
Parliament can make laws to prescribe the manner in which these acts and records are to be proved and their effect determined.
Historical_Context: This provision is crucial for the unity and integrity of the nation's legal and administrative framework. It ensures that a person does not have to re-litigate a civil case in another state and that the official acts and records of one state are respected in another, facilitating inter-state commerce and movement.
Landmark_Cases: This principle is fundamental to the execution of decrees and the recognition of official documents across state borders.
Related_Articles: This article promotes judicial and administrative unity across states.

Title: Adjudication of disputes relating to waters of interState rivers or river valleys
Original_Description:
(1) Parliament may by law provide for the adjudication of any dispute or complaint with respect to the use, distribution or control of the waters of, or in, any inter-State river or river valley.

 

(2) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, Parliament may by law provide that neither the Supreme Court nor any other court shall exercise jurisdiction in respect of any such dispute or complaint as is referred to in clause (1).
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament can make a law to decide disputes related to the use, distribution or control of water from rivers that flow through more than one State.

Also, Parliament can provide by law that such disputes will not be decided by the Supreme Court or any other court.

This means that special arrangements can be made to settle river water disputes between States.

Ex : If two States disagree about sharing water from a river that flows through both of them, Parliament can create a special body to resolve the dispute instead of regular courts handling it.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to create a mechanism for adjudicating inter-state river water disputes.
Allows Parliament to exclude the jurisdiction of all courts, including the Supreme Court, over such disputes.
This power is an exception to the Supreme Court's original jurisdiction under Article 131.
Historical_Context: The framers recognized that inter-state river water disputes are highly technical and politically sensitive, and may not be suitable for regular court proceedings. This article allows for the creation of specialized expert tribunals. Under this article, Parliament has enacted the Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956, and several tribunals (e.g., for Cauvery, Narmada, Krishna rivers) have been constituted.
Landmark_Cases: State of Karnataka vs Union of India & Anr (2017) (Cauvery Dispute): The Supreme Court clarified that while it cannot act as a tribunal itself, its power of judicial review under Article 136 to hear appeals from the tribunal's award is not completely ousted. It can intervene to ensure the tribunal acts within the law.
Related_Articles: Article 131 (Original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court)

Title: Provisions with respect to an inter-State Council
Original_Description:
If at any time it appears to the President that the public interests would be served by the establishment of a Council charged with the duty of —

 

(a) inquiring into and advising upon disputes which may have arisen between States;

 

(b) investigating and discussing subjects in which some or all of the States, or the Union and one or more of the States, have a common interest; or

 

(c) making recommendations upon any such subject and, in particular, recommendations for the better co-ordination of policy and action with respect to that subject, it shall be lawful for the President by order to establish such a Council, and to define the nature of the duties to be performed by it and its organisation and procedure.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can establish a Council if it is considered useful for public interest.

This Council can examine and give advice on disputes between States.

Also, it can discuss subjects in which the Union and one or more States, or several States together, have a common interest.

In addition, it can make recommendations for better coordination of policies and actions on such matters.

The President can also decide the duties, structure and working procedure of this Council.

This means that there can be a special body to promote cooperation and discussion between the Union and the States.

Ex : If several States have a common issue like transport policy, such a Council can discuss it and suggest coordinated solutions.
Subject: Relation between Union & States
Key_Points: Empowers the President to establish an Inter-State Council.
The Council's role is to inquire into and advise upon disputes between states, investigate subjects of common interest, and make recommendations for better policy coordination.
The Council is an advisory body to promote cooperative federalism.
Historical_Context: Although the provision existed since the inception of the Constitution, the Inter-State Council was established for the first time only in 1990 on the recommendation of the Sarkaria Commission on Centre-State Relations. It is a forum for dialogue and discussion between the Centre and the states, headed by the Prime Minister.
Landmark_Cases: This article provides the constitutional basis for a key institution of cooperative federalism.

Title: Interpretation
Original_Description: In this Part, “Finance Commission” means a Finance Commission constituted under article 280.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that in this Part of the Constitution, the term Finance Commission means the Finance Commission that is created under Article 280.

This means that whenever the words Finance Commission are used in this Part, they refer only to the Commission set up under that specific article.

Ex : If this Part mentions the Finance Commission while discussing tax distribution, it means the body formed under Article 280.
Subject: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
Key_Points: This is a definitional article for Part XII.
It clarifies that any reference to the 'Finance Commission' in this Part refers to the one constituted under Article 280.
Historical_Context: This article serves as an introductory definition for Part XII, which deals with the complex financial relations between the Union and the States, a core aspect of fiscal federalism.
Landmark_Cases: This is a purely definitional article.

Title: Taxes not to be imposed save by authority of law
Original_Description: No tax shall be levied or collected except by authority of law.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that no tax can be imposed or collected unless there is a law that allows it.

This means that the government cannot collect money as tax on its own without passing a proper law through the legislature.

Only a valid law made by Parliament or a State Legislature can authorize the collection of tax.

Ex : The government cannot suddenly start collecting a new tax from citizens unless a law has been properly passed to allow it.
Subject: Finance, Property,Contracts and Suits
Key_Points: Establishes a fundamental principle of the rule of law in fiscal matters.
No tax can be levied or collected without the authority of a validly enacted law.
This means the executive cannot impose taxes on its own; it requires the sanction of the legislature (Parliament or a State Legislature).
Historical_Context: This article is a bulwark against arbitrary taxation, a principle derived from the English Magna Carta ('no taxation without representation'). While it is not a Fundamental Right, it is a crucial constitutional right that protects citizens and businesses from arbitrary financial demands by the government.
Landmark_Cases: This principle is the foundation of all tax law and litigation in India. Any tax imposed through a government notification or rule without a clear legislative backing can be challenged under this article.

Title: Consolidated Funds and public accounts of India and of the States
Original_Description:
(1) Subject to the provisions of article 267 and to the provisions of this Chapter with respect to the assignment of the whole or part of the net proceeds of certain taxes and duties to States, all revenues received by the Government of India, all loans raised by that Government by the issue of treasury bills, loans or ways and means advances and all moneys received by that Government in repayment of loans shall form one consolidated fund to be entitled “the Consolidated Fund of India”, and all revenues received by the Government of a State, all loans raised by that Government by the issue of treasury bills, loans or ways and means advances and all moneys received by that Government in repayment of loans shall form one consolidated fund to be entitled “the Consolidated Fund of the State”.

 

(2) All other public moneys received by or on behalf of the Government of India or the Government of a State shall be credited to the public account of India or the public account of the State, as the case may be.

 

(3) No moneys out of the Consolidated Fund of India or the Consolidated Fund of a State shall be appropriated except in accordance with law and for the purposes and in the manner provided in this Constitution.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that all revenues received by the Government of India, all loans raised by it, and all money received in repayment of loans will form one fund called the Consolidated Fund of India.

Similarly, all revenues received by a State government, all loans raised by it, and all money received in repayment of loans will form the Consolidated Fund of that State.

Also, all other public money received by or on behalf of the Government of India or a State government will be placed in the Public Account of India or of the State.

However, no money can be taken out of the Consolidated Fund of India or of a State unless it is allowed by law and used in the manner provided in the Constitution.

This means that government money is kept in special funds and cannot be spent without legal approval.

Ex : Before the government spends tax money on building roads, it must get approval through a law that allows withdrawal from the Consolidated Fund.
Subject: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
Key_Points: Creates the Consolidated Fund of India and the Consolidated Fund of each State.
All government revenues (like taxes) and loans are credited to this fund.
No money can be withdrawn from this fund except through an 'appropriation made by law' (i.e., after passing the Appropriation Bill in the legislature).
Also creates the Public Account of India and of each State, where other public moneys (like provident fund deposits, small savings) are held.
Withdrawals from the Public Account do not require parliamentary appropriation.
Historical_Context: This article establishes the primary accounts for all government transactions and reinforces legislative control over public money. The distinction between the Consolidated Fund and the Public Account is crucial; the government acts as a banker for money in the Public Account and must repay it, whereas money in the Consolidated Fund is government revenue.
Landmark_Cases: This is a fundamental article governing public finance.

Title: Contingency Fund
Original_Description:
(1) Parliament may by law establish a Contingency Fund in the nature of an imprest to be entitled “the Contingency Fund of India” into which shall be paid from time to time such sums as may be determined by such law, and the said Fund shall be placed at the disposal of the President to enable advances to be made by him out of such Fund for the purposes of meeting unforeseen expenditure pending authorisation of such expenditure by Parliament by law under article 115 or article 116.

 

(2) The Legislature of a State may by law establish a Contingency Fund in the nature of an imprest to be entitled “the Contingency Fund of the State” into which shall be paid from time to time such sums as may be determined by such law, and the said Fund shall be placed at the disposal of the Governor of the State to enable advances to be made by him out of such Fund for the purposes of meeting unforeseen expenditure pending authorisation of such expenditure by the Legislature of the State by law under article 205 or article 206.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament can create a Contingency Fund of India for emergency or unexpected expenses.

This fund is placed at the disposal of the President so that money can be advanced to meet urgent expenses before Parliament gives formal approval.

Similarly, a State Legislature can create a Contingency Fund of the State, which is placed at the disposal of the Governor for urgent expenses.

However, any money taken from this fund must later be approved by the legislature and returned to the fund.

This means that the government can quickly handle emergencies but must later get proper approval.

Ex : If there is a sudden natural disaster, money can be taken from the Contingency Fund to provide immediate relief before formal approval is obtained.
Subject: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
Key_Points: Allows Parliament to establish a Contingency Fund of India, which is placed at the disposal of the President.
Similarly, allows State Legislatures to establish a Contingency Fund of the State, placed at the disposal of the Governor.
This fund is used to meet unforeseen or urgent expenditure.
Any expenditure from this fund must subsequently be approved by the legislature, and the amount withdrawn must be returned to the fund.
Historical_Context: The Contingency Fund acts as an imprest account for the executive to deal with emergencies and natural disasters when the legislature is not in session to approve the expenditure. It provides the necessary financial flexibility for urgent situations while ensuring eventual legislative oversight.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article related to public finance.

Title: Duties levied by the Union but collected and appropriated by the States
Original_Description:
(1) Such stamp duties and such duties of excise on medicinal and toilet preparations as are mentioned in the Union List shall be levied by the Government of India but shall be collected,—

 

(a) in the case where such duties are leviable within any Union territory, by the Government of India, and

 

(b) in other cases, by the States within which such duties are respectively leviable.

 

(2) The proceeds in any financial year of any such duty leviable within any State shall not form part of the Consolidated Fund of India, but shall be assigned to that State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that certain duties are levied by the Government of India but are collected by the States in most cases.

If such duties are collected within a Union territory, they are collected by the Government of India. In other cases, they are collected by the State where they are applicable.

Also, the money collected from these duties in a State does not go to the Consolidated Fund of India. Instead, it is assigned to that State.

This means that although the Union has the power to levy these duties, the revenue goes to the States.

Ex : If a stamp duty covered under this rule is collected in a State, that State keeps the money instead of sending it to the Centre.
Subject: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
Key_Points: Deals with taxes that are levied by the Union but collected and kept by the states.
Examples include stamp duties on bills of exchange, cheques, etc., and excise duties on medicinal and toilet preparations mentioned in the Union List.
The revenue from these duties goes directly to the state where they are collected and does not form part of the Consolidated Fund of India.
Historical_Context: This article is part of the scheme of distribution of financial resources between the Centre and the states. It assigns certain revenue sources to the states for their administrative convenience, even though the power to legislate and fix the rate of the tax lies with the Centre. Note: The relevance of this article has changed significantly after the introduction of GST.
Landmark_Cases: This is a fiscal federalism article.

Title: Service tax levied by Union and collected and appropriated by the Union and the States
Original_Description:
(1) Taxes on services shall be levied by the Government of India and such tax shall be collected and appropriated by the Government of India and the States in the manner provided in clause (2).

 

(2) The proceeds in any financial year of any such tax levied in accordance with the provisions of clause (1) shall be —

 

(a) collected by the Government of India and the States;

 

(b) appropriated by the Government of India and the States,

 

in accordance with such principles of collection and appropriation as may be formulated by Parliament by law.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that taxes on services were to be levied by the Government of India and collected and shared between the Union and the States according to law made by Parliament.

However, this article has been repealed and is no longer in force because service tax has been included under the goods and services tax system.

This means that the earlier system for service tax is replaced by the new GST system.

Ex : Earlier, service tax on services like consulting was governed under this rule, but now it is covered under GST.
Subject: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
Key_Points: This article, which dealt with Service Tax, has been repealed.
It provided for service tax to be levied by the Union and collected and appropriated by both the Union and the States.
Service tax has been subsumed into the Goods and Services Tax (GST).
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 88th Amendment Act, 2003, to provide a constitutional basis for the levy of tax on services. It remained inoperative for a long time. With the introduction of GST, which covers both goods and services, this article became redundant and was repealed by the 101st Amendment Act, 2016.
Landmark_Cases: Not applicable as the article is repealed.

Title: Taxes levied and collected by the Union but assigned to the States
Original_Description:
(1) Taxes on the sale or purchase of goods and taxes on the consignment of goods shall be levied and collected by the Government of India but shall be assigned and shall be deemed to have been assigned to the States on or after the 1st day of April, 1996 in the manner provided in clause (2).

 

Explanation. — For the purposes of this clause, —

 

(a) the expression “taxes on the sale or purchase of goods” shall mean taxes on sale or purchase of goods other than newspapers, where such sale or purchase takes place in the course of inter-State trade or commerce;

 

(b) the expression “taxes on the consignment of goods” shall mean taxes on the consignment of goods (whether the consignment is to the person making it or to any other person), where such consignment takes place in the course of inter-State trade or commerce.
