Historical_Context: This article establishes the role of the Union as a guardian of the states and the constitutional order. While seemingly protective, the duty to ensure that the state government is functioning according to the Constitution is the basis on which the controversial power of President's Rule (Article 356) is invoked.
Landmark_Cases: S.R. Bommai vs Union of India (1994): The Supreme Court extensively discussed this article as providing the rationale for the imposition of President's Rule, while also laying down strict limits to prevent its misuse.

Title: Provisions in case of failure of constitutional machinery in States
Original_Description:
(1) If the President, on receipt of a report from the Governor of a State or otherwise, is satisfied that a situation has arisen in which the Government of the State cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of this Constitution, the President may by Proclamation —

 

(a) assume to himself all or any of the functions of the Government of the State and all or any of the powers vested in or exercisable by the Governor or any body or authority in the State other than the Legislature of the State;

 

(b) declare that the powers of the Legislature of the State shall be exercisable by or under the authority of Parliament;

 

(c) make such incidental and consequential provisions as appear to the President to be necessary or desirable for giving effect to the objects of the Proclamation, including provisions for suspending in whole or in part the operation of any provisions of this Constitution relating to any body or authority in the State:

 

   Provided that nothing in this clause shall authorise the President to assume to himself any of the powers vested in or exercisable by a High Court, or to suspend in whole or in part the operation of any provision of this Constitution relating to High Courts.

 

(2) Any such Proclamation may be revoked or varied by a subsequent Proclamation.

 

(3) Every Proclamation under this article shall be laid before each House of Parliament and shall, except where it is a Proclamation revoking a previous Proclamation, cease to operate at the expiration of two months unless before the expiration of that period it has been approved by resolutions of both Houses of Parliament:

 

   Provided that if any such Proclamation (not being a Proclamation revoking a previous Proclamation) is issued at a time when the House of the People is dissolved or the dissolution of the House of the People takes place during the period of two months referred to in this clause, and if a resolution approving the Proclamation has been passed by the Council of States, but no resolution with respect to such Proclamation has been passed by the House of the People before the expiration of that period, the Proclamation shall cease to operate at the expiration of thirty days from the date on which the House of the People first sits after its reconstitution unless before the expiration of the said period of thirty days a resolution approving the Proclamation has been also passed by the House of the People.

 

(4) A Proclamation so approved shall, unless revoked, cease to operate on the expiration of a period of six months from the date of issue of the Proclamation:

 

   Provided that if and so often as a resolution approving the continuance in force of such a Proclamation is passed by both Houses of Parliament, the Proclamation shall, unless revoked, continue in force for a further period of six months from the date on which under this clause it would otherwise have ceased to operate, but no such Proclamation shall in any case remain in force for more than three years:

 

   Provided further that if the dissolution of the House of the People takes place during any such period of six months and a resolution approving the continuance in force of such Proclamation has been passed by the Council of States, but no resolution with respect to the continuance in force of such Proclamation has been passed by the House of the People during the said period, the Proclamation shall cease to operate at the expiration of thirty days from the date on which the House of the People first sits after its reconstitution unless before the expiration of the said period of thirty days a resolution approving the continuance in force of the Proclamation has been also passed by the House of the People:

 

   Provided also that in the case of the Proclamation issued under clause (1) on the 11th day of May, 1987 with respect to the State of Punjab, the reference in the first proviso to this clause to “three years” shall be construed as a reference to five years.

 

(5) Notwithstanding anything contained in clause (4), a resolution with respect to the continuance in force of a Proclamation approved under clause (3) for any period beyond the expiration of one year from the date of issue of such Proclamation shall not be passed by either House of Parliament unless —

 

(a) a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, in the whole of India or, as the case may be, in the whole or any part of the State, at the time of the passing of such resolution, and

 

(b) the Election Commission certifies that the continuance in force of the Proclamation approved under clause (3) during the period specified in such resolution is necessary on account of difficulties in holding general elections to the Legislative Assembly of the State concerned:

 

   Provided that nothing in this clause shall apply to the Proclamation issued under clause (1) on the 11th day of May, 1987 with respect to the State of Punjab.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can impose President's Rule in a State if the government of that State cannot function according to the Constitution.

This can be done based on a report from the Governor or other information.

Under President's Rule, the President takes over the executive functions of the State, and Parliament takes over the legislative powers.

The State Assembly may be suspended or dissolved.

The Proclamation must be approved by both Houses of Parliament within two months.

It can continue for six months at a time, but generally not beyond three years.

After one year, it can continue only if a National Emergency is in force and the Election Commission certifies that elections cannot be held.

Ex : President's Rule has been imposed in several States when no party was able to form a stable government.
Subject: Emergency Provisions
Key_Points: Empowers the President to impose 'President's Rule' in a state.
This can be done if the President is satisfied that there is a failure of the constitutional machinery in the state.
The state government is dismissed, and the State Assembly is either suspended or dissolved.
The executive power of the state is assumed by the President, and the legislative power by Parliament.
The proclamation must be approved by Parliament within two months.
It can last for a maximum of three years with parliamentary approval every six months.
Historical_Context: This is arguably the most controversial article of the Constitution. Intended as a last resort to restore constitutional order in a state, it has been widely criticized for its frequent misuse by central governments to dismiss politically inconvenient state governments. The scope of this article was significantly curtailed by the Supreme Court's Bommai judgment.
Landmark_Cases: State of Rajasthan vs Union of India (1977): The Court initially took a restrictive view on judicial review of this power.

S.R. Bommai vs Union of India (1994): A landmark judgment that established that the imposition of President's Rule is subject to judicial review. It held that the majority of a government should be tested on the floor of the House and that the power cannot be used for mala fide reasons.
Related_Articles: Article 355 (Duty of the Union to protect States)
Article 365 (Effect of failure to comply with directions)

Title: Exercise of legislative powers under Proclamation issued under article 356
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how laws are made for a State during President's Rule.

When Parliament takes over the legislative powers of a State under Article 356, it can make laws for that State.

Parliament can also give this law-making power to the President, who may further delegate it to another authority.

The President can also allow expenditure from the State’s Consolidated Fund when the Lok Sabha is not in session.

Laws made during President's Rule will continue to remain valid even after the Rule ends, until the State Legislature changes or repeals them.

Ex : A law made by Parliament for a State during President's Rule will remain in force even after an elected government returns.
Subject: Emergency Provisions
Key_Points: Specifies how the legislative power of a state is exercised during President's Rule.
Parliament can make laws for the state.
Parliament can delegate this law-making power to the President or any other authority specified by it.
Laws made by Parliament or the President for the state during this period are known as 'President's Acts'.
These laws continue to be in force even after President's Rule ends, until they are repealed or altered by the state legislature.
Historical_Context: This article provides the mechanism for the Union to legislate for a state when its own legislature is not functioning. The delegation of this power to the President is for administrative convenience, allowing laws to be made for the state even when Parliament is not in session.
Landmark_Cases: This is a procedural article detailing legislative functions during President's Rule.

Title: Suspension of provisions of article 19 during emergencies
Original_Description:
(1) While a Proclamation of Emergency declaring that the security of India or any part of the territory thereof is threatened by war or by external aggression is in operation, nothing in article 19 shall restrict the power of the State as defined in Part III to make any law or to take any executive action which the State would but for the provisions contained in that Part be competent to make or to take, but any law so made shall, to the extent of the incompetency, cease to have effect as soon as the Proclamation ceases to operate, except as respects things done or omitted to be done before the law so ceases to have effect:

 

   Provided that where such Proclamation of Emergency is in operation only in any part of the territory of India, any such law may be made, or any such executive action may be taken, under this article in relation to or in any State or Union territory in which or in any part of which the Proclamation of Emergency is not in operation, if and in so far as the security of India or any part of the territory thereof is threatened by activities in or in relation to the part of the territory of India in which the Proclamation of Emergency is in operation.

 

(2) Nothing in clause (1) shall apply —

 

(a) to any law which does not contain a recital to the effect that such law is in relation to the Proclamation of Emergency in operation when it is made; or

 

(b) to any executive action taken otherwise than under a law containing such a recital.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that when a National Emergency is declared due to war or external aggression, the rights under Article 19 are automatically suspended.

During this period, the government can make laws or take actions that may limit the freedoms under Article 19.

However, this automatic suspension does not apply if the emergency is declared on the ground of armed rebellion.

Any law made under this provision will stop having effect once the emergency ends, except for actions already taken.

Ex : During a war emergency, restrictions may be placed on freedom of speech in the interest of national security.
Subject: Emergency Provisions
Key_Points: Provides for the automatic suspension of the six fundamental freedoms under Article 19 when a National Emergency is declared.
This automatic suspension happens only when the emergency is declared on the grounds of 'war' or 'external aggression', not 'armed rebellion'.
During this period, the state can make any law or take any executive action that abridges the rights under Article 19.
Such laws become void as soon as the emergency ceases to operate.
Historical_Context: The 44th Amendment Act, 1978, introduced significant changes to this article. Before the amendment, Article 19 was suspended even during an emergency declared on grounds of 'internal disturbance'. The amendment restricted the automatic suspension only to emergencies arising from war or external aggression, providing a crucial safeguard against the misuse of emergency powers to suppress internal dissent.
Landmark_Cases: The interpretation of this article was central to cases during the 1962 and 1971 emergencies.
Related_Articles: Article 19 (Protection of certain rights regarding freedom of speech, etc)
Article 359 (Suspension of the enforcement of other rights)

Title: Suspension of the enforcement of the rights conferred by Part III during emergencies
Original_Description:
(1) Where a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, the President may by order declare that the right to move any court for the enforcement of such of the rights conferred by Part III (except articles 20 and 21) as may be mentioned in the order and all proceedings pending in any court for the enforcement of the rights so mentioned shall remain suspended for the period during which the Proclamation is in force or for such shorter period as may be specified in the order.

 

(1A) While an order made under clause (1) mentioning any of the rights conferred by Part III (except articles 20 and 21) is in operation, nothing in that Part conferring those rights shall restrict the power of the State as defined in the said Part to make any law or to take any executive action which the State would but for the provisions contained in that Part be competent to make or to take, but any law so made shall, to the extent of the incompetency, cease to have effect as soon as the order aforesaid ceases to operate, except as respects things done or omitted to be done before the law so ceases to have effect:

 

   Provided that where a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation only in any part of the territory of India, any such law may be made, or any such executive action may be taken, under this article in relation to or in any State or Union territory in which or in any part of which the Proclamation of Emergency is not in operation, if and in so far as the security of India or any part of the territory thereof is threatened by activities in or in relation to the part of the territory of India in which the Proclamation of Emergency is in operation.

 

(1B) Nothing in clause (1A) shall apply —

 

(a) to any law which does not contain a recital to the effect that such law is in relation to the Proclamation of Emergency in operation when it is made; or

 

(b) to any executive action taken otherwise than under a law containing such a recital.(

 

(2) An order made as aforesaid may extend to the whole or any part of the territory of India:

 

   Provided that where a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation only in a part of the territory of India, any such order shall not extend to any other part of the territory of India unless the President, being satisfied that the security of India or any part of the territory thereof is threatened by activities in or in relation to the part of the territory of India in which the Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, considers such extension to be necessary. 

 

(3) Every order made under clause (1) shall, as soon as may be after it is made, be laid before each House of Parliament.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that during a National Emergency, the President can suspend the right of people to approach courts for enforcement of certain Fundamental Rights.

This suspension is done through a special Presidential Order.

However, the rights under Articles 20 and 21 cannot be suspended under any circumstances.

The suspension applies only to the enforcement of rights, not to the rights themselves.

The order must be placed before Parliament.

Ex : During an emergency, a person may not be able to approach the court to enforce certain Fundamental Rights if their enforcement has been suspended by Presidential Order.
Subject: Emergency Provisions
Key_Points: Authorizes the President to suspend the right to move any court for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights during a National Emergency.
Unlike Article 358, this is not an automatic suspension; it requires a specific Presidential Order.
The President can specify which Fundamental Rights (their enforcement) are to be suspended.
The rights under Articles 20 and 21 (protection in respect of conviction and right to life and personal liberty) can never be suspended.
The suspension applies only to the enforcement of the rights, not the rights themselves.
Historical_Context: This article gives the executive wide powers to curtail fundamental rights during an emergency. The infamous ADM Jabalpur case during the 1975 Emergency saw the Supreme Court rule that even the right to life could be suspended. This was corrected by the 44th Amendment Act, 1978, which explicitly protected Articles 20 and 21 from being suspended under any circumstances, a landmark safeguard for individual liberty.
Landmark_Cases: ADM Jabalpur vs Shivkant Shukla (1976) (The Habeas Corpus case): A now-overruled judgment where the Supreme Court held that during an emergency, the right to move a court for habeas corpus against illegal detention was suspended.
The 44th Amendment was a direct legislative response to overturn the effect of this judgment.

Title: Provisions as to financial emergency
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can declare a Financial Emergency if the financial stability or credit of India or any part of it is threatened.

The Proclamation must be approved by both Houses of Parliament within two months.

During a Financial Emergency, the Union can give financial directions to States.

The President can order reduction of salaries and allowances of government employees, including judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.

Money Bills passed by State Legislatures may be required to be reserved for the President’s consideration.

This provision has never been used so far.

Ex : If India faces a severe financial crisis, a Financial Emergency can be declared to control government spending.
Subject: Emergency Provisions
Key_Points: Empowers the President to declare a Financial Emergency.
This can be done if the financial stability or credit of India (or any part of it) is threatened.
The proclamation must be approved by Parliament with a simple majority within two months.
During a financial emergency, the Union can give financial directions to the states.
The President can direct the reduction of salaries and allowances of all government employees, including judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.
Historical_Context: This article was included as a measure to deal with a severe national economic crisis. It grants the central government sweeping powers to enforce fiscal discipline across the country. To date, a financial emergency has never been declared in India.
Landmark_Cases: As this article has never been invoked, there is no case law on it.

Title: Protection of President and Governors and Rajpramukhs
Original_Description:
(1) The President, or the Governor or Rajpramukh of a State, shall not be answerable to any court for the exercise and performance of the powers and duties of his office or for any act done or purporting to be done by him in the exercise and performance of those powers and duties:

 

   Provided that the conduct of the President may be brought under review by any court, tribunal or body appointed or designated by either House of Parliament for the investigation of a charge under article 61:

 

   Provided further that nothing in this clause shall be construed as restricting the right of any person to bring appropriate proceedings against the Government of India or the Government of a State.

 

(2) No criminal proceedings whatsoever shall be instituted or continued against the President, or the Governor of a State, in any court during his term of office.

 

(3) No process for the arrest or imprisonment of the President, or the Governor of a State, shall issue from any court during his term of office.

 

(4) No civil proceedings in which relief is claimed against the President, or the Governor of a State, shall be instituted during his term of office in any court in respect of any act done or purporting to be done by him in his personal capacity, whether before or after he entered upon his office as President, or as Governor of such State, until the expiration of two months next after notice in writing has been delivered to the President or the Governor, as the case may be, or left at his office stating the nature of the proceedings, the cause of action therefor, the name, description and place of residence of the party by whom such proceedings are to be instituted and the relief which he claims.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President of India and the Governor of a State cannot be taken to court for actions they perform while carrying out their official duties. They are not personally answerable in any court for decisions made as part of their official powers and responsibilities.

However, the conduct of the President can still be examined if Parliament starts a formal process to investigate charges under the Constitution. This means that while regular courts cannot question official acts, Parliament can review the President’s conduct in a special situation.

Also, this protection does not stop a person from filing a case against the Government of India or a State Government. This means that even if the President or Governor is protected personally, the government itself can still be taken to court.

In addition, no criminal case can be started or continued against the President or a Governor while they are in office.

Also, no court can order their arrest or imprisonment during their time in office.

However, if someone wants to file a civil case against the President or a Governor for something done in their personal capacity, they must first give a written notice. This notice must be given two months before filing the case and must clearly explain the reason for the case and the relief being asked for.

This means that the highest offices are protected during their term so they can work without fear of legal pressure.

Ex : If a Governor makes an official decision as part of government work, no one can file a criminal case against him while he is in office.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Provides personal immunity to the President and Governors from legal proceedings.
They are not answerable to any court for the exercise of their official powers and duties.
Complete immunity from criminal proceedings during their term of office.
No process for arrest or imprisonment can be issued against them during their term.
Civil proceedings can be instituted against them for personal acts only after giving two months' prior notice.
Historical_Context: This article is designed to protect the dignity and independence of the highest constitutional offices of the Union and the States. It ensures that the President and Governors can perform their official duties without fear of politically motivated or vexatious litigation. The immunity is for their term of office and does not apply to their actions after they demit office.
Landmark_Cases: The scope of this immunity has been tested in courts, which have held that while the President/Governor is personally immune, their actions can be challenged and the government can be held responsible for them.
Related_Articles: Article 14 (Equality before law - this is an exception to it)

Title: Protection of publication of proceedings of Parliament and State Legislatures
Original_Description:
(1) No person shall be liable to any proceedings, civil or criminal, in any court in respect of the publication in a newspaper of a substantially true report of any proceedings of either House of Parliament or the Legislative Assembly, or, as the case may be, either House of the Legislature, of a State, unless the publication is proved to have been made with malice:

 

   Provided that nothing in this clause shall apply to the publication of any report of the proceedings of a secret sitting of either House of Parliament or the Legislative Assembly, or, as the case may be, either House of the Legislature, of a State.

 

(2) Clause (1) shall apply in relation to reports or matters broadcast by means of wireless telegraphy as part of any programme or service provided by means of a broadcasting station as it applies in relation to reports or matters published in a newspaper.

 

   Explanation. — In this article, “newspaper” includes a news agency report containing material for publication in a newspaper .
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that a person cannot be punished in a civil or criminal court for publishing a true report of what happened in Parliament or in a State Legislature.

Also, this protection applies only if the report is mostly true and is not published with bad intention.

However, this protection does not apply if the meeting was a secret sitting. If the proceedings were secret, then publishing them will not be protected.

In addition, this rule applies not only to newspapers but also to reports broadcast on radio or similar communication systems.

This means that media and people can report what happens in legislative meetings without fear, as long as they report honestly.

Ex : If a newspaper prints a correct report of a debate in Parliament, it cannot be punished for doing so.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Provides legal immunity to persons (including the media) for publishing a 'substantially true report' of the proceedings of Parliament or a State Legislature.
This protection covers both civil and criminal proceedings (e.g., defamation).
The immunity does not apply if the publication is proved to have been made with malice.
It also does not apply to the publication of proceedings of a secret sitting.
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 44th Amendment Act, 1978. It gives constitutional protection to media freedom in reporting on legislative proceedings, which is essential for transparency and public accountability. It was enacted to undo the restrictions placed on the media during the Emergency.
Landmark_Cases: This article is a key safeguard for freedom of the press.

Title: Disqualification for appointment on remunerative political post
Original_Description:
A member of a House belonging to any political party who is disqualified for being a member of the House under paragraph 2 of the Tenth Schedule shall also be disqualified to hold any remunerative political post for duration of the period commencing from the date of his disqualification till the date on which the term of his office as such member would expire or till the date on which he contests an election to a House and is declared elected, whichever is earlier.

 

Explanation. — For the purposes of this article, —

 

(a) the expression “House” has the meaning assigned to it in clause (a) of paragraph 1 of the Tenth Schedule; 

 

(b) the expression “remunerative political post” means any office —

 

(i) under the Government of India or the Government of a State where the salary or remuneration for such office is paid out of the public revenue of the Government of India or the Government of the State, as the case may be; or

 

(ii) under a body, whether incorporated or not, which is wholly or partially owned by the Government of India or the Government of a State and the salary or remuneration for such office is paid by such body,

 

except where such salary or remuneration paid is compensatory in nature.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that if a member of Parliament or a State Legislature is disqualified under the anti-defection law, that person cannot hold any paid political post during the period of disqualification.

Also, this disqualification will continue until the original term of that member ends or until the person is re-elected in an election, whichever happens earlier.

In addition, a paid political post means any position under the central or state government, or under a government-owned body, where salary or payment comes from public money. It does not include simple payments meant only to cover expenses.

This means that a member who loses their seat because of defection cannot immediately be given another paid political position.

Ex : If a legislator is removed for switching parties, that person cannot be made the paid chairperson of a government board during that period.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Adds a further disqualification for a legislator who is disqualified under the anti-defection law (Tenth Schedule).
Such a disqualified member is also barred from holding any 'remunerative political post' for the duration of their disqualification.
'Remunerative political post' is defined as an office under the government or a government-owned body for which a salary is paid.
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 91st Amendment Act, 2003, which also limited the size of ministries. The purpose was to strengthen the anti-defection law by closing a loophole. It prevents a disqualified defector from being immediately rewarded with a lucrative post like the chairmanship of a public sector undertaking.
Landmark_Cases: This article strengthens the anti-defection law.

Title: Bar to interference by courts in disputes arising out of certain treaties, agreements, etc
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution but subject to the provisions of article 143, neither the Supreme Court nor any other court shall have jurisdiction in any dispute arising out of any provision of a treaty, agreement, covenant, engagement, sanad or other similar instrument which was entered into or executed before the commencement of this Constitution by any Ruler of an Indian State and to which the Government of the Dominion of India or any of its predecessor Governments was a party and which has or has been continued in operation after such commencement, or in any dispute in respect of any right accruing under or any liability or obligation arising out of any of the provisions of this Constitution relating to any such treaty, agreement, covenant, engagement, sanad or other similar instrument.

 

(2) In this article —

 

(a) “Indian State” means any territory recognised before the commencement of this Constitution by His Majesty or the Government of the Dominion of India as being such a State; and

 

(b) “Ruler” includes the Prince, Chief or other person recognised before such commencement by His Majesty or the Government of the Dominion of India as the Ruler of any Indian State.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that courts, including the Supreme Court, cannot decide disputes related to certain agreements made before the Constitution started between the Government of India and rulers of former princely states.

Also, this rule applies to disputes about rights or duties that come from those old agreements.

However, the President may still ask the Supreme Court for advice under a special provision of the Constitution.

This means that matters related to old treaties with princely states are kept outside the normal court system.

Ex : If there is a dispute about an old agreement made before 1950 with a princely ruler, a regular court cannot decide that issue.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Bars the jurisdiction of all courts, including the Supreme Court, over disputes arising from pre-Constitution treaties, agreements, and covenants entered into by the rulers of the former princely states.
This ouster of jurisdiction applies to disputes where the Government of India was a party.
The only exception is the advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 143.
Historical_Context: This article was included to treat the integration of the princely states and the agreements made with their rulers as political acts, keeping them outside the purview of ordinary legal challenges. It was intended to give finality to the political settlements made during the unification of India. The abolition of privy purses by the 26th Amendment was a major event related to this provision.
Landmark_Cases: Madhav Rao Scindia vs Union of India (1971) (Privy Purses case): The Supreme Court initially struck down the presidential order derecognizing the rulers but this was later overturned by the 26th Constitutional Amendment.
Related_Articles: Article 143 (Advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court)

Title: Recognition granted to Rulers of Indian States to cease and privy purses to be abolished
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that from the date when a certain constitutional amendment started in 1971, former rulers of Indian states stopped being officially recognized as rulers.

Also, from that date, the payment called privy purse, which was earlier given to these rulers by the government, was completely stopped.

In addition, all rights and duties connected with these payments were ended.

This means that former rulers and their successors no longer had any special legal recognition or financial benefits from the government.

Ex : After 1971, a former prince could not claim yearly payment from the government as privy purse.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Abolished the recognition of the rulers of the former princely states.
Abolished the 'privy purse', which was a tax-free payment made annually by the Government of India to the former rulers and their successors.
Extinguished all rights, liabilities, and obligations related to the privy purse.
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 26th Amendment Act, 1971. The system of privy purses and special privileges for former rulers was seen as contrary to the principles of an egalitarian and democratic republic. After a failed attempt to abolish them by an executive order (which was struck down by the Supreme Court in the Madhav Rao Scindia case), the government passed this constitutional amendment to achieve its objective.
Landmark_Cases: Madhav Rao Scindia vs Union of India (1971) is the key case preceding this amendment.

Title: Special provisions as to major ports and aerodromes
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, the President may by public notification direct that as from such date as may be specified in the notification —

 

(a) any law made by Parliament or by the Legislature of a State shall not apply to any major port or aerodrome or shall apply thereto subject to such exceptions or modifications as may be specified in the notification, or

 

(b) any existing law shall cease to have effect in any major port or aerodrome except as respects things done or omitted to be done before the said date, or shall in its application to such port or aerodrome have effect subject to such exceptions or modifications as may be specified in the notification.

 

(2) In this article —

 

(a) “major port” means a port declared to be a major port by or under any law made by Parliament or any existing law and includes all areas for the time being included within the limits of such port;

 

(b) “aerodrome” means aerodrome as defined for the purposes of the enactments relating to airways, aircraft and air navigation.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President has the power to make special rules for major ports and airports.

Also, the President can decide that certain laws made by Parliament or a State Legislature will not apply to a major port or airport, or will apply with changes.

In addition, the President can stop an existing law from applying to a major port or airport from a certain date, except for actions already taken under that law.

This means that special arrangements can be made for important ports and airports if needed.

Ex : If a special rule is needed for a major seaport, the President can direct that a particular state law will not apply there.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Gives the President the power to make special provisions for major ports and aerodromes.
The President can direct, by public notification, that any law of Parliament or a State Legislature will not apply to a major port or aerodrome, or will apply with certain modifications.
This power can also be used to modify the application of existing laws.
Historical_Context: This article recognizes the special status and national importance of major ports and aerodromes, which are crucial hubs for trade, commerce, and national security. It allows the Union executive to create a uniform and specialized legal regime for these areas, overriding general state or central laws if necessary for their efficient functioning and development.
Landmark_Cases: This is an administrative power-conferring article.
Related_Articles: Union List (which lists major ports and airways as Union subjects)

Title: Effect of failure to comply with, or to give effect to, directions given by the Union
Original_Description: Where any State has failed to comply with, or to give effect to, any directions given in the exercise of the executive power of the Union under any of the provisions of this Constitution, it shall be lawful for the President to hold that a situation has arisen in which the Government of the State cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of this Constitution.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that if a State does not follow the directions given by the Union government under the Constitution, the President may treat this as a serious problem.

Also, the President can decide that because of this failure, the State government is not being run according to the Constitution.

This means that ignoring Union directions can lead to strong action under the Constitution.

Ex : If a State refuses to follow a constitutional direction from the Union government, the President may take steps against that State government.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Provides for a consequence if a state fails to comply with directions given by the Union government.
If a state fails to comply, the President can deem that a situation has arisen where the government of the state cannot be carried on in accordance with the Constitution.
This effectively becomes a ground for the imposition of President's Rule under Article 356.
Historical_Context: This article is a powerful tool that backs the Union's administrative directions to the states (under Articles 256 and 257) with a serious sanction. It is a significant unitary feature of the Constitution, providing a constitutional basis for the President to act against a non-compliant state government.
Landmark_Cases: S.R. Bommai vs Union of India (1994): The Supreme Court discussed this article as one of the grounds for invoking President's Rule, while cautioning that it should not be used for trivial reasons and must be based on a deliberate and serious failure by the state.

Title: Definitions
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that certain important words used in the Constitution have specific meanings.

Also, these meanings must be used whenever those words appear in the Constitution, unless the situation clearly requires a different meaning.

In addition, it defines many terms such as agricultural income, Anglo-Indian, article, clause, corporation tax, debt, goods, High Court, pension, Proclamation of Emergency, public notification, Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, taxation, tax on income, Union territory, and many others.

It also explains in detail what is meant by tax on the sale or purchase of goods. This includes not only direct sales but also transfers of goods in works contracts, hire purchase, use of goods for payment, supply of goods by associations to members, and supply of food or drinks for payment.

This means that the Constitution clearly explains the meaning of important words so there is no confusion.

Ex : When the Constitution uses the term Scheduled Castes, it means the groups officially declared under the relevant article.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: This is a major interpretation clause, providing definitions for 30 key terms used throughout the Constitution.

Some important definitions include: 'agricultural income', 'Anglo-Indian', 'corporation tax', 'existing law', 'goods', 'High Court', 'Ruler', 'Scheduled Castes', 'Scheduled Tribes', 'tax on income', and 'Union territory'.
Clause (29A) was added later to provide an expansive definition of 'tax on the sale or purchase of goods' to cover things like works contracts, hire-purchase, and leases.
Historical_Context: A definition clause is essential in any complex legal document to ensure clarity and consistency. Many of the definitions in this article are crucial for understanding the division of powers, financial provisions, and special provisions of the Constitution. Clause (29A), added by the 46th Amendment, 1982, was a significant change made to overcome a Supreme Court judgment (Gannon Dunkerley) and expand the states' power to levy sales tax.
Landmark_Cases: State of Madras vs Gannon Dunkerley & Co. (1958): This case led to the insertion of clause (29A) in Article 366.
The definitions in this article are frequently cited in a vast range of constitutional cases.
Related_Articles: The various articles where the defined terms are used.

Title: Interpretation
Original_Description:
(1) Unless the context otherwise requires, the General Clauses Act, 1897, shall, subject to any adaptations and modifications that may be made therein under article 372, apply for the interpretation of this Constitution as it applies for the interpretation of an Act of the Legislature of the Dominion of India.

(2) Any reference in this Constitution to Acts or laws of, or made by, Parliament, or to Acts or laws of, or made by, the Legislature of a State, shall be construed as including a reference to an Ordinance made by the President or, to an Ordinance made by a Governor, as the case may be.

(3) For the purposes of this Constitution “foreign State” means any State other than India:

Provided that, subject to the provisions of any law made by Parliament, the President may by order declare any State not to be a foreign State for such purposes as may be specified in the order.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains how the Constitution should be interpreted when there is doubt about the meaning of words.

Also, it says that a general law used for interpreting other laws in India should also be used for understanding the Constitution, unless the situation clearly requires something different.

In addition, when the Constitution mentions a law made by Parliament or a State Legislature, it also includes an Ordinance made by the President or a Governor.

It further explains that the term foreign State means any country other than India. However, the President has the power to declare that a particular country will not be treated as a foreign State for certain purposes.

This means that there are clear rules for understanding and applying the Constitution properly.

Ex : If the Constitution refers to a law passed by Parliament, it also includes an Ordinance issued by the President.
Subject: Miscellaneous
Key_Points: Provides rules for the interpretation of the Constitution.
It states that the General Clauses Act, 1897, should be used for interpretation, just as it is for other central acts.
It clarifies that any reference to an Act of a legislature also includes a reference to an Ordinance promulgated by the President or a Governor.
Defines 'foreign State' as any State other than India, but gives the President the power to declare any state not to be a foreign state for specific purposes.
Historical_Context: This article provides a standard legal framework for interpreting the Constitution, ensuring consistency with other laws. The power of the President to declare a state as 'not foreign' was used to accommodate India's relationship with Commonwealth countries and other nations with which it had special ties.
Landmark_Cases: This article is a tool for interpretation and is cited by courts in a wide variety of constitutional cases to define terms and apply principles of statutory interpretation.

Title: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, Parliament may in exercise of its constituent power amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution in accordance with the procedure laid down in this article.

 

(2) An amendment of this Constitution may be initiated only by the introduction of a Bill for the purpose in either House of Parliament, and when the Bill is passed in each House by a majority of the total membership of that House and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of that House present and voting, it shall be presented to the President who shall give his assent to the Bill and thereupon] the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill:

 

   Provided that if such amendment seeks to make any change in —

 

(a) article 54, article 55, article 73, article 162 or article 241, or

 

(b) Chapter IV of Part V, Chapter V of Part VI, or Chapter I of Part XI, or

 

(c) any of the Lists in the Seventh Schedule, or

 

(d) the representation of States in Parliament, or

 

(e) the provisions of this article,

 

the amendment shall also require to be ratified by the Legislatures of not less than one-half of the States by resolutions to that effect passed by those Legislatures before the Bill making provision for such amendment is presented to the President for assent.

 

(3) Nothing in article 13 shall apply to any amendment made under this article.

 

(4) No amendment of this Constitution (including the provisions of Part III) made or purporting to have been made under this article [whether before or after the commencement of section 55 of the Constitution (Fortysecond Amendment) Act, 1976] shall be called in question in any court on any ground.

 

(5) For the removal of doubts, it is hereby declared that there shall be no limitation whatever on the constituent power of Parliament to amend by way of addition, variation or repeal the provisions of this Constitution under this article.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament has the power to change the Constitution. It can add new provisions, change existing ones, or remove them, by following the special procedure given in this article.

Also, any proposal to change the Constitution must begin with a Bill introduced in either House of Parliament.

In addition, the Bill must be passed in each House by a special majority. This means it must be supported by more than half of the total members of that House and also by at least two-thirds of the members who are present and voting.

After both Houses pass the Bill, it is sent to the President, who must give approval. Then the Constitution stands changed according to that Bill.

However, if the amendment affects certain important matters such as the election of the President, the powers of the Union and States, the lists of subjects, the representation of States in Parliament, or this amendment article itself, then at least half of the State Legislatures must also approve it before it is sent to the President.

This means that changing the Constitution is possible, but it requires strong support and sometimes approval from the States.

Ex : If Parliament wants to change the rules about how powers are divided between the Union and States, it must get approval from at least half of the State Legislatures.
Subject: Amendment of the Constitution
Key_Points: Grants 'constituent power' to the Parliament to amend the Constitution.
An amendment can be initiated only in Parliament, not in state legislatures.
Most provisions require a 'special majority' for amendment: a majority of the total membership of the House AND a majority of two-thirds of the members present and voting.
Provisions affecting the federal structure require a special majority plus ratification by at least half of the state legislatures.
There is no provision for a joint sitting to pass a constitutional amendment bill.
Historical_Context: This article has been the most litigated provision of the Constitution. The central question has been the scope of Parliament's amending power. A series of confrontations between Parliament and the Judiciary over amendments affecting fundamental rights (especially the right to property) culminated in the Kesavananda Bharati case.
Landmark_Cases: Shankari Prasad vs Union of India (1951): Held that Parliament's power to amend under Article 368 includes the power to amend Fundamental Rights.
Golaknath vs State of Punjab (1967): Overruled Shankari Prasad, stating that Fundamental Rights are 'transcendental and immutable' and cannot be amended.

Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala (1973): Overruled Golaknath and established the 'Basic Structure Doctrine', holding that Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights, but cannot alter its 'basic structure'.
Minerva Mills vs Union of India (1980): Reaffirmed the Basic Structure Doctrine and struck down clauses added by the 42nd Amendment that had tried to give Parliament unlimited amending power.

Title: Temporary power to Parliament to make laws with respect to certain matters in the State List as if they were matters in the Concurrent List
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that for the first five years after the Constitution started, Parliament had temporary power to make laws on certain matters that normally belonged to the States.

Also, these matters included trade and commerce within a State and the production and supply of important goods like cotton, paper, food items, coal, iron and steel.

In addition, Parliament could also make laws about offences related to these matters and about court powers connected to them.

However, after five years, any law made by Parliament under this temporary power would stop being effective, except for actions already taken under it.

This means that Parliament had extra temporary power to manage essential goods during the early years of the country.

Ex : In the first five years after 1950, Parliament could make laws about the supply of coal even though it was normally a State subject.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: This was a temporary provision.
For the first five years of the Constitution, it gave Parliament concurrent power to legislate on certain subjects that were otherwise in the State List.
These subjects included trade and commerce in essential goods like cotton, foodstuffs, coal, iron, and steel.
Historical_Context: This article was included to ensure that the central government had adequate power to regulate the economy and ensure the supply of essential commodities during the initial years of the Republic, when the country was still facing post-partition economic challenges. As the five-year period has long expired, this article is now obsolete.
Landmark_Cases: This was a transitional provision and is no longer operative.
Related_Articles: This is a historical provision with no current applicability.

Title: Temporary provisions with respect to the State of Jammu and Kashmir
Original_Description: Article 370, which granted special autonomy to the state of Jammu and Kashmir, was abrogated on August 5, 2019. This marked a significant constitutional change, as the state was reorganized into two Union Territories: Jammu & Kashmir (with a legislative assembly) and Ladakh (without a legislative assembly). The special provisions and autonomy previously enjoyed by Jammu and Kashmir under this article are no longer applicable, ensuring uniform application of the Indian Constitution across the entire region.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that earlier there were special provisions for the State of Jammu and Kashmir.

Also, it allowed the State to have special autonomy and its own Constitution, and limited the power of Parliament over that State.

However, in August 2019, these special provisions were made inoperative. The State was reorganized into two Union Territories called Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh.

This means that the Constitution of India now applies fully and uniformly to that region.

Ex : After 2019, laws made by Parliament apply to Jammu and Kashmir in the same way as they apply to other parts of India.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Originally a 'temporary provision', it granted special autonomous status to the State of Jammu & Kashmir (J&K).
It limited the legislative power of the Indian Parliament over J&K to the subjects of Defence, Foreign Affairs, and Communications.
It allowed J&K to have its own Constitution.
The provisions of this article were rendered inoperative on August 5, 2019, through a Presidential Order (C.O. 272).
Historical_Context: Article 370 was incorporated into the Constitution to accommodate the special conditions under which the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir acceded to India in 1947. For over 70 years, it defined the unique relationship between the state and the Union. Its abrogation in 2019 was a momentous and controversial event, leading to the full application of the Indian Constitution to the region and the reorganization of the state into two Union Territories.
Landmark_Cases: Sampat Prakash vs State of J&K (1970): The Supreme Court upheld the power of the President to issue orders modifying the application of constitutional provisions to J&K.

In Re: Article 370 of the Constitution (2023): A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court unanimously upheld the President's 2019 orders that abrogated the special status of Jammu & Kashmir under Article 370.

Title: Special provision with respect to the States of Maharashtra and Gujarat
Original_Description:
(2) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, the President may by order made with respect to the State of Maharashtra or Gujarat], provide for any special responsibility of the Governor for —

 

(a) the establishment of separate development boards for Vidarbha, Marathwada, and the rest of Maharashtra or, as the case may be, Saurashtra, Kutch and the rest of Gujarat with the provision that a report on the working of each of these boards will be placed each year before the State Legislative Assembly;

 

(b) the equitable allocation of funds for developmental expenditure over the said areas, subject to the requirements of the State as a whole; and

 

(c) an equitable arrangement providing adequate facilities for technical education and vocational training, and adequate opportunities for employment in services under the control of the State Government, in respect of all the said areas, subject to the requirements of the State as a whole.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can give special responsibilities to the Governor of Maharashtra and Gujarat.

Also, the Governor can be given responsibility to create separate development boards for certain regions such as Vidarbha and Marathwada in Maharashtra, and Saurashtra and Kutch in Gujarat.

In addition, the Governor must ensure fair distribution of development funds among these regions, while keeping in mind the needs of the whole State.

The Governor must also ensure fair opportunities in technical education, vocational training, and government jobs for people of these regions.

This means that certain regions within these States are given special attention for balanced development.

Ex : If one region in Maharashtra is less developed, special funds and opportunities can be arranged for that region through a development board.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Provides for special responsibilities of the Governor of Maharashtra and Gujarat.
This is for the establishment of separate development boards for specific regions: Vidarbha and Marathwada in Maharashtra; and Saurashtra and Kutch in Gujarat.
The purpose is to ensure equitable allocation of development funds and opportunities for these regions.
Historical_Context: This article was introduced after the bifurcation of the state of Bombay into Maharashtra and Gujarat in 1960. It was designed to address the developmental aspirations and concerns of backward regions within these new states, ensuring that they receive adequate attention and resources.
Landmark_Cases: This article provides the framework for regional development boards in these states.
Related_Articles: Articles 371A to 371J (Special provisions for other states)

Title: Special provision with respect to the State of Nagaland
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that special protection is given to the State of Nagaland.

Also, any law made by Parliament about Naga religious or social practices, Naga customary laws, decisions based on Naga customs in civil or criminal matters, and ownership or transfer of land and its resources will not apply to Nagaland unless the Nagaland Legislative Assembly agrees to apply it by passing a resolution.

This means that Nagaland has the power to decide whether such central laws will apply in the State.

In addition, the Governor of Nagaland has special responsibility for law and order in the State for as long as internal disturbances continue. While performing this responsibility, the Governor must consult the Council of Ministers but can use his own judgment in deciding what action to take.

If there is any doubt about whether the Governor should act on his own judgment, his decision will be final. Also, if the President later decides that this special responsibility is no longer needed, the President can end it.

The Governor must also ensure that any money given by the Government of India for a specific purpose is used only for that purpose.

Also, a regional council must be created for the Tuensang district. The Governor can make rules about how this council will be formed, how members will be chosen, their qualifications, their term, their salaries, how meetings will be conducted, and other necessary matters.

For a certain period after Nagaland was formed, the Governor had special control over the administration of Tuensang district. The Governor could decide how funds were divided between Tuensang and the rest of the State, decide whether State laws would apply there, and even make special regulations for peace and development.

In addition, special arrangements were made for representation in the State Assembly and Parliament.

If any difficulty arises in applying these provisions, the President can issue an order to solve the problem, but only within a limited time.

This means that Nagaland has special constitutional protection for its customs, land, and administration.

Ex : If Parliament makes a law about land ownership, it will not automatically apply in Nagaland unless the State Assembly agrees.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Provides extensive special provisions for Nagaland to protect its unique culture and customs.
No Act of Parliament concerning Naga religious or social practices, customary law, or ownership of land and resources applies to Nagaland unless the state's Legislative Assembly so decides.
Assigns a special responsibility to the Governor with respect to law and order in the state.
Provided for special administrative arrangements for the Tuensang district for a period of time.
Historical_Context: This article was inserted by the 13th Amendment Act, 1962, which created the state of Nagaland. It was the result of a political agreement (the 16-Point Agreement) between the Government of India and the Naga People's Convention, aimed at ending the Naga insurgency and protecting the distinct identity of the Naga people. It is a key example of asymmetric federalism.
Landmark_Cases: This article is central to the legal and political framework governing Nagaland.

Title: Special provision with respect to the State of Assam
Original_Description: Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, the President may, by order made with respect to the State of Assam, provide for the constitution and functions of a committee of the Legislative Assembly of the State consisting of members of that Assembly elected from the tribal areas specified in 2 [Part I] of the table appended to paragraph 20 of the Sixth Schedule and such number of other members of that Assembly as may be specified in the order and for the modifications to be made in the rules of procedure of that Assembly for the constitution and proper functioning of such committee.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can create a special committee within the Legislative Assembly of Assam.

Also, this committee will include members who are elected from the tribal areas of Assam, along with some other members as specified by the President.

In addition, the President can decide what functions this committee will perform and can make changes to the Assembly rules so that the committee works properly.

This means that tribal areas in Assam are given a special voice within the State Legislature.

Ex : Members elected from tribal areas can form a committee to discuss and handle issues that mainly affect those areas.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Allows the President to provide for the creation of a special committee within the Assam Legislative Assembly.
This committee is to consist of members elected from the Tribal Areas of Assam (as specified in the Sixth Schedule).
The President's order can also specify the functions of this committee.
Historical_Context: Inserted by the 22nd Amendment Act, 1969, this article was part of the reorganisation of Assam. It aimed to provide a legislative forum for the tribal areas of Assam within the state assembly, ensuring that their specific interests and concerns were addressed in the state's legislative process.
Landmark_Cases: This is an administrative provision for the Assam Legislature.
Related_Articles: Article 244A (Formation of an autonomous State within Assam)

Title: Special provision with respect to the State of Manipur
Original_Description:
(1) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, the President may, by order made with respect to the State of Manipur, provide for the constitution and functions of a committee of the Legislative Assembly of the State consisting of members of that Assembly elected from the Hill Areas of that State, for the modifications to be made in the rules of business of the Government and in the rules of procedure of the Legislative Assembly of the State and for any special responsibility of the Governor in order to secure the proper functioning of such committee.

 

(2) The Governor shall annually, or whenever so required by the President, make a report to the President regarding the administration of the Hill Areas in the State of Manipur and the executive power of the Union shall extend to the giving of directions to the State as to the administration of the said areas.

 

   Explanation.—In this article, the expression “Hill Areas” means such areas as the President may, by order, declare to be Hill areas.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can create a special committee in the Legislative Assembly of Manipur.

Also, this committee will consist of members elected from the Hill Areas of the State.

In addition, the President can make changes in government rules and Assembly procedures to ensure that this committee functions properly. The Governor may also be given special responsibility to ensure that the committee works effectively.

The Governor must send a report to the President every year, or whenever required, about how the Hill Areas are being administered. The Union government can also give directions to the State regarding the administration of these areas.

This means that the Hill Areas of Manipur receive special attention and supervision.

Ex : If there is an issue affecting people living in the Hill Areas, the special committee can focus on that issue and the Governor can report it to the President.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Allows the President to provide for the creation of a committee in the Manipur Legislative Assembly.
This committee is to consist of members elected from the Hill Areas of the state.
Assigns a special responsibility to the Governor to ensure the proper functioning of this committee.
The Governor must submit an annual report to the President on the administration of the Hill Areas.
Historical_Context: Inserted by the 27th Amendment Act, 1971, this article was designed to protect the interests of the tribal communities residing in the Hill Areas of Manipur. It provides a mechanism for their representation and involvement in the governance of the state, recognising the distinct geography and demographics of the region.
Landmark_Cases: This article is central to the administration of the Hill Areas of Manipur.
Related_Articles: This is a specific provision for Manipur.

Title: Special provisions with respect to the State of Andhra Pradesh
Original_Description:
(1) The President may by order made with respect to the State of Andhra Pradesh provide, having regard to the requirements of the State as a whole, for equitable opportunities and facilities for the people belonging to different parts of the State, in the matter of public employment and in the matter of education, and different provisions may be made for various parts of the State.

 

(2) An order made under clause (1) may, in particular, —

 

(a) require the State Government to organise any class or classes of posts in a civil service of, or any class or classes of civil posts under, the State into different local cadres for different parts of the State and allot in accordance with such principles and procedure as may be specified in the order the persons holding such posts to the local cadres so organised;

 

(b) specify any part or parts of the State which shall be regarded as the local area —

 

(i) for direct recruitment to posts in any local cadre (whether organised in pursuance of an order under this article or constituted otherwise) under the State Government;

 

(ii) for direct recruitment to posts in any cadre under any local authority within the State; and

 

(iii) for the purposes of admission to any University within the State or to any other educational institution which is subject to the control of the State Government;

 

(c) specify the extent to which, the manner in which and the conditions subject to which, preference or reservation shall be given or made —

 

(i) in the matter of direct recruitment to posts in any such cadre referred to in sub-clause (b) as may be specified in this behalf in the order; 

 

(ii) in the matter of admission to any such University or other educational institution referred to in sub-clause (b) as may be specified in this behalf in the order,

 

to or in favour of candidates who have resided or studied for any period specified in the order in the local area in respect of such cadre, University or other educational institution, as the case may be.

 

(3) The President may, by order, provide for the constitution of an Administrative Tribunal for the State of Andhra Pradesh to exercise such jurisdiction, powers and authority [including any jurisdiction, power and authority which immediately before the commencement of the Constitution (Thirty-second Amendment) Act, 1973, was exercisable by any court (other than the Supreme Court) or by any tribunal or other authority] as may be specified in the order with respect to the following matters, namely: —

 

(a) appointment, allotment or promotion to such class or classes of posts in any civil service of the State, or to such class or classes of civil posts under the State, or to such class or classes of posts under the control of any local authority within the State, as may be specified in the order;

 

(b) seniority of persons appointed, allotted or promoted to such class or classes of posts in any civil service of the State, or to such class or classes of civil posts under the State, or to such class or classes of posts under the control of any local authority within the State, as may be specified in the order;

 

(c) such other conditions of service of persons appointed, allotted or promoted to such class or classes of posts in any civil service of the State or to such class or classes of civil posts under the State or to such class or classes of posts under the control of any local authority within the State, as may be specified in the order. 

 

(4) An order made under clause (3) may —

 

(a) authorise the Administrative Tribunal to receive representations for the redress of grievances relating to any matter within its jurisdiction as the President may specify in the order and to make such orders thereon as the Administrative Tribunal deems fit;

 

(b) contain such provisions with respect to the powers and authorities and procedure of the Administrative Tribunal (including provisions with respect to the powers of the Administrative Tribunal to punish for contempt of itself) as the President may deem necessary;

 

(c) provide for the transfer to the Administrative Tribunal of such classes of proceedings, being proceedings relating to matters within its jurisdiction and pending before any court (other than the Supreme Court) or tribunal or other authority immediately before the commencement of such order, as may be specified in the order;

 

(d) contain such supplemental, incidental and consequential provisions (including provisions as to fees and as to limitation, evidence or for the application of any law for the time being in force subject to any exceptions or modifications) as the President may deem necessary.

 

(5) The Order of the Administrative Tribunal finally disposing of any case shall become effective upon its confirmation by the State Government or on the expiry of three months from the date on which the order is made, whichever is earlier:

 

   Provided that the State Government may, by special order made in writing and for reasons to be specified therein, modify or annul any order of the Administrative Tribunal before it becomes effective and in such a case, the order of the Administrative Tribunal shall have effect only in such modified form or be of no effect, as the case may be.

 

(6) Every special order made by the State Government under the proviso to clause (5) shall be laid, as soon as may be after it is made, before both Houses of the State Legislature.

 

(7) The High Court for the State shall not have any powers of superintendence over the Administrative Tribunal and no court (other than the Supreme Court) or tribunal shall exercise any jurisdiction, power or authority in respect of any matter subject to the jurisdiction, power or authority of, or in relation to, the Administrative Tribunal.

 

(8) If the President is satisfied that the continued existence of the Administrative Tribunal is not necessary, the President may by order abolish the Administrative Tribunal and make such provisions in such order as he may deem fit for the transfer and disposal of cases pending before the Tribunal immediately before such abolition.

 

(9) Notwithstanding any judgment, decree or order of any court, tribunal or other authority, —

 

(a) no appointment, posting, promotion or transfer of any person —

 

(i) made before the 1st day of November, 1956, to any post under the Government of, or any local authority within, the State of Hyderabad as it existed before that date; or

 

(ii) made before the commencement of the Constitution (Thirty-second Amendment) Act, 1973, to any post under the Government of, or any local or other authority within, the State of Andhra Pradesh; and 

 

(b) no action taken or thing done by or before any person referred to in sub-clause (a),

 

shall be deemed to be illegal or void or ever to have become illegal or void merely on the ground that the appointment, posting, promotion or transfer of such person was not made in accordance with any law, then in force, providing for any requirement as to residence within the State of Hyderabad or, as the case may be, within any part of the State of Andhra Pradesh, in respect of such appointment, posting, promotion or transfer.

 

(10) The provisions of this article and of any order made by the President thereunder shall have effect notwithstanding anything in any other provision of this Constitution or in any other law for the time being in force.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that the President can make special rules for Andhra Pradesh to ensure fair opportunities in public jobs and education for people from different parts of the State.

Also, the President can divide government jobs into local groups for different regions and decide how people will be assigned to these groups.

In addition, certain areas of the State can be treated as local areas for recruitment to government jobs and for admission to universities and other educational institutions. Preference or reservation can be given to people who have lived or studied in those areas for a specified time.

The President can also create an Administrative Tribunal for the State to deal with disputes related to government jobs, promotions, seniority, and service conditions.

This Tribunal can hear complaints, pass orders, and even take over pending cases from other courts except the Supreme Court. Its decisions become effective unless modified by the State Government within a specified time.

Also, except for the Supreme Court, other courts cannot interfere in matters handled by the Tribunal.

The President can abolish the Tribunal if it is no longer needed and arrange for pending cases to be transferred.

This article also protects past appointments and actions from being declared invalid just because certain residence rules were not followed at that time.

The provisions of this article override other provisions of the Constitution if there is a conflict.

This means that special arrangements are made to ensure balanced opportunities across different regions of the State.

Ex : If one region feels it is not getting enough government jobs, special local rules can be created to give fair chances to people from that region.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Empowers the President to make orders providing for equitable opportunities in public employment and education for people from different regions of the state of Andhra Pradesh (now applicable to both Andhra Pradesh and Telangana).
Allows for the organization of civil service posts into local cadres.
Provides for the establishment of an Administrative Tribunal to adjudicate service disputes, with its jurisdiction overriding that of the High Court.
The provisions of this article override any other provision of the Constitution.
Historical_Context: Inserted by the 32nd Amendment Act, 1973, this article was the result of a political settlement (the 'Six-Point Formula') to address regional agitations and ensure balanced development and opportunities in Andhra Pradesh. After the bifurcation of the state in 2014, its provisions continue to apply to both Andhra Pradesh and Telangana.
Landmark_Cases: The validity and scope of this article, particularly its clause overriding other constitutional provisions, have been subjects of judicial interpretation. The provision excluding the High Court's jurisdiction was read down in the L. Chandra Kumar case.
Related_Articles: Article 371E (Establishment of Central University in Andhra Pradesh)

Title: Establishment of Central University in Andhra Pradesh
Original_Description: Parliament may by law provide for the establishment of a University in the State of Andhra Pradesh.
Simplified_Description:
This article explains that Parliament has the power to establish a University in the State of Andhra Pradesh by making a law.

This means that Parliament can create a central university in that State whenever it decides to do so.

Ex : Parliament can pass a law to set up a new central university in Andhra Pradesh.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Empowers Parliament to establish a Central University in the state of Andhra Pradesh.
This is an enabling provision.
Historical_Context: This article was also part of the package introduced by the 32nd Amendment Act, 1973, to address regional aspirations in Andhra Pradesh. The University of Hyderabad was established under this provision.
Landmark_Cases: This is a power-conferring article.

Title: Special provisions with respect to the State of Sikkim
Simplified_Description:
This article explains special provisions for the State of Sikkim after it became a part of India.

Also, the Legislative Assembly of Sikkim must have at least thirty members.

The Assembly that existed at the time Sikkim joined India was treated as the official Legislative Assembly under the Constitution, and its members were considered properly elected.

In addition, Sikkim was given one seat in the House of the People, and special rules were made for its representation.

Parliament can make laws to protect the rights and interests of different sections of Sikkim’s population, including reserving seats in the Assembly.

The Governor of Sikkim has special responsibility for maintaining peace and ensuring fair social and economic progress for all sections of the population.

All property, courts, authorities, and laws that existed in Sikkim before joining India continued to operate until changed by a competent authority.

The President could make necessary changes in laws for a limited time to bring them in line with the Constitution.

Courts cannot decide disputes related to old agreements made before Sikkim became part of India, except under special advisory provisions.

The President can also extend other Indian laws to Sikkim with necessary changes.

This means that Sikkim was smoothly integrated into India while protecting its special conditions.

Ex : After Sikkim became a State, its existing laws continued to apply until they were officially changed.
Subject: Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
Key_Points: Contains extensive special provisions for the state of Sikkim.
Sets the minimum strength of the Sikkim Legislative Assembly at 30 members.
Empowers Parliament to provide for reservation of seats in the Assembly to protect the rights and interests of different sections of the Sikkimese population.
Gives the Governor a special responsibility for peace and for ensuring the social and economic advancement of different sections of the population.
Validates all laws and actions taken during the transition period when Sikkim joined India.
